9.2
DATA COLLECTION
The City utilizes a CMMS program, Cartegraph software, which provides the means to
capture, retrieve and track collection system maintenance activities. “How-To” documents
have been created for data entry with examples specific to San Mateo’s collection system.
Staff is trained on use of this program with administration and quality control provided by
the CMMS administrator (Data Management Division).
Sewer crew daily records provide information that assists staff analyze the sanitary sewer
collection system. Included on crew daily log and work order sheets are: location of work;
pipe or manhole ID#; length of pipe worked on, whether activities were part of an
emergency response or preventative maintenance (citywide district cleanings and/or “hot
spot” repetitive cleaning); any structural or maintenance problems discovered in the pipe
and their severity; whether additional follow-up is needed; staff names; equipment and
material used; and start and end times.
Field staff enter all information into the CMMS program with assistance from office staff on
a case by case basis. Using data stored in the CMMS, together with discussions between
management, crew and engineering, the City updates maintenance activities as
appropriate.
Contractors are sometimes utilized in addition to internal staff for completing targeted and
district-wide condition assessment projects. Closed-circuit video (CCTV) investigations
are used to identify problems within sewer pipes and to provide an overall condition rating
of each pipe. Modifications to cleaning schedules can be made using the results from this
analysis. Capital improvement projects are also identified as needed to rehabilitate
specific pipelines and reduce cleaning cycle frequency.
CCTV data and condition scores are also used to determine specific rehabilitation
methods, and to coordinate repairs with other public works efforts, such as road
rehabilitation/reconstruction.
Last Update: May, 2025

Pipeline Observation System Management (POSM) CCTV software is utilized to further
assist with the condition assessment of pipes and reduce the amount of time between
receipt of video and repair of pipes.
Through accurate and timely updates to asset data, preventive maintenance activities,
and pipeline inspection activities and results, City staff have the tools needed to make
informed decisions related to sewer management. This proactive approach to sewer
management has contributed to effective management of maintenance-related spills
through a consistent and systematic cleaning schedule .

SSMP PROGRAM AUDITS
ELEMENT 10: SSMP PROGRAM AUDITS
SWRCB Waste Discharge Requirement:
As part of the Sewer System Management Plan (SSMP), the Enrollee shall include
internal audit procedures, appropriate to the size and performance of the system, for
the Enrollee to comply with section 5.4 (Sewer System Management Plan Audits) of this
General Order.
At a minimum, these audits must occur every two three years and a report must be
prepared and kept on file. This audit shall focus on evaluating the effectiveness of the
SSMP and the Enrollee’s compliance with the SSMP requirements, including
identification of any deficiencies in the SSMP and steps to correct them. The audit
must:
•
Evaluate the implementation and effectiveness of the Enrollee’s Sewer System
Management Plan in preventing spills;
•
•
Evaluate the Enrollee’s compliance with the most updated General Order;
Identify Sewer System Management Plan deficiencies in addressing ongoing
spills and discharges to waters of the State; and
•
Identify necessary modifications to the SSMP Plan to correct deficiencies;
The Enrollee shall submit a complete audit report that includes:
•
•
Audit findings and recommended corrective actions;
A statement that sewer system operators’ input on the audit findings has been
considered; and
•
A proposed schedule for the Enrollee to address the identified deficiencies.
10.1 
SUMMARY
The City audits and updates its SSMP on a 3-year basis. Updates may occur more
frequently if the Inspection Superintendent recommends modifications, or if regulations
change between audits. The audit process is documented in the SSMP Audit Form shown
in Appendix 10. The audit form provides structure for a systematic review of each SSMP
element in order to ensure the SSMP contains current information, regulatory
requirements are satisfied, and programs are effective and meeting City’s goals for the
operation of the collection system stated in Element 1. If updates or changes are required,
the content and timeline to complete those change will should be described in the audit
form. The most recent audit showing the results of the City’s SSMP evaluation was
completed in February 2025 and is included in Appendix 10.3. The City’s audit form is
shown in Appendix 10.2 of this document.
As part of the audit process, City staff develops a timeline for updates to critical
information in the SSMP, such as contact information, names of the key staff in the
response chain of communication, or other similar data as needed. A comprehensive
SSMP update will occur every 6 years, as required by the WDR.

COMMUNICATION PROGRAM
ELEMENT 11: COMMUNICATION PROGRAM
SWRCB Waste Discharge Requirement:
•
The Enrollee shall communicate on a regular basis with the public for spills and
discharges resulting in closures of public areas, or that enter a source of drinking
water; and the development, implementation, and update of its Plan (i.e., SSMP),
including opportunities for public input to Plan implementation and updates;
•
The Enrollee shall also create a plan of communication with owners/operators of
systems that connect into the Enrollee’s system, including satellite systems, for
system operation, maintenance, and capital improvement-related activities.
The Department of Public Works realizes the importance of clear and informative
communication with our residential and commercial customers. Ongoing outreach
programs include annual mailings, targeted letters and door hangers to increase
awareness of how sewer fees are used, sewer operations and maintenance and best
management practices, and information and programs for maintaining and rehabilitating
private sewer laterals.
The City’s SSMP is available for access by the public and is published in the Public Works
page of the City’s website. SSMP attachments including documents related to the
communication program, such as the Private Sewer Lateral Cost Sharing Program
(PSLCSP) program application, reimbursement grant form, etc., are also available in the
City’s website.
11.1 
SEWER FEES AND SEWER FUND
On an annual basis, the City reviews its Sewer Enterprise Fund revenues and the funding
required to operate, maintain, rehabilitate, and improve its sewer infrastructure. As part of
the annual review, the City evaluates the need for potential sewer fee rate increases. If a
rate increase is proposed, staff mails a Public Hearing Notice, in compliance with
Proposition 218 (Section 6 of Article XIIID of the Constitution of the State of California),
explaining a proposed increase to sewer fees and why the rate increase is necessary. In
2023, the City implemented a five-year sewer rate increase beginning in fiscal year
2023/2024 through 2027/2028. The Proposition 218 Rate Increase Notice can be found
on this City webpage: https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/1085/Sewer-Service-Charge.
11.2 
PRIVATE SEWER LATERAL COST SHARING PROGRAM
The City promotes private lateral replacements by property owners through a PSLCSP,
established in 2011. In 2013, the City Council approved a revised sewer lateral cost
sharing program. The revised program provided cost sharing of 50% of the cost of a full
lateral replacement only, up to a maximum of $5,000, based on the lowest of the three
written estimates required to be submitted by the property owner. The property owner
must also submit finalized permits in order to be eligible for the cost sharing. In 2018, the
City enhanced the program to maintain the same cost sharing methods as approved in
2013, while improving outreach of the program and limiting eligibility of applications to a
three-month time frame. This new timeframe allows for more owners to participate in the
Last Update: May, 2025
11 - 1
City of San Mateo SSMP
Element 11: COMMUNICATION PROGRAM
200 of 891

--- page 201 ---
City of San Mateo
Public Works Department
Environmental Services Division
COMMUNICATION PROGRAM
program rather than waiting for inactive applications to complete the process. In 2024, the
maximum reimbursement for cost sharing was reduced to $2,500.
Program materials can be found at this City webpage:
https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/1910/Citywide-Private-Lateral-Cost-Sharing-Pr
11.3 
SEWER MAINTENANCE PUBLIC OUTREACH
Staff from the Field Maintenance Division (Sewer Maintenance) meet with residents daily
while responding to emergency sewer calls. Crews take this opportunity to talk with
residents about the public sewer collection system and private sewer lateral maintenance
concerns. In addition to in-person outreach approaches, the City utilizes various
communication channels, including mailers, post cards, signage on A-frames, social
media, e-newsletters, and community meetings.
Sewer crews utilize door-hangers as part of response activities as well. Crews document
the condition of the sewer mainline, actions taken, and planned follow up activities. These
door-hangers are left at the affected properties and include a phone number for the public
to call in case there are further questions.
The City is developing outreach materials to advise residents not to discharge items to the
sanitary sewer such as non-disposable “disposable” wipes and other products other than
toilet paper. These materials will be included in a future update to this SSMP and added to
the City’s website.
As part of the City’s Spill Emergency Response Plan (SERP), staff developed a public
notification e-mail system to advise residents along waterways after a spill may have
impacted water quality (please refer to Element 6, SERP of this SSMP document). Staff
mails public notices to properties adjacent to waterways to maintain updated e-mail
distribution lists.
11.4 
FOG PROGRAM OUTREACH
Regulatory Compliance Division staff attend public venues/events to distribute information
to residents about the importance of keeping sewers free of fats, oils and grease (FOG),
as well as best management practices for sewer systems. FOG Program outreach
materials can be found in Appendix 7.1, FOG Program, Public Outreach Exhibits.
Sewer maintenance crews work in coordination with Regulatory Compliance Inspectors to
provide outreach materials to residents in San Mateo. After a sanitary sewer spill has
occurred from a blockage caused by FOG, the Regulatory Compliance staff mails a cover
letter and WEF brochure “Fat Free Sewers” to these residents (Appendix 7.1).
11.5

There are no appendix items for Element 11.

JOINT POWERS AGREEMENT
BETWEEN THE CiTY OF SAN MATEO AND THE ESTERO MUNICIPAL IMPROVEMENT DISTRICT

FOR OPERATION OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO WASTEWATER TREATMENT PLANT AND CONSTRUCTION OF
CAPITAL IMPROVEMENTS UNDER THE CLEAN WATER PROGRAM

This AGREEMENT, made and entered into this HL day of Agni , 2017, by and between the
CITY OF SAN MATEO, here and'after called “City” or “San Mateo” and the ESTERO MUNICIPAL
IMPROVEMENT DISTRICT, hereinafter called “Estero” or “EMID”, pursuant to the Joint Exercise of Powers
Act, Government Code.6500 et seq.

RECITALS:

WHEREAS, the parties have previously entered into two joint powers agreements dating back to
1974 which set forth the apportionment of capacities, maintenance and operations:costs, and capital
Improvement:costs for shared wastewater treatment facilities and wish to.continue a mutually beneficial
arrangement under a new joint powers agreement; and

WHEREAS, the parties’ most recent agreement is the Joint Powers Agreement made and entered
into July 17, 1989, as amended on January 17, 1990 and September 5, 2000 (“1989 Joint Powers
Agreement”);-and

WHEREAS, the 1989.Joint Powers Agreement rescinded the previous joint powers. agreement
entered into on June 3, 1974 and amended February 4, 1976 (“1974 Joint Powers Agreement”); and

WHEREAS, the parties now wish to rescind the 1989 Joint Powers Agreement and enter into anew
Joint Powers Agreement for ongaing operation of the Wastewater Treatment Piant and to reflect the “Clean

‘Water Program,” a comprehensive suite of capital improvements, replacements, new treatment processes,

and rehabilitation of the parties’ Jointly Owned Facilities that are necessary for capacity-assurance,
reliability, and to meet regulatory compliance requirements, as defined in the 2015 validation studies
completed by CH2M, the Program Management Team, on the 2014 Draft Integrated Wastewater Master
Plan prepared by Caroilo Engineers and any modification thereto; and

WHEREAS, the Joint Powers Agreement is intended to define and clarify the parties’ respective
purchased capacities in the Wastewater Treatment Piant upon completion of the Clean Water Program, and

the distribution.of capital improvement costs incurred in connection with the Clean Water Program; and

WHEREAS, the parties wish to preserve the ongoing operation and maintenance provisions as set
forth in the 1989 Joint Powers Agreement, as amended, and restated herein, and

NOW, THEREFORE, IT iS AGREED BY THE PARTIES HERETO

The agreement entitled “Joint Powers Agreement between City of San Mateo and the Estero
Municipal improvement District for Construction and Operation of the Water Quality Control Plant” dated
July 17, 1989, and any and all amendments thereto, Is hereby rescinded..

In consideration of the mutual promises set forth herein, the City of San Mateo and Estero
Municipal improvement District agree as follows:

SECTION 1
DEFINITIONS

The following definitions apply to terms used in this agreement:

eet Basis of Design

"Basts of Design” is the criteria, based on engineering and assumed water quality, used to design the

Jointly Owned Facilities and establish the design capacity of a Unit Process.

1.2 Capital lmprovement Costs

“Capital Improvement Costs” shall mean all costs incurred by the Lead Agency to develop,
Implement, and compiete construction of Clean Water Program improvements of the WWTP Jointly Owned
Facilities, including costs of construction contracts, professional consultant services, material and equipment
procurement, compliance with regulatory requirements and mitigation measures, and Staff Costs of the
Lead Agency. Such Capital improvement Costs inciude, but.are not limited to, program, project and
construction management, planning, validating, testing, modelling, and technical studies, architectural
design, legal services, fiscal or economic studies, right of Way acquisition, commissioning and placing
operating costs incurred by Estero in connection with Clean Water Program work. In addition, Capital
Improvement Costs.inciude any liability the Lead Agency incurs In prosecuting the program-work-and any
claims adjusting, claims investigation, and costs incurred in defending claims (including reasonable attorney
fees) brought by any consultant, contractor or third party for damagesarising out of the prosecution of the
program work.

1.3 Design Capacity.

“Oesign Capacity” is the maximum capacity, expressed in millions of gallons per day (MGD), at which
a Jointly Owned Facility or Unit Process is designed to operate,.based on the Basis of Design and assumed
water quality, and to meet National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) requirements.

1.4 Jointly Owned Facilities

As used in this Agreement, “Jointly Owned Facilities” is defined.as all WWTP treatment, conveyance
and disposal facilities from the point of entry of the influent sewage lines into the WWTP facilities to the
effluent outfall point In the San Francisco Bay, including associated buildings (laboratory, administration
building, etc.) which are used for treatment of wastewater to comply with regulatory requirements.

The sanitary sewer collection and conveyance systems independently owned by the City of San

Mateo and Estero which deliver sewage to the WWTP are not Jointly Owned Facilities and.are not:subject to
this Agreement..

15 Lead Agency

“Lead Agency” is the agency responsible for performance of tasks required to develop, implement
and complete construction of Clean Water Program Improvements, or any portion thereof, on behalf of the
parties, and to administer this Agreement by and through its program management office, elected and
appointed officials, employees, and agents, in accordance with the Program Management Plan.

1.6 Maximum Altowable Flow

“Maximum Allowable Flow” is the maximum flow expressed in millions of gallons per day (MGD)
that each party is authorized to convey to the WWTP upon commissioning and completion of all WWTP
Clean Water Program projects and shall. not exceed the Purchased Capacity. The Maximum Allowable Flow
for Jointly Owned Facilities is shown in Exhibit 2.

1.7 Operating and Maintenance Costs

The term "Operating and Maintenance Costs” shall mean any all costs dnd expenses, whether direct
or indirect, incurred for the administration, including the administration of the pretreatment program,
operation, maintenance, improvement, and repair of the Jointly Owned Facilities; including but not limited
to supplies, equipment.and materials, fabor, services, power, chemicals, regulatory requirements Including
fines, mitigations, laboratory contro! and monitoring, insurance and liabilities, administration, legal costs,
and incidental items incurred during normal operations.

1.8 Overtoad

The term “Overload” means hydraulic loads, organic loads, and/or solids loads, that cause’a Unit.
Pracess or processes to exceed the Design Capacity and/or cause Jointly Owned Facilities to not meet NPDES
requirements.

1.9 Partner Agen
“Partner Agency” is the agency not serving as Lead Agency
1.10 Plant.

The terms “Plant”,“WWTP”, and “Wastewater Treatment Plant” are interchangeable and shall
mean the Wastewater Treatment Plant located within the City of San Mateo.

1.11 Purchased Capacity

“Purchased-Capacity” is the maximum capacity of the Jointly Owned Facilities to procéss sewage
expressed in millions of gallons per day (MGD) that each party has Purchased by payment of its
proportionate share of the Jointly Owned Facilities and Unit Processes as shown in Exhibit 1.

If, upon completion of the Clean Water Program Improvements or thereafter, a Unit Process(és) of

the Jointly Owned Facilities are operated within discharge.requirements at actual flows lesser than Design
Capacity or Purchased Capacity, the Purchased Capacity ratios’ shall remain unchanged.

1.12 Staff Costs
"Staff Costs” are the actual costs incurred by Lead Agency staff for hours:spent In direct participation
in activities to develop, implement, and complete Clean Water Program Improvements. Staff Costs include

butare not limited to costs associated with preliminary planning, validation, and testing to determine the
feasibility and parameters of treatment works, engineering, project arid program management,

1.13 Unit Process

“Unit Process” Is a physical, chemical, biological or mechanical treatment function which serves a
specific purpose or treatment objective in the advancement of the quality of wastewater and associated
byproducts.

SECTION 2
CLEAN WATER PROGRAM IMPROVEMENTS

2.1 Clean Water Program Improvements

The Clean Water Program includes all improvements, replacements, new treatment processes,
expansion and rehabilitation of the Jointly Owried Facilities necessary for capacity assurance, reliability,-and
to meet regulatory compliance requirements, as defined in 2015 validation studies completed by CH2M, the
Program Management Team on the 2014 Draft Integrated Wastewater Master Plan prepared by Carollo
Engineers and any modification thereto, hereinafter “The Clean Water Program” and includes all work to
demolish, remove, replace, build, modify, expand, and construct new Jointly Owned Facilities. The work
shall include but not be limited to, preliminary planning and engineering, implementing and maintaining the
program management office (PMO), environmental, architectural, legal, fiscal, and/or economic
investigations and/or studies, surveys, designs, plans, working drawings, public outreach activities,
specifications, procedures, or other necessary-actlons, construction, erection, roadway, environmental or
of treatment works, including Inspections or supervision of any of the foregoing items and all other work for
possible unforeseen changed regulatory requirements and field conditlons, project administration and.Clean
Water Program administration by San Mateo, engineering.and arbitration support for claim resolutions and
lawsults against third parties other than San Mateo and Estero.

The Clean ‘Water Program includes the construction of new dry and wet weather processes and
storage facilities including but not limited to the following: new headworks, new equalization basins for
WWTP influent equalization storage, biologically and chemically enhanced bioreactor, and.associated
equipment and chemical additions; new dual use clarifiers and associated equipment, piping, primary
sludge/scum pump stations, and chemical additions; advanced activated sludge processes (including
Biological Nutrient Removal (BNR) facilities), membrane. filtration, associated equipment and chemical
additions, disinfection using either ultraviolet light for dry weather flaw or chidrine for wet weather flow,
distribution, and storage, blowers, equipment, and other modifications to existing Jointly Owned Facllities
and miscellaneous improvements associated with the dry and wet weather treatment facilities and water
reclamation facilities to meet NPDES compliance requirements, supply recycled water, and replace aging
infrastructure requiring rehabllitations or replacements. The miscellaneous improvements include site
improvements, roadways; building-structures, and other features common to the plant site and surrounding
area, parking lots, community related walkways and/or a meeting learning center, artistic features, parks,
fencing, WWTP security, chemical. handling, storage-and distribution facilities and equipment, odor control
facilities, and electrical, IT, and SCADA-related and associated equipment, facilities, and both software and
hardware products.

Lead Agency’

2.3

Power and Duties of San Mateo

San Mateo shall have the following powers and duties:

a. Toselect the Clean Water Program Manager and Program Manager Advisor consultant to
oversee the day-to-day activities of the planning, design, program implementation,
communications, costs and:schedule tracking, and construction of the Clean Water Program.

b. To solicit bids, award, and enter into contracts for the Clean Water Program.

c. To review and provide comments on contracts for Program Management Advisor consultant,
engineering and related designs services, awards of contract for Clean Water Program construction

Owned Facilities.
d. To pay Its share of Clean Water Program Capital Improvement Costs.

e. To administer the construction, and review.and approve construction contract progress
payments and change orders as needed.

f. Todetermine the adequacy of performance during the construction of the projects.

g. To apply for and receive any permits or consents required from other public agencies'and
entities for Jointly Owned Facilities under the Clean Water Program.

h. To acquire by purchase, iease, gift, condemnation, or otherwise, any real property or interest
therein which it finds to be reasonably necessary for the Jointiy Owned Facilities construction
laydown, materials storage or general construction activities during and after The Clean Water
Program. Any property that is reused or repurposed that is not associated with the Clean Water
Program will result in Lead Agency refunding the Parties’ contributions using cost sharing ratios in
Exhibit 1 or agreed upon by Lead Agency.and Partner Agency if different than ratios established in
Exhibit 1.

i. To approve, reject, or modify contracts for management, engineering:and related studies and
construction work, change orders, claim resolutions, and the acceptance of the completed
construction of the Jointly Owned Facilities.

j.. To manage Clean Water Program budgets including. contingency funds:

k. To establish and implement administrative procedures and Program Management Plan for the
administration of this Agreement‘and management of the Clean Water Program.

|. To provide to Estero final staff reports and notices, reports and studies, and subsequent
approved contracts, change orders, and cost accounting, for any significant-actions or approvals
taken by the San Mateo City Council in execution of the Clean Water Program.

m. To take other actions necessary to impiement and complete construction of the Clean Water
Program, including leading all Clean Water Program Public Outreach Activities,

2.5 Financial Participations and Commitments

a. Financial Participation

Each party agrees to pay its proportionate share of the Clean Water Program Capital !mprovement
Costs based on Purchased Capacity in accordance with the designated proportions shown in Exhibit 1.

b. Budgeting

On or about December 1st of each year, San Mateo shall provide Estero with the preliminary capital
improvement budget for Clean Water Program Improvements for the fiscal year beginning July 1st of the
next year. The parties shall review the preliminary budgets and respond in writing by June 1st of each year
to one:another-any- objections.and reasons therefore. In the absence of any such notification, it shall be
deemed that the preliminary budget is acceptable. San Mateo shall prepare a final budget by July 1st of each
year. The parties shall endeavor to resolve.all conflicts that may arise concerning that budget. The
provisions of Section 6.11 shall apply to unresolved disputes.

\

c. Payments

Progress Payments: San Mateo shall invoice Estero quarterly for its proportionate share of actual
Capital improvement Costs incurred by San Mateo during the quarter preceding the invoice based on the
cost sharing percentages shown In Exhibit 1. Estero shall make Progress Payments as follows: Estero shall
pay to San Mateo within 30 days of delivery of an invoice to Estero’s Public Works Department all moneys
designated in said invoice, even if any or all amounts are disputed, Invoices shall provide reasonable
documentation of costs. Interest will accrue on any amount that remains unpaid after 30 days from delivery
of bills at the prime rate.

SECTIDN 3

PURCHASED CAPACITY AND USAGE

3.1 Purchased Capacity

For cost and ownership sharing purposes, Purchased Capacity is equal to the Maximum Allowable
Flow from each party as defined in Exhibit.1. Actual flows.and loads délivered to the Plant shal! not exceed
the Basis of Design for the Jointly Owned Facilities as shown in Exhibit 1 or cause an Overload. Purchased
Capacities of Jointly Owned Facilities are shown in Exhibit 1 to this Agreement.

3.2 Usage in Excess of Purchased Capacity

Either party may temporarily utillze any unused Purchased Capacity in the Jointly Owned Facilities to
the extent available and unused by the party owning that capacity. Purchased Capacity is unused when the
actual flows (Peak Wet Weather or Average Day Maxirnum Month) from the party owning such capacity is
less than the Purchased Capacity for a particular Jointly Owned Facility. Elther party may enter agreements
to allow non-parties to utilize its Purchased Capacity. The party entering the outside agreement shall not
require consent of the other party to this Agreement. Any capacity used by a non-party pursuant to such an
outside agreement shali be considered, for purposes of this section, Purchased Capacity being used by the

‘party, and therefore unavailable to the other party of this Agreement. Any and all flow delivered to the

Plant which originates from a non-party shall be considered flow delivered from the party with whom the
non-party is under-agreement to utilize capacity and such flow will be included in the proportional usage for
Purposes of calculating each party's share of the Jointly Owned Facilities’ Operating and Maintenance Costs.

A party utilizing capacity in excess of its existing Purchased Capacity for three (3) separate
occurrences (3 exceedances of the Peak Wet Weather Flow or 3 months where the Average Flow exceeds
the Average Day Maximum Month) in one calendar year, or a party desiring capacity in excess.of its
Maximum Allowable Flow, can either purchase the capacity from the other party at. replacement cost
depreciated or shall request that San Mateo initiate a Plant expansion forthwith to provide the needed
Purchased Capacity or Maximum Allowable Flow increase. For Peak Wet Weather Flow capacity.an
occurrence is defined as an exceedance of the Peak Wet Weather Flow within a 24 hour period.

Nothing in this section shall be interpreted to require a party to sell its share of the unused
Purchased Capacity. Unless legally prohibited, San Mateo shall initiate said Plant expansion upon agreement
of San Mateo and Estero. The exhibits shali be amended in the event that the parties modify the Purchased
Capacities.

{f the parties cannot reach either an agreement for the purchase and sale.of capacity or an
agreement to expand the Plant, the party in need of additional capacity may solely fund Plant expansion.and
take ownership of the additional capacity created by the expansion. In such an instance, the Plant

3.3 Metering

San Mateo has established'a metering station and will establish a sampling station that measures
actual flows delivered to the Ptant to calculate capacity usage of San Mateo and Estero. Sampling shail be
performed if wastewater flows exceed treatment loading capacity that causes an Overload. The metering
and sampling station aré open to Estero during regular business hours for purposes of checking the metering
system and obtaining wastewater samples. The meter shall be calibrated according to the manufacturer's
recommended schedule and documentation of each calibration shat| be provided to Estero.

3.4 Consequences of Overload

lf a party causes an Overload, the parties must convene to address any significant harm to the
environment, overflows and/or non-compliance with NPDES permit requirements. Any resultant capital
investments will be the contributing party's or parties’ responsibilities and allocated in accordance with
Exhibit 1, based on the identified cause through a determination process led by the Lead Agency in
coordination with ail impacted partners/parties. San Mateo shall lead the investigation into any Overload
that caused permit compliance issues, recdiimend and implement solutions, and allocate costs based on
both oreither party’s contribution to exceeding the Basis of Design and noncompliance event. San Mateo
will share results of any such investigation with Estero. Disputes:regarding the. finding or outcome of any
investigation shall be resolved according to Section 6.11 — Dispute Resolution.

3.5 Treatment Cost.Loading Factor

This toading factor addresses any additional Plant operational, storage, énergy, and/or treatment
costs or usage needs. The treatment cost loading factor shall be determined based on the required
additional Plant wastewater treatment and/or extra utilities expenses needed to meet'a revised Basis.of
Design to treat the additional load. The need for a treatment cost loading factor shall be determined by
sampling the flow that causes the need for additional treatment or. greater energy requirements to meet a
loading, then an additional wastewater pollutant treatment cost loading factor may be applied to either
party.

3.6 Use of By-Products

Either party may use any by-products of the treatment processes for their own use. If both parties
wish to use the by-product, it shall be shared in accordance with the parties’ ownership and cost distribution
of the process generating the by-product as shown in Exhibit 1. If neither party wishes to use the.by-
product or there is remaining by-product after the parties’ use, it may be sold to a third party; the parties
shall share the proceeds of the.sale in accordance with their Purchased Capacities. {f in the future either or
both parties wish to construct facilities to further process the byproducts for other beneficial-uses by the
parties or a third party, the parties agree to amend this agreement Upon teritis agreed to by the parties.

The Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) processing facility and fueling station constructed in 2016 are
owned and operated solely by City of San Mateo, and-San Mateo is entitled to usage of 100% of the refined
byproduct from this facility.

ONGOING OPERATION AND MAINTENANCE OF JOINTLY OWNED FACILITIES

4.1 General

San Mateo agrees to operate and maintain the Jointly Owned Facilities in a reasonable and prudent
manner.

4:2 Powers and Duties of San Mateo

a. To maintain, repair, replace, improve, and operate the facilities, and to do any and all things

parties in a manner authorized by iaw and permits.

improvements and.operation, maintenance, and replacement costs; and to provide for the
distribution of these-costs between the Agencies.

d. To operate and maintain the Jointly Owned Facilities in a reasonable.and prudent.manner.

e. To schedule meetings at least quarterly for senior management staff from San Mateo and Estero
to discuss operation and maintenance of the Jointly Owned Facilities.

f. Tocomply with applicable laws and regulations and toact reasonably and prudently.

d. To pay bills as stated in Section 4.5.

e. Tomake inquiries where Estero has questions concerning the operation of the Jointly Owned
Facilities.

writing to San Mateo within 120 days after the bill Is received or its objection shail be deemed waived,
unless within 18 months the parties.concur theré has beén an error in a biil, The parties shali timely pay any
amounts that.are not disputed.

SECTION 5S
LIABILITY

S.1 Shared Liability

The parties agree that any liability incurred to a non-party shall be apportioned based Gpon each
parties’ ownership and cost distribution share of the Jointly Owned Facilities, and calculated as the average
of the ownership and cost distribution shares of the Jointly Owned Facilities as shown in Exhibit 1. For the
purposes of this.section liability. includes any liability arising under tort.or contract principles as weli as
liability for violations of any state or federai laws or regulations related to wastewater treatment or
discharge or water quailty standards.

in the. event that either party recovers insurance proceeds from third party insurers, inciuding
commercial carriers and joint powers insurance pools, all recovered moneys shall be shared in accordance.
with the parties’ respective ilability shares. The parties-shall pay their respective shares of any deductibles
or self-insured retentions and any amounts exceeding then-existing limits of coverages.

5.2 Good Faith

San Mateo and Estero-shall both conduct themselves with respect to this Agreement and to the
construction, maintenance, operation, and administration of the Jointly Owned Facilities in good faith and in
a prudent, professional manner.

5.3 Compliance with Federal and State Laws and Regulations

The parties agree to comply with any and all laws, rules, regulations, standards,.and requirements of
any Federal or State board, commission, agency, or similar body, and the decisions of any court of
competent jurisdiction.

5.4 Insurance

San Mateo shall purchase and maintain insurance against loss or damage to the Jointly Owned
Facilities, including workers’ compensation insurance and general liability insurance. The general liability
policy shall cover, to the extent reasonably available, the ownership, maintenance, Operation, and use of the
Jointly Owned Facilities and all incidental activities including but not limited to structural alterations,
construction, and demolition and associated hazards. The policy{-ies) shall name the Estero Municipal
Improvement District, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents as. additional insureds. The
cost of such policies shall be considered operating costs and part:of the annual budget tr the Jointly Owned
Facilities, notwithstanding any self-insured retentions and deductibles.

SECTION 6
GENERAL PROVISIONS

6:1 Fiscal Year

The fiscal year applicable to this agreement shall be from July 1st of each year to and including the
following June 30th.

6.2 Access to Records

Any and all books, documents, papers, and records prepared and/or maintained by either party for
any.activity undertaken under this Agreement shall be made accessible.to the other party upon request or
to any outside party as may be required by law.

6.3 Pretreatment Industrial Permit Requirements:and Sewer Use Ordinances

Each party shall maintain sewer use regulations, including pretreatment programs, permit
requirements, and enforcement procedures to ensure compliance with the discharge: restrictions and
limitations'set forth herein and as required by any and all Federal State and regional laws, rules, regulations
and standards.

Neither party shall knowingly permit any entity, within or outside its jurisdiction, to discharge
sewage into its wastewater collection system unless that entity complies with the pretreatment program as
required by the parties’ respective.sewer use. regulations and any regulatory agency with enforcement
authority for pretreatment regulations and waste discharge requirements. If any party learns that any
entity, within or outside its jurisdiction, is discharging sewage Into its wastewater collection system in
comiaienton of the pretreatment program, that party shail inimediately make steps to either obra
regulations rmipoea upon the City and Estero by regulatory agencies ¢ are considered to be : a part of this
Agreement.and shall be binding upon the parties.

If San Mateo duly authorizes any individual to inspect and enforce industrial pretreatment
requirements of Federal, State, or Regional Agencies or locat limitations and standards, Estero shall allow
that authorized individual access to discharger facilities and enable the individual through ordinance or
resolution to sample, inspect, and enforce applicable regulations regarding illegal discharges. The individual
shall notify Estero before the inspection or enforcement action is performed. Costs of this inspection and
sampling program incurred by San Mateo shall be included as a cost of the operation and maintenance
expenses of the Jointly Owned Facilities.

6.4 Ownership

San Mateo shail retain title to all presently owned lands for the Jointly Owned Facilities and Estero
shall have no intérest in said lands. Either party may acquire real property at its sole éxpense or either party
may request that the other concur in the joint purchase of real property which it jointly believes is needed
for the operation of the Jointly Owned Facilities. The parties, through a separate viii may purchase
real property as undivided Interests based on Exhibit 1.

The Jointly Owned Facilities shall be jointly owned as undivided interests based upon the ownership
and cost distribution as defined in Exhibit 1.

6.5 Separate Agreements and Facilities

Nothing in this Agreement shali prohibit the parties from entering into separate agreements
concerning sewerage facilities which are not a part of the Jointly Owned Facilities.as defined herein.
Nothing in this Agreement shal! prohibit the parties from entering into an agreement(s) to provide sewer
services to areas.outside of their respective service boundaries provided that such agreement(s) does not
cause a party to exceed its Purchased Capacity.

Each party shall be responsible for ensuring full compliance with the terms of this Agreement by any
other entity to which that party provides sewer service. ©

6.6 Notification of New Significant Di reer:

Either party shall notify the other party in writing 30 days prior to the connection of any user or
wastewater treatment services whose need for treatment capacity in the Jointly Owned Facilities is
anticipated to exceed 100,000 gallons per day or otherwise cause an Overload.

6:7 Termination and.Withdrawal
a. Termination.

The parties may mutually agree to terminate this agreement at any time by written agreement.
Estero’s right to obtain wastewater treatment services from San Mateo shall survive termination of this
Agreement, provided that Estero shall be required to pay for the costs of such treatment. based on actual
flows and treatment costs on a quarterly basis.

b. Withdrawal.

A party may withdraw from this Agreement at any time upon giving the other party five years
written notice prior to the proposed date of withdrawal. Notice may be given with.fewer than five years
notice if such withdrawal-would be. consistent with the NPDES permitting cycle, subject to the consent of the
non-withdrawing party. The withdrawing party may not sell, lease, or transfer its rights, or be relieved of its
obligations under this Agreement, without the consent of the other party. As used in this section, the term
“obligations” shall include, but not be jimited to, bonded indebtedness, capital contributions, and Operating

6.8 Notices

Unless otherwise designated in writing by the respective parties, all notices, correspondence, claims,
bills or other documents transmitted by one party to the other shall be addressed as follows with copies to
the respective directors of Public Works:

6.9 Severability

Should any part, term, or provision of this Agreement be decided by a court of competent
jurisdiction to be illegal or in conflict with any law of the State of California, the United States, or otherwise
be rendered unenforceable or ineffectual, the validity of the remaining portions or provisions shall not be
affected thereby.

6.10 Successor; Assignment

This Agreement shall be binding upon, and shall inure to the benefit of the:successors of the parties.
Except to the extent expressly provided herein, neither party may sell, convey, or assign any right.or
obligation hereunder without the consent of the.other.

6:11 Dispute Resolution

This section governs all disputes over the parties' respective rights and obligations under this
Agreement or arising out of the ongoing operation or maintenance of the Jointly Owned Facilities.

a. Mediation. After providing written notice in accordance with section 6.8 Notices, either
party may submit any dispute for mediation. The parties shall meet in mediation witbin 30 days of
recetving notice. The mediator may be any person or entity deemed mutually acceptable by the
parties, including a consultant or expert specializing in the.subject matter of the dispute. In the
absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one name from.a mediators listed by either
the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and Conciliation Service, or other agreed-
upon service. The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. The cost of mediation shall be borne
equally by the parties. Neither party shall be deemed the prevailing party. The mediation process,
once commenced by a meeting with the medlator, shall last until agreement Is reached by the
parties but not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time Is.extended by the parties. The.
decision of the mediator shall not be binding unless agreed upon in writing by the parties. No party
shall be permitted to file a legai action without first meeting in mediation and making a good faith
attempt to reach a mediated settlement.

ORIGINAL

AMENDMENT TO THE JOINT POWERS AGREEMENT BETWEEN THE CITY OF SAN MATEO
AND THE ESTERO MUNICIPAL IMPROVEMENT DISTRICT FOR OPERATION OF THE CITY OF
SAN MATEO WASTEWATER TREATMENT PLANT AND CONSTRUCTION OF CAPITAL
IMPROVEMENTS UNDER THE CLEAN WATER PROGRAM

This Amendment is entered into this 19 _ day of _Feb__, 2019, by and between the City of San Mateo,
hereinafter called “San Mateo”, and the Estero Municipal Improvement District, hereinafter called
“Estero”. ‘

RECITALS

WHEREAS, on April 17, 2017, the parties adopted the Joint Powers Agreement Between the City of San
Mateo and the Estero Municipal Improvement District for Operation of the City of San Mateo
Wastewater Treatment Plant and Construction of Capital Improvements under the Clean Water Program
(JPA).

WHEREAS, the parties now wish to amend the JPA to:

1. Revise and simplify the parties' cost sharing and payment obligations during the Clean
Water Program

2. Specify the term and duration of the Clean Water Program

3. Clarify the parties' cost sharing obligations for operating and maintenance costs and capital
improvement costs after the completion of Clean Water Program improvements

4. Specify a date by which the parties will commence discussion and negotiation of a future
amendment to the JPA.

- Now, therefore, the parties agree as follows:
1. Section 2.5.a “Financial Participation" is replaced with the following:

Each party agrees to pay its proportionate share of Clean Water Program Capital Improvement Costs
and other capital improvement costs incurred between January 1, 2015 and the recording of the Notice
of Completion of the construction of Clean Water Program Capital Improvements by San Mateo, which
shall not be unreasonably delayed.

The parties’ proportionate shares of Clean Water Program Capital Program Improvement Costs and all
other capital improvement costs shall be: .

J SanMateoS~*~S~s Estero SCSCSCS~C“CS~SsCSSY
' | Capital Improvement Costs 75.88% L _

Program Management Office 87.94% —
Costs

After the recording of the Notice of Completion of the construction of Clean Water Program Capital
Improvements by San Mateo, the parties’ proportionate shares of capital improvements shall be
determined in accordance with section 4.5 Billing and Payments.

2. Section 2.5.e "Disbursement of Funds obtained through the San Mateo-Foster City Public
Financing Authority” is added to the JPA as follows:

222 of 891


--- page 223 ---
The parties have formed the San Mateo-Foster City Public Financing Authority (“PFA”) to obtain
financing for the Clean Water Program Capital Improvement Costs. San Mateo is authorized and
responsible for authorizing payments for Clean Water Program Capital Improvement Costs using funds
obtaining by and through the PFA.

San Mateo shall provide, or cause to be provided, all copies of invoices and payments requisitions to
EMID on a monthly basis. EMID shall notify San Mateo within thirty (30) days of any dispute over the
allocation of costs included in those invoices.

3. Section 6.15 "Future Amendments to this Agreement" is added as follows:

The parties shall commence discussion and negotiation of a further amendment to this Agreement no
later January 31, 2029.

4. The parties further agree that this Amendment will sunset on June 30, 2030.

In witness whereof, the parties hereto have caused this Amendment to be executed by their
respective officers and placed into effect the day and year first above written.

of ZLulf , 1989, by and between the TOWN OF
HILLSBOROUGH, a municipal corporation in the County of San Mateo,
State of California, hereinafter called "Town"; the CITY OF SAN
MATEO, a municipal corporation in the County of San Mateo, State of
California, hereinafter called "City"; the CRYSTAL SPRINGS COUNTY
SANITATION DISTRICT, a sanitation district in the County of San
Mateo, State of California, hereinafter called "District"; and the
COUNTY OF SAN MATEO, a political subdivision of the State of
California, hereinafter called “County”.

agreements dated May 3, 1955, March 20, 1962, and October 1, 1979,
which set forth their arrangements and relationships regarding the
discharge, pumping, transmission, and treatment of sewage; and

facilities by Town, District, County, and City;

Section 1. Definition of Service Areas and Trunk Sewers

a. Polhemus Trunk Sewer. For purposes of this Agreement,
there is hereby designated the Polhemus Trunk Sewer, which is
defined as follows:

All sanitary sewerage conveyances and appurtenances
necessary to convey sanitary sewerage from the
beginning of the trunk sewer in Polhemus Road 50 feet,
more or less, southeasterly of Tower Road,
northwesterly along Polhemus Road, to the intersection
of the Polhemus Trunk Sewer with the northerly
boundary of Service Area B, at or near the District's
Measuring Device (as defined below), all as generally
shown on Exhibit A hereto.

b. Crystal Springs/El Cerrito Trunk Sewer. For purposes
of this Agreement, there is hereby designated the Crystal Springs/
El Cerrito Trunk Sewer, which is defined as follows:

All sanitary sewerage conveyances and appurtenances
necessary to convey sanitary sewerage from the
intersection of the Polhemus Trunk Sewer with the
northerly boundary of Service Area B, northerly along
Crystal Springs Road to El Cerrito Avenue to the
intersection of the Crystal Springs/El Cerrito Trunk
Sewer with the corporate city limits of City and the
northerly boundary of Service Area C, at or near the
El Cerrito Measuring Device, all as generally shown on
Exhibit A hereto.

c. Tilton/Idaho Trunk Sewer. For purposes of this
Agreement, there is hereby designated the Tilton/Idaho Trunk Sewer,
which is defined as follows:

Those sanitary sewerage conveyances or appurtenances
necessary to convey sanitary sewerage from the
intersection of the Crystal Springs/El Cerrito Trunk
Sewer with the corporate city limits of the City and

Avenue Pump Station, generally east down Tilton
Avenue, south along Idaho Street, and east across U.S.

dad. Yew/Borel Trunk Sewer. For purposes of this

boundaries of the Town and the City from the Tobin

Section 2. Definition of Jointly Used Facilities

a. Once the sewage from Service Areas A, B, C, D, and E
reaches the intake to the Dale Avenue Pump Station, it flows in
common through the Dale Avenue Pump Station, through the trunk
sewers connecting the Dale Avenue Pump Station and the Water
Quality Control Plant (hereinafter WQCP), through the WQCP for
treatment, and then through an outfall line to San Francisco Bay.
The scum, grit, and ash removed from the sewage at the WQCP is
transported to disposal sites.

b. All of the facilities, equipment, personnel, and
processes used in the conveyance and disposal of wastewater

described in Subsection (a) above is defined for purposes of this
Agreement as Jointly Used Facilities.

Section 3. Ownership of Trunk Sewers and Jointly Used
Facilities

a. Those portions of the Polhemus Trunk Sewer that are
within the boundaries or corporate limits of the District, County,

~4~—

228 of 891


--- page 229 ---
or City, respectively, hereby remain under the ownership of the
District, County, or City, respectively.

b. The Crystal Springs/El Cerrito Trunk Sewer hereby
remains under the ownership of the Town.

c. The Tilton/Idaho and Yew/Borel Trunk Sewers remain
under the ownership of the City.

ad. The Jointly Used Facilities remain under the ownership
of the City, except as may be otherwise defined by agreement
between the City and Estero Municipal Improvement District
[hereinafter EMID]; however, no portion or section of any agreement
between the City and EMID shall be construed as creating any
ownership rights in the Jointly Used Facilities by County,
District, or Town.

e. Whenever the boundaries of one of the parties to this
Agreement are altered through annexation or deannexation so that a
portion of a trunk sewer now lying within the boundaries of a party
then lies within the boundary of another party to this Agreement,
that portion of the trunk sewer shall come under the ownership of
the party within which the trunk sewer then lies.

Section 5. lculation Ww

a. Extraneous Sewer Flow.

For purposes of this Agreement, Extraneous Sewer Flow is
the difference between the total yearly flow measured at the
District Measuring Device and the flows determined by multiplying
twelve (12) times the average monthly amount of domestic water

delivered to all properties in Service Areas A and B for the five

_- 2

230 of 891


--- page 231 ---
(5) winter months of November, December, January, February, and
March. The total Extraneous Sewer Flow shall be pro-rated between
the City, County, and District in the same ratio as each party's
share of total domestic water deliveries in Service Areas A and B
is to the total domestic water deliveries in the two Service Areas.

b. Service Area A.

(i) County. The total County flow from Service Area
A shall be twelve (12) times the average monthly amount of
domestic water delivered to properties in Service Area A
jn the unincorporated area of the Service Area for the
five (5) winter months of November, December, January,
February, and March, plus the County's share of the
Extraneous Sewer Flow.

c. Service Area B.

(i) City. The total City flow in and from Service
Area B shall be twelve (12) times the average monthly
amount of domestic water Gelivered to properties in
Service Area B in the corporate City limits area of the
Service Area for the five (5) winter months of November,
December, January, February, and March, plus the City's
share of the Extraneous Sewer Flow.

(ii) District. The total District flow in and from
Service Area B shall be the total yearly flow measured at
the District's Measuring Device, less the sum of the total
flows calculated for the County flow from Service Area A
and the City flow from Service Area B.

(iii) County. The total County flow in and from

Service Area B shall be the County flow from Service Area
A.

231 of 891


--- page 232 ---
ad. Service Area C.

(i) Town. The total Town flow in and from Service
Area C shall be the total yearly flow measured at the El
Cerrito Measuring Device, less the sum of the total flows
measured at the District's Measuring Device and the City
flow from Service Area D.

(ii) County. The total County flow in and from
Service Area C shall be the County flow from Service Area
B.

(iii) District. The total District flow in and from
Service Area C shall be the District flow from Service
Area B.

(iv) City. The total City flow in and from Service
Area C shall be the City flow from Service Areas B and D.

e. Service Area D.

(i) City. The total City flow from Service Area D
shall be twelve (12) times the average monthly amount of
domestic water delivered to properties in Service Area D
in the corporate city limits area of the Service Area for
the five (5) winter months of November, December, January,
February, and March.

f. Service Area E.

(i) Town. The total Town flow from Service Area E
shall be twelve (12) times the average monthly amount of
domestic water delivered to properties in Service Area E
within the corporate Town limits area of the Service Area
for the five (5) winter months of November, December,
January, February, and March.

=Ox

232 of 891


--- page 233 ---
g. Tilton/Idaho Trunk Sewer.

(i) City. The total City flow in the Tilton/Idaho
Trunk Sewer shall be the total flow measured at the intake
to the Dale Avenue Pump Station from the Tilton/Idaho
Trunk Sewer, less the total flow measured at the El
Cerrito Measuring Device, and plus the City flow from
Service Area C.

(ii) County, District, and Town. The respective
flows of County, District, and Town shall be each party's
flows from Service Area C.

h. Yew/Borel Trunk Sewer.

(i) City. The total City flow in the Yew/Borel Trunk
Sewer shall be either:

(a) Twelve (12) times the average monthly amount of
domestic water delivered to properties in the corporate
City limits served by the Yew/Borel Trunk Sewer for the
five (5) winter months of November, December, January,
February, and March; or

(b) The total flow measured at the intake to the Dale
Avenue Pump Station from the Yew/Borel Trunk Sewer, less

the Town flow from Service Area E.

(1i) Town. The total Town flow in the Yew/Borel
Trunk Sewer shall be the Town flow from Service Area E.

i. Jointly Used Facilities Flow.

(i) Flow from Intake to Dale Avenue Pump Station to
Intake of the WQCP.

233 of 891


--- page 234 ---
(a) County and District. The County and District
flows shall be each party's flows as determined for the
Tilton/Idaho Trunk Sewer.

(b) Town. Town flow shall be the Town's flows
determined for the Tilton/Idaho and Yew/Borel Trunk Sewers.

(c) City. City flow shall be the total metered flow
at the intake of the WQCP, less the total metered flow at
the Mariners Island #2 Pump Station, the total metered
fiow from EMID, and the total County, District, and Town
flows described in Subsections (a) and (b) above.

(ii) Flow through WQCP and Disposal and Outfall
System.

(a) County, District, and Town. The County,
District, and Town flows shall be the same as calculated
under Subsection (i) above.

(b) City. City flows shall be the total metered flow
at the intake to the WQCP, less the total metered flow
from EMID and the County, District, and Town flows
described in Subsection (a) above.

j. If metered flows are available for any of the flows
that are to be measured by domestic water delivery pursuant to this
Section and the metered flows have been measured by the same five
(5) months or more, then those metered flows shall be substituted
for the domestic water delivery measurements for the applicable
flows.

Section 6. Records and Reports of Flows and Costs
Regarding the Trunk Sewers

a. The Town, the District, and the County shall

a iw

234 of 891


--- page 235 ---
separately maintain and provide records to the City of domestic
water consumption for properties in the Town, District, and County
limits, respectively, in Service Areas A, B, C, and E, pursuant to
Section 5.

b. The District shall maintain and provide records to the
City from the District Measuring Device.

c. From the records provided and its own records for City
consumption and flows, including the El Cerrito Measuring Device,
the City shall calculate domestic water consumption and flows
pursuant to the provisions of Section 5.

ad. Each party shall maintain records of the costs of
capital replacements and enhancements to, debt service for, and
operation and maintenance of, that portion of each Trunk Sewer
defined in Section 1 above so owned and maintained by that party.

e. The operation, maintenance, and capital costs shall
include any necessary labor, equipment, or materials used in the
performance of the work, plus any mutually agreed-upon overhead or
markup charges not-to-exceed 15% and normally levied by that party
for such services. Each party shall notify the other parties to
the Agreement before initiating any change in the computation of
overhead or markup charge and obtain their approval before applying
the change to the computations under this Agreement.

Section 7. Proration of Trunk Sewer Costs

a. No later than July 30 of each year, each party shall
submit to the City an accounting of the domestic consumption,
flows, and costs calculated and recorded pursuant to Sections 5 and

6 above for the preceding 12-month period of July 1 to June 30.

b. City will then calculate and prorate these costs in
the ratio that each party's flow through each of the five (5) trunk

a has

235 of 891


--- page 236 ---
sewers defined in Section 1 above bear to the total flows passing
through that trunk sewer.

Section 8. Proration of Sewage Treatment Charges

a. City shall annually adopt a budget for the operation,
maintenance, repair, and improvement of the Jointly Used
Facilities, including all costs and expenses, whether direct or
indirect, incurred for the operation, maintenance, repair, and
improvement of the Jointly Used Facilities, including but not
limited to, supplies and materials, labor, services, power,
chemicals; regulatory requirements, fines, mitigations, laboratory
control and monitoring, insurance and liabilities, judgments,
administration, legal costs, repair and replacement costs, and
capital improvement costs.

b. Sewage treatment charges for conveyance and treatment
of wastewater through the Jointly Used Facilities and emanating
from Service Areas A, B, C, D, and E shall be charged to and paid
by each of the parties to this Agreement as follows:

(i) For pumping and conveyance from the intake of the
Dale Avenue Pump Station to the intake to the WQCP, in the
ratio that each individual party's flows of wastewater as
measured and reported pursuant to Section 5 of this
Agreement bears to the total flows through this portion of
the sewage system; and

(ii) For treatment, discharge, and disposal through
the WQOCP, disposal and outfall systems of the Jointly Used
Facilities, in the ratio that each party's flows of
wastewater as calculated pursuant to Section 5 bears to
the total flows of wastewater through the WQCP.

c. City shall calculate the ratios and costs assigned
pursuant to this Section.

236 of 891


--- page 237 ---
Section 9. Notice of Expenditures and Method of Payment

a. Any party that initiates a new capital expenditure,
other than those identified in Section 12 below, or increases its
level of maintenance that results in an impact to any other party
of greater than $20,000 in any fiscal year pursuant to this
Agreement shall meet, discuss, and reach agreement on the
scheduling of the proposed project and payments. Nothing in this
Subsection, however, shall affect the method of proration of any
project's costs as provided by this Agreement. This Subsection
shall not apply to the undertaking of emergency repairs so long as
notice is immediately given to the affected parties.

b. Not later than April 1 of each year and in
coordination with the other parties to this Agreement, the City
shall prepare a statement estimating the flows and costs for the
coming fiscal year as calculated pursuant to Sections 7 and 8 above
for the Trunk Sewers and Jointly Used Facilities, including any
capital additions, and the proportionate amount to be paid by each
party to this Agreement during the coming fiscal year. This will
be an estimate only for purposes of assisting the parties in
budgeting and to make quarterly estimated payments. These cost
estimates are not warranted to be actual costs for the fiscal year.

c. Quarterly on July 1, October 1, January 1, and April 1
of each year, the parties to this Agreement shall deposit with the
City one-fourth of the estimated cost as provided pursuant to
Subsection (b) above; however, the October 1 payment for each
fiscal year shall be increased or decreased to adjust the amount
payable by the party by the difference between the actual amount
due from such party for the final Section 10 total invoice from the
City for Jointly Used Facilities Charges and Trunk Sewers Costs and
the estimated amounts billed and paid by parties hereto for the
previous fiscal year.

i

237 of 891

Section 10. Invoicing of Parties to this Agreement

a. City shall issue a closing invoice or credit to each
party for the respective costs of the trunk sewer maintenance costs
and sewage treatment charges as calculated for the preceding fiscal
year pursuant to Sections 7 and 8 above no later than September 1
of each year.

b. It is expressly agreed and understood by the parties
to this Agreement that the City has no responsibility whatsoever
nor is any warranty or guarantee whatsoever made by City that the
measurements, calculations, or records made, maintained, or
provided pursuant to this Agreement by the other parties are.
accurate, nor that the City offers to or will act as a guarantor,
collector, or adjustor between the parties for sums or credits due
between the parties. The City's compilation, calculation, and
invoicing is only offered to facilitate the resolution of the
various interests involved in this Agreement.

Section 11. Use Rights

a. Town, District, and County shall have a right to use
Capacity in the Jointly Used Facilities. These rights to use the
Jointly Used Facilities are subject to the Joint Powers Agreement
then in existence between the City and EMID (hereinafter JPA),
including, but not limited to: (a) the rights of EMID under the
JPA to use the WQCP, and the disposal and outfall systems of the
Jointly Used Facilities; and (b) the reserved right of City to use
90.54% of its JPA share of capacity in the WQCP, and disposal and
outfall systems of the Jointly Used Facilities.

b. The maximum shares of capacity in the Jointly Used
Facilities are percentage shares of the City's rights pursuant to
the JPA to a share of the capacity of the WQCP, and disposal and
outfall systems of the Jointly Used Facilities: Town, 4.16%;
District, 4.93%; County, 0.37%. The parties may exceed these

~14-

238 of 891


--- page 239 ---
established capacities until such time that another party to this
Agreement makes demand for its unused capacity; upon written
demand of another party to return capacity, the using party shall
take steps within twelve (12) months to return the capacity by
conservation, pretreatment, or whatever other unilateral steps are
necessary to reduce its excess flows to its assigned maximum share
of capacity. However, at no time may any party other than the City
cause or allow the flows comprising the City's share pursuant to
the JPA in the capacity of the WQCP to be exceeded without the
written consent of the City.

Section 12. Upgrading of Jointly Used Facilities

The parties are aware of and concur in the upgrading of
the Jointly Used Facilities that is projected to occur during the
coming five (5) years. The Town, District, and County shall pay to
City the annual charges provided by Sections 8, 9, and 10 above
that include each party’s share of the cost of the upgrading and
modification of Jointly Used Facilities. The parties are cognizant
that City has agreed to finance such capital costs to the Town,
District, and County through issuance of City bonds; City will
obtain reimbursement for that financing through Section 9 annual
charges. The costs for the upgrading shall be shared by the
parties as provided in Section 8.

Section 13. Overloading
No party shall cause or permit any of the Jointly Used

Facilities to be overloaded, whether hydraulically, biologically,
chemically or otherwise.

Section 14. Limitation on Discharge
a. The parties agree not to discharge or to allow the
discharge into any portion of the Jointly Used Facilities of any of

the pollutants violating the standards or limitations established

oli«

239 of 891


--- page 240 ---
by the Environmental Protection Agency, the State Water Resources
Control Board, the Regional Water Quality Control Board, San
Francisco Bay Region, or any other Federal, State, or Regional
Agency, local standards or limitations established by the City
pursuant to these Federal, State, or Regional Agencies'
regulations, directions, orders, or requests, or standards and
limitations established through or in the City's discharge permits
from Federal, State, or Regional Agencies. In addition, the
parties agree not to discharge or to allow to be discharged into
any portion of the Jointly Used Facilities any wastewater with a
sulfide content exceeding 0.2 parts per million total.

b. Each party agrees that it will prevent the
introduction of pollutants into the Jointly Used Facilities that
would cause interference or upset with the operation of those
Jointly Used Facilities, including causing odor, poisoning of
biota, nuisance, or interference with the use and disposal of
sludge, ash, or other residual byproducts.

c. Each party shall perform or have performed reasonable
sampling and analysis to ensure compliance with this Section.

Gd. Each party agrees it shall immediately pay the cost of
correcting all damage caused, fines, or judgments, as well as all
sampling or analytical costs and the cost of any protective
measures necessary to neutralize, contain, divert, and/or remove,
pollutants discharged by the party exceeding the limitations set
forth herein.

Section 15. Compliance With Federal and State Laws and
Regulations

The parties agree to comply with any and all Federal,
State, and local laws, the rules, regulations, standards,
mitigation, and requirements of any Federal, State, Regional, or

local board, commission, agency, or similar body, and the decisions

it a

240 of 891


--- page 241 ---
of any court of competent jurisdiction which must be complied with
to enable the City to safely and legally operate the Jointly Used
Facilities and to qualify for and obtain Federal, State, or
Regional permits or grants for the construction, improvement,
administration, operation, maintenance, or repair of the Jointly
Used Facilities.

Section 16. Pretreatment Industrial Permit Requirements
and Sewer Use Ordinance

a. Each party shall maintain sewer use regulations,
including a pretreatment program, permit process and enforcement
procedures to ensure compliance with the discharge restrictions and
limitations set forth herein and as required by any and all
Federal, State, Regional, and local laws, rules, regulations and
standards. Within sixty (60) days of a written request by the City
to enact or amend the party's sewer use regulations to comply with
this Section, the party shall do so.

b. No party shall knowingly permit any entity, within or
without its jurisdiction, to discharge sewage into its wastewater
collection system unless that entity complies with the pretreatment
program in effect in City. If a party learns that any entity,
within or without its jurisdiction, is discharging sewage into its
wastewater collection system in contravention of the pretreatment
program in effect in City, that party shall immediately take steps
to either obtain compliance with the pretreatment program or halt
further such discharges. The existing City Sanitary Sewer Use
Rules and Regulations are attached to this Agreement as Exhibit B.
It is expected that significant amendments to the City Sanitary
Sewer Use Rules and Regulations will be made in the coming six (6)
months. Whenever an amendment is made to the City Sanitary Sewer
Use Rules and Regulations, City shall provide each of the parties
with a copy of the amendment.

c. If the City duly authorizes any person to act as

ly =

241 of 891


--- page 242 ---
Industrial Waste Inspector in order to inspect and enforce
industrial pretreatment requirements of Federal, State, or Regional
Agencies or local limitations and standards, each party shall allow
that authorized person access to discharger facilities and enable
the inspector through ordinance or resolution to sample, inspect,
and police illegal discharges. The Inspector shall notify the
agency in which the inspection or enforcement action is to be made
before the inspection or enforcement action is performed. Costs of
this inspection and sampling program incurred by City shall be
included as a cost of the operation and maintenance expenses of the
Jointly Used Facilities.

Section 17. Notification of New Significant Dischargers

Each party shall notify the City in writing thirty (30)
days prior to the connection of any user of wastewater treatment
services whose need for treatment capacity in the Jointly Used
Facilities exceeds 100,000 gallons per day.

Section 18. Treatment of Wastewater

It is agreed by the parties that City neither guarantees
nor warrants to the parties that the Jointly Used Facilities will
be capable of adequately treating the parties’ discharges. In no
event shall City be liable to the other parties to this Agreement
for any damage or injury resulting from the Jointly Used
Facilities' inability or failure to satisfactorily receive, hold,
treat, dispose, or otherwise handle or convey wastewater.

Section 19. llection ms_an r Trunk wer

Each party to this Agreement shall maintain those portions
of its collection and distribution systems and trunk sewers that
are not designated as one of the five (5) Trunk Sewers in Section 1
or Jointly Used Facilities in Section 2 but that are within their

jurisdiction, at no expense whatsoever to any of the other parties.

Section 20. Severability of Obligations

It is specifically understood that the obligations of each
of the parties herein are several and not joint, and that neither
one shall be obligated to pay any sum due from another.

Section 21. Good Faith

The parties shall both conduct themselves with respect to
this Agreement and to the construction, maintenance, operation, and
administration of the Trunk Sewers designated in Section 1 and the
Jointly Used Facilities in good faith and in a prudent,
professional manner. Additionally, each party shall timely and
diligently keep the other parties informed about any and all
matters occurring within its jurisdiction that bear functionally,
financially, or in any other foreseeable way on the relationships
or obligations of the parties as defined by this Agreement.

Section 22. Fiscal Year
For purposes of this Agreement, the fiscal year shall be
the fiscal year of City, as may be established from time to time,

being at the date of this Agreement the period from July 1 of each
year to and including the following June 30.

Section 23. Access to Records

Any and all books, documents, papers, and records prepared
and/or maintained by any party to this Agreement for any activity
undertaken under this Agreement shall be made accessible to the

other parties upon request or to any construction grant agency as
may be required by law. :

Section 24. Termination and Supercession of Agreements
It is mutually agreed by all parties hereto that

Section 25. Term

This Agreement shall become effective on the date the
parties have executed same, and shall remain in full force and
effect until amended or terminated. In the event any party
requests termination, that party shall submit a written notice of
withdrawal at least five (5) years preceding the proposed date of
withdrawal; provided, however, that no voluntary withdrawal may
occur during the period that any bonded indebtedness for the
upgrading of the Jointly Used Facilities described in Section 12 is
outstanding.

each and every covenant and provision of this Agreement shall be
deemed to be a material condition of this Agreement.

Should any part, term, or provision of this Agreement be
decided by a court or competent jurisdiction to be illegal or in
conflict with any law of the State of California, the United
States, or otherwise be rendered unenforceable or ineffectual, the
validity of the remaining portions or provisions shall not be
affected thereby.

Section 31. Disputes

If the parties to a dispute regarding a controversy or

thereof, agree in writing, the dispute shall be settled by

entered in any Court having jurisdiction thereof.

In the event there are new or unforeseen conditions not
covered by this Agreement or conditions that cause any portion of
this Agreement to become obsolete, undefined, or underfinanced, the
Parties agree to meet, discuss, and reach a written agreement to
resolve those new and unforeseen circumstance effects.

not constitute a continuing waiver or a waiver of a subsequent
breach of the same or any other provision of this Agreement.

SANT SRY SEWER USE RULES AND REGULATIONS
ARTICLE I

GENERAL PROVISIONS

SECTION 1.01 Purpose and Policy. These Sanitary Sewer Use Rules and
“Reculations set uniform requirements for discharges into the wastewater
collection and treatment system and enable the City of San Mateo to comply with
the administrative provisions of the Clean Water Grant Regulations, the water
quality requirements set by the Regional Water Quality Control Board and the
applicable effluent limitations, national standards of performance, toxic and
pretreatment effluent standards, and all other discharge eriteria which are
required or authorized by State or Federal law, and to derive the maximum
public benefit by regulating the quality and quantity of wastewater discharged
into the City of San Mateo's sewer system.

These Rules and Regulations provide a means for determining wastewater volumes,
constituents and characteristics, the setting of charges and fees, and the
issuance of permits to certain users.

Revenues derived from the application of these Rules and Regulations shall be
used to defray the City's cost of operating and maintaining adequate wastewater
collection and treatment systems and to provide sufficient funds for capital
outlay, bond service costs, capital improvements, and Gepreciation. Charges
and fees, as hereinafter referred to, are and have been established pursuant to
City of San Mateo Ordinances.

These Rules and Regulations are not intended to be inconsistent with or repeal
any provisions of said Ordinances or any amendments thereto, and in the event
that any provision of these Rules and Regulations is found to be inconsistent
with any provision of said Ordinances, the provisions of said Ordinances shall
sapply. = ; ;

SECTION 1.02 Definitions.

1)

2)

3)

4)

5)

ACT or "the Act." The Federal Water Pollution Control Act, also known @s the
Clean Water Act, as amended, 33 U.S.C. 1251, et sed.

BOD or Biochemical Oxygen Demand. "BOD" or "biochemical oxygen demanda" shall
mean the measure of decomposable organic material in domestic or industrial
wastewaters as represented by the oxygen utilized in decomposition over a
period of incubation of five days at 20 degrees C. and as determined by the
appropriate procedure in “Standard Methods".

COD or Chemical Oxygen Demand. "COD" or "chemical oxygen demand"-shall mean
the measure of chemically decomposable material in domestic or industrial
wastewater as represented by the oxycen utilized as determined by the

appropriate procedure described in "Standard Methods”.

Discharger. "Dischargex™ shall mean any person who discharges or causes 4
@ischarge to a public sewer.

issolved Solids. "Dissolved solids” or "Gissolved matter” shall mean the
solid matter in solution in the wastewater and shall be obtained by evaporation
of a sample from which all suspended matter has been removed by filtaration as
determined by the procedures in "Standard Methods".
