AMENDMENT TO

AGREEMENT BETWEEN CITY OF SAN MATEO AND CITY OF BELMONT FOR
SANITARY SEWER MAIN CONNECTIONS AND CHARGES

THIS AMENDMENT is entered into this 18t day of _MARCH , 2004,
by and between the CITY OF SAN MATEO, hereinafter called “San Mateo”, and the
CITY OF BELMONT, hereinafter called “Belmont”.

RECITALS

WHEREAS, the parties have previously entered into an Agreement for Sanitary
Sewer Main Connections and Charges dated October 20, 1981, and now wish to amend
said Agreement;

WHEREAS, Belmont finds it difficult, if not impossible, to provide water
consumption data for the months of October through March as required by recital Section
7(b) of the current agreement;

WHEREAS, Belmont is unable to supply the consumption data by April of each year to
San Mateo as required by Recital Section 8;

WHEREAS, San Mateo requires additional time at year end to close the fiscal year and
provide Belmont with either a refund or request for additional payment beyond the September |
date as required by Recita) Section 9;

NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS HEREBY AGREED BY THE PARTIES HERETO,
AS FOLLOWS:

1, - Recital, Recital Section 7(b) of the current agreement is revised to
change the consumption months used to arrive at the fee for conveyance, treatment and
disposal of sewage from October through March to November through April. Recital
Section 7(b) now reads:

A fee for conveyance, treatment, and disposal of sewage. The amount of the fee
to be arrived at by taking the average of five winter months (November through April) of
household potable water metered consumption (extrapolated to 12 months) for sewer
users in BELMOMT and SAN MATEO and dividing it into the pro rata cost share of
budgets for annual conveyance, treatment, and disposal of sewage to be expended by AN
MATEO in behalf of SAN MATEO and BELMONT, thereby to obtain a cost per 100
cubic feet of potable water used to convey, treat, and dispose of sewage emanating from
such domestic potable water usage. Based on that amount per 100 cubic feet of water
consumption, multiply the metered water usage of BELMONT properties to arrive at a
total amount for conveyance, treatment, and disposal due SAN MATEO from
BELMONT.

2. Recital Recital Section 8 of the current agreement is revised to
change the month that the consumption data will be supplied by Belmont to San Mateo

U:\pw\P WENG\HILLJ\Sewer4\AMENDBELMONTAGREE. doc Page | of Z

ORIGINAL

_ be 337 of 891


--- page 338 ---
from April to June and to change the consumption months used to arrive at the fee for
conveyance from October through March to November through April. Additionally,

Belmont is to supply supporting backup (i.e., actual water consumption for each month).
Recital Section 8 now reads:

BELMONT will supply SAN MATEO with the five winter months metered water
usage (November through April) (averaged and extrapolated to 12 months usage
including supporting backup) in June of each year for each BELMONT property or unit
served in BELMONT. Winter consumption readings minimize distortion of charges due
to irrigation usage in gardens. SAN MATEO will provide budget figures and actual last
year’s expenditures figures to BELMONT in July of each year.

3. Recital Recital Section 9 of the current agreement is revised to
change the September 1 time of additional payment or refund to October 1, Recital
Section 9 now reads:

Time of Payments ~ Payments pursuant to said formula shall be made during the
month of July of each fiscal year as compensation in advance for estimated costs for such
fiscal year. Subsequent additional payments or refunds are to be determined after the
close of the fiscal year not later than October 1, of the succeeding fiscal year to adjust the
amount to become equivalent to the cost experienced during the fiscal year if different
than the advance payment made at the beginning of the fiscal year.

All other terms shall remain unchanged and in full force and effect. ,

SECTION 1 – INTRODUCTION
The City of San Mateo Department of Public Works (CITY) owns, operates and maintains the public
sanitary sewer collection system and storm drainage facilities within the City of San Mateo. The CITY
performs a thorough condition assessment of the public sewer mainlines and manholes on a five year
schedule to adequately track the condition of these assets and plan for replacement prior to failure.
The information provided in this manual is intended to supplement any contractual requirements that bind
a contractor performing this work for the CITY.
1.1 MANUAL CONTENTS
This manual includes the general procedures required to mobilize for, perform, and document conditions
found while inspecting manhole and underground pipe utilities in the City of San Mateo utilizing closed
circuit television inspection (CCTV). This manual is intended for use in conjunction with NASSCO
Standards for sewer mainline, sewer lateral and sewer manhole condition assessment. Use of NASSCO
instructions, methods of data capture and codes is required for CITY projects. Current NASSCO PACP
and MACP certification of all CCTV operators is required for all contracted CCTV work. Nothing in this
manual alters NASSCO standards or codes. This manual provides data collection and submittal
procedural information specific to San Mateo.
1.2 IMPORTANCE OF TELEVISION INSPECTION
CITY has a primary responsibility to protect and maintain its facilities and to prevent sewage spills that
could create public health hazards or damage to the environment. CCTV provides information about the
condition of the pipes so they can be properly maintained, repaired, and/or replaced. CCTV information
is used to schedule routine maintenance, identify critical repair/rehabilitation needs and prioritize these
repairs, and establish budgets for rehabilitation, system improvements, spot repairs and large-scale
projects. CCTV is also used to verify the quality of preventative maintenance cleanings, new pipe
construction and pipeline rehabilitation.
Captured CCTV data will be used primarily for the following purposes:




Develop a condition rating for each line.
Provide follow-up maintenance and/or structural recommendations.
Display results on a map.
Establish benchmarks to compare with future inspections of the same pipe.
1.3 STANDARDIZED PROCEDURES AND CODES
It is important for all CCTV inspections to be completed to a uniform standard. The use of uniform codes
to describe conditions and defects allows the reviewer to gain a good understanding of the condition of a
pipe and be able to assess deterioration of a pipe from one inspection to the next. All CCTV performed
by CCTV contractors must be performed by NASSCO certified operators using current NASSCO
standards and coding system that can be found in current NASSCO documents. Video, still images and
data shall be delivered to the City on a portable hard-drive for large scale projects or on digital video discs
(DVDs) for small projects. The City shall specify the media storage type at the start of the contract.
Database shall be an unmodified NASSCO-PACP Certified Database.
350 of 891

--- page 351 ---
For large sewer basin CCTV contracts, upon completion of the first 1000 LF, contractor shall submit data
and video logs for review and acceptance prior to continuing work. For smaller contracts, project
manager may request a submittal of a portion of the initial data capture. Requests made by CITY staff to
alter the data and video collection process shall be adhered to for the remainder of the contract.
1.4 CCTV REQUIREMENTS SPECIFIC TO SAN MATEO



All CCTV contractors performing work for the City of San Mateo shall use NASSCO PACP
Standards Version 6 or higher.
In addition to televising pipes, all manholes shall be panned with the video camera and visually
inspected.
All contractors shall first upload the City of San Mateo sewer GIS layer information for use
within CCTV software inside the CCTV vehicle prior to commencing work. Utilizing the GIS
layer during CCTV operations will minimize data entry errors.

If contractor confirms that information in the GIS data provided by CITY is inaccurate, such as
pipe size, length, material or the flow direction inside the pipe (if PipeID has the upstream
manhole and downstream manhole reversed), contractor shall make notations in the Additional
Information Header field.


Contractor shall refer to table 4.2-1 for data matching of GIS fields with CCTV Header fields.
Contractor shall follow NASSCO Header guidelines. Tables 4.2-1, 4.2-2 and 4.2-3 give
additional information specific to the City of San Mateo for completing the header section.
CITY requires CCTV data submittal on a portable external hard drive for large projects. DVD’s
can be used for smaller projects as directed by the CITY.
For CCTV contracts that cover a large sewer basin, pre-cleaning activities shall include use of
hydro-cleaning as well as mechanical cleaning equipment where necessary to clear the pipe and
allow video capture of the entire length of pipe.



Contractors providing CCTV following a completed pipe rehabilitation project must submit
CCTV information for the entire length of pipe and follow all requirements of this Manual and
NASSCO PACP standards.



Contractor shall follow CITY guidelines for buried manholes described in section 3.7.
Contractor shall follow CITY guidelines for video Start and End points described in section 4.3.
If contractor utilizes continuous defect coding, contractor shall ensure that the defect end code is
entered at the appropriate footage and not at the end of the video run.
SECTION 2 – PRE-INSPECTION ACTIVITIES
This section includes basic information on pre-inspection activities including all activities required to
assess the requirements for working at the job site, mobilize for the field and set up equipment before
actually performing the CCTV.
2.1 SITE ASSESSMENT
It is important to be familiar with the CCTV project area; the location of sewer assets, direction of flow
and access issues to sewers in public right-of-ways and in utility easements. Further, knowledge of
historical sewer problems encountered in different areas of the City will help the contractor prepare for
and mobilize appropriate hydraulic and/or mechanical equipment necessary to effectively clean sewer
pipes for successful CCTV.
Blockage Removal - CITY contracts for cleaning and CCTV work anticipate shifting the burden of
removing blockages within sewer pipes (aside from structural issues) to the CCTV contractor. CITY
crews are not to be dispatched except under extenuating circumstances and as directed by the CITY
351 of 891

--- page 352 ---
project manager. CCTV contractor shall utilize their own hydraulic and mechanical cleaning equipment
to adequately clear and remove blockages if the traditional four passes with a hydraulic jet nozzle has
failed to adequately clear and remove blockages.
Plugging or bypassing - Plugging or bypassing needs shall be determined by CITY project manager
during project development unless otherwise specified in the contract Where projects call for flow
control, contractor shall provide plans describing methods that will be used for plugging and bypassing.
During project work, Contractor shall obtain approval from the CITY project manager prior to initiating
plugging or bypassing.
Large diameter pipes: Large diameter pipes often require special procedures for flow control, lighting,
and camera travel or use of sonar equipment. Only CCTV crews with the appropriate equipment and
training to perform large diameter pipe inspection should do so.
2.2 COUNTER CALIBRATION
The footage counter for the camera must be calibrated at least every two weeks during CCTV operations.
The footage counter must be accurate to 0.5 feet per 100 feet. The calibration is performed by checking
the cable counter against a measured length of 400 feet. The date of last calibration should be recorded for
every CCTV report.
2.3 PUBLIC NOTIFICATION – ACCESS TO PRIVATE PROPERTY
Property owners must be notified if access to property is required. CCTV contractors must follow the
CITY prescribed easement access procedures for the project being performed and provide no less than 72
hour notification to residents prior to entering onto private property. Sample notifications will be
provided by the CITY project manager. Notifications must include on-site contractor and CITY direct
emergency phone numbers should residents experience sewer problems during the project.
2.4 SAFETY
CCTV operators and field crew shall all be thoroughly knowledgeable about the following documents
describing procedures for activities related to CCTV:

Current GREENBOOK STANDARD SPECIFICATIONS FOR PUBLIC WORKS
CONSTRUCTION sections including but not limited to the following sections:
o
o
o
Section 7-10 – Public Convenience and Safety
Section 500-1.1.4– Cleaning and Preliminary Inspection
Section 500-1.1.5– Television Inspection



Current CALTRANS STANDARDS for traffic control procedures when required by the City or
CALTRANS.
Equipment owner’s manuals (for CCTV vehicle, Cleaning vehicle and cleaning tools and
equipment operation, maintenance, and troubleshooting) .
Software user’s manuals (for software operation, maintenance, and troubleshooting).
352 of 891

--- page 353 ---
 
For contracted work: Special Procedures (SP-) outlined in contract documents which refer
to modifications or additions to sections in the Standard Specifications which have the same
first numbers (e.g., 200-2).
Traffic Control and Safety - Traffic control is normally required to perform CCTV. GREENBOOK or
CALTRANS STANDARD must be followed at all times. The City will determine whether the traffic
control is adequate. Flashing lights must be used for all night work.
Atmospheric Testing - Atmospheric Testing must be performed even if entry into the manhole is not
planned. This prevents personnel leaning over the manhole from being overcome by noxious gasses and
allows emergency confined space entry if necessary. If the gas detector alarm sounds, contact a
supervisor immediately.
NOTE: If the safety of field personnel or the public, or safe use of field equipment, is threatened at any
time during the CCTV process, the field activities should be stopped and the site secured. If CITY field
personnel are performing the CCTV, the supervisor should be notified immediately. If a CCTV contractor
is performing the CCTV, the prime contractor and CITY’S project manager should be notified
immediately.
SECTION 3 – DATA COLLECTION/PERFORMANCE STANDARDS
This section describes CCTV performance standards, including general information and definitions,
quality standards, record keeping requirements, digital data formatting, appropriate screen text
information and narration, and special CCTV procedures.
3.1 CCTV PERFORMANCE
CCTV performance includes the following:





Consistent use of NASSCO standard forms and codes
Uniform compliance with setup and inspection procedures
Quality picture and audible records
Suitable camera speed, lighting, and panning
Accuracy when recording file names and electronic data
3.2 CCTV QUALITY STANDARDS
Camera Travel Speed: The speed of travel shall be slow enough to inspect each pipe joint, tee
connection, structural deterioration, infiltration and inflow sources, and deposits. Camera speed should
not, at any time, travel at a rate of more than 30 feet per minute. The camera speed should be steady or
slower when recording features and defects.
Clarity: All video and still picture images must be clear and sharp. The camera operator should adjust
focus, zoom, and lighting as needed to obtain a satisfactory image. The recorded image from the CCTV
camera must be free of fog or haze in the pipe. If the camera lens becomes obscured with condensation,
grease, scum, or debris, the camera should be removed from the pipe and cleaned.
Lighting: Lighting in the pipe should be such that the pipe is illuminated and there is a minimum amount
of glare. Lighting should be adjusted as needed according to the size of the pipe to provide a clear picture
of the entire periphery of the pipe for all conditions encountered.
353 of 891

--- page 354 ---
Visibility: Where practical, CCTV inspections will be conducted in a manner that provides an
unobstructed view of the entire pipe. Identifying structural defects is important as they influence the
long-term integrity of the pipe. If debris or obstructions impede visibility, the pipe needs to be cleaned
and camera inspection rescheduled. 85% to 100% of the inside of the pipe must be visible at all times for
successful condition assessment. In some cases, nighttime work may be required in order to obtain
appropriately low flow levels for CCTV. If maximum pipe wall visibility and sufficiently low flow levels
cannot be obtained even at night, flow plugging and/or bypass pumping may be required. Plugging
should only be performed under the direction and approval of the CITY. For new pipe construction
acceptance inspection, nearly 100 percent of the circumference of the pipe wall circumference must be
visible. A small amount of water will be introduced for purposes of sag identification.
Verification of Map Length: If a pipe’s map length (as indicated by the written distance shown on sewer
or storm drain maps, plan sheets, or contained in GIS Field=LENGTH) and the CCTV video counter
length differ by more than 5 feet, the field length should be verified by measuring between the centerlines
of the manholes using a measuring tape or wheel.
3.3 RECORD KEEPING
Sewer Pipe Segment Reference Number (Pipe ID): The CITY has developed a standardized system for
assigning unique identification numbers for each sewer pipe segment. This convention joins the upstream
MH# with the downstream MH# separated with an underscore and omitting the dashes used within each
manhole ID.
For example, a pipe with an upstream manhole ID of 431-08X and a downstream manhole ID of 410-22X
would be:
43108X_41022X.
Should two pipes travel from one manhole to the same downstream manhole, the overflow pipe would be
distinguished from the original pipe by replacing both “X” with “O” for overflow.
In NO instance should the PipeID be altered so that downstream manhole ID is listed first and upstream
manhole ID listed after the underscore.
Utilization of this pipe identifier is extremely important as it assists staff link external data to GIS sewer
mainline layers to analyze and display information.
CCTV Field Log: A field log is to be used by the CCTV crew (and cleaning crew if contracted for both
clean and CCTV) to track daily work. This log should be returned with the completed fieldwork at the
end of the day (by CITY employees) or when data are delivered (by contractors). Logs shall specify the
date, number of persons working, and shall list the specific pipe segments cleaned or CCTV’d
(PipeID’s), time spent per pipe segment and equipment used.
Portable External Hard Drive or DVD Submittal: Upon submittal, DVD’s and external hard drives
shall become the property of the CITY.
Portable External Hard Drive or DVD Labels: All DVDs/External Hard Drives must be properly
identified with the project number and/or name (provided by project manager). DVD’s must be labeled
with the Pipe IDs of the inspections on the DVD.
3.4 DIGITAL DATA FORMAT & FILE NAMING
CCTV Software: Inspection software used by the contractor may be Wincam, POSM, or Granite XP.
Any other proposed software format must be approved by CITY project manager prior to any work being
354 of 891

--- page 355 ---
performed. CCTV contractors must provide the data in a format (unaltered PACP Version 6 or higher
database) that will permit uploading to the CITY’s computerized maintenance management system.
CCTV Video: The full CCTV video must be captured in MPEG format. Other formats may be accepted
but contractor must obtain approval from CITY project manager prior to any work being performed. In
addition to televising pipes, all manholes shall be panned with the video camera and visually inspected.
Video File Naming: Video files should be named in accordance with the following convention:
UpstreamNodeID-DownstreamNodeID-mmddyy-F/R.mpg
For Sanitary Sewer Pipe Segments:




UpstreamNodeID = Upstream MH# (no dashes)
DownstreamNodeID = Downstream MH# (no dashes)
mmddyy is the date of the inspection
F or R indicates whether the CCTV direction is upstream-to-downstream (F) or downstream-to-
upstream (R)
For example, the file name of a sewer pipe inspection conducted on August 13, 2011, starting at
upstream manhole 431-08X and extending to downstream manhole 410-22X would be:
43108X-41022X -081311-F.mpg
For Storm Sewer Pipe Segments:




UpstreamNodeID = Upstream MH# or Catchbasin (CB)# (no dashes)
DownstreamNodeID = Downstream MH# or CB# (no dashes)
mmddyy is the date of the inspection
F or R indicates whether the CCTV direction is upstream-to-downstream (F) or downstream-to-
upstream (R)
For example, the file name of a storm pipe inspection conducted on August 13, 2011, starting at
upstream storm catchabsin 12M-05 and extending to downstream storm manhole 12L-32 would be:
12M05-12L32-081311-F.mpg
If digital video files and data are to be copied onto a DVD, information shall be grouped in a logical
manner (e.g., by area of inspection).
Still Picture Captures: Still images shall be captured similarly for all observed defects with a severe
rating and/or as directed by CITY. In addition, additional still images should be captured every 100 feet
to illustrate the typical condition of the pipe. Still images should be in jpeg format at least 640 x 480
resolution and should utilize the file naming convention as described below.
Picture File Naming: Still picture files should be named in accordance with the following convention
where xxx is the footage location of the defect or observation (to the nearest foot).
UpstreamNodeID-DownstreamMH#-mmddyy-F/R-xxx.jpg
355 of 891

--- page 356 ---
For example, the file name for a still image at footage 123.4 for the example inspection described above
would be:
43108X-41022X-081311-F-123.jpg
If two or more images are captured at the same footage, add .a, b, etc. after the footage, e.g.:
43108X-41022X-081311-F-123a.jpg
43108X-41022X-081311-F-123b.jpg
Still image files are to be copied onto the same portable external hard drive or DVD as the corresponding
video and data files for the pipe segment.
3.5 SCREEN TEXT
Start-up Screen Text: The following information shall be provided as text on the video recording at the
start manhole prior to camera moving up the pipe. This text should be displayed for approximately 10
seconds or for the duration of the Start-up Narration, whichever is longer.











Upstream and downstream node numbers – or PipeID (GIS Field=FACILITYID)
Direction of camera travel
Location (GIS Field=UPMHLOC)
Date and time of day
Weather conditions
Pipe Diameter (GIS Field=DIAMETER)
Total length of pipe (as stated on plan sheets; or GIS Field=LENGTH)
Pipe material (at start of pipe)
Depth of upstream and downstream manholes
CCTV company (or City of San Mateo if taken by San Mateo crews)
Operator’s name (or CITY staff name if internal)
Running Screen Text: During CCTV, the running screen must include the following information. The
display of this information must in no way obscure the central focus of the pipe being inspected.


Running footage (distance traveled)
Upstream and downstream node numbers – or PipeID
End Screen Text. The end point of the inspected pipe segment should be indicated with screen text for
approximately 10 seconds. The ending screen text should indicate:



Ending footage – footage from video counter
Date and time of day
Upstream and downstream node numbers – or PipeID
3.6 NARRATION
Language and Background Noise: The CCTV video recordings are part of CITY
permanent records and should not contain inappropriate language, idle chatter, background noise, and
discussions between the operator and other crew members.
Start-up Narration: A voice narration must be included in the video recording. This narration must
include the following information at the beginning of each pipe segment:



Upstream and downstream MH#’s
Direction of camera travel
Depth of flow
356 of 891

--- page 357 ---




Date and time of day
Weather conditions
Pipe size
Pipe material (at start of pipe)
Running Narration: All observations, including lateral taps (service connections) along the length of the
pipe shall be narrated. Narration shall include a description of the observation and clock position, if
applicable.
End Narration: At the conclusion of the inspection of a pipe segment, the operator should state the final
CCTV footage and indicate that the CCTV of the pipe segment is complete. . If the survey is abandoned
before completion of the pipe segment, narration shall state the reason the survey was abandoned.
3.7 SPECIAL CCTV PROCEDURES: MANHOLE MARKING/UNCOVERING
Buried Manholes
If the CCTV crew encounters a buried manhole, they should notify CITY project manager immediately.
Every effort should be made by contractor to locate and uncover, or locate and mark the location of the
buried manhole. If unsuccessful in uncovering the manhole, CCTV may proceed through the buried
manhole, however, a new file should be started, treating the CCTV inspection as a separate pipe segment
from the initial pipe segment.
If it is not possible to CCTV through the manhole, and CCTV contractor has made every effort to uncover
the manhole, contractor shall notify CITY that the manhole needs to be exposed in order to complete the
inspection. After contractor has marked the location of the manhole, CITY project manager will identify
whether contractor or CITY crews shall uncover the manhole.
SECTION 4 – STANDARDIZED CCTV CODES
This section covers the information and standard formats and codes required for completion of the CCTV.
This manual is intended for use as a complement to NASSCO Standards for sewer mainline, sewer lateral
and sewer manhole condition assessment. When in doubt, refer to most recent NASSCO PACP binder.
4.1 GENERAL GUIDELINES
The following guidelines must be followed when completing the header section of the CCTV form:

If the CCTV extends through a manhole and spans more than one pipe segment, a new header
form must be completed at the start of each subsequent pipe segment.

If a reverse set-up is required because the CCTV in the downstream direction could not be
completed, a new header form must be completed at the start of the reverse set-up CCTV. (In NO
instance should the PipeID be altered so that downstream manhole ID is listed first and upstream
manhole ID listed after the underscore).
4.2 REQUIRED HEADER INFORMATION
The header information contains pertinent data about the individual pipe being inspected. The header
data entry is completed in the field to provide as much information as possible about the inspection work.
Table 4.2-1 lists required header information for each CCTV inspection and provides an explanation of
appropriate responses to each item expected by the CITY.
Much of this information is stored in the CITY’s GIS sewer layers. Upload of San Mateo’s GIS layers

There are no airports in San Mateo.

backyard easements. If no easement exists, “Y” may be used.
Z
Other
If “Z” is used, provide details in the Additional Comments field.

will assist in determining the criticality for sewer repairs.

4.3 CAMERA START AND END CODES
The location at which the camera footage counter is set to 0.00 should be at the start of the pipe at the
manhole or access point wall. Two codes shall always be entered at the start point:
1) the access point code along with the M ID# entered into the Remarks field, and
2) the water level code with the appropriate percentage for height of water in Value 1
field (to nearest 5%).
The point where the camera footage counter is stopped is at the end of the pipe at the (typically
downstream) manhole or access point wall. Two codes shall always be entered at the end point:
1) the access point code along with the MH ID# entered into the Remarks field, and
362 of 891

--- page 363 ---
2) the water level code with the appropriate percentage for height of water in Value 1 field (to
nearest 5%).
4.4 CONTINUOUS DEFECTS
Contractor will follow NASSCO guidelines for continuous defects. If the camera goes under water, it
cannot be assumed that the defect continues. The defect must be closed out where the camera first goes
into the water.
4.5 – CCTV OBSERVATION CODES
The City uses NASSCO Version 6 or higher standard pipe observation codes.

 Workers (on foot) exposed to vehicular traffic must wear orange flagging

signs, or barricades shall be used when necessary.

thoroughfare, a system of traffic controls must be used.

NOZZLE ANGLES AND EFFECTS

Nozzles use water volume and pressure to accomplish two things at once, climb the pipe and clean it.
The angles used in the nozzle determine the amount of thrust that is developed and the effect of the
cleaning jets on the pipe.

Nozzle cleaning jets provide a strong stream of water to remove or loosen materials. The water jets
diffuse rapidly once they clear the nozzle orifice, The greater the distance between the nozzle and the
pipe walls, the less effective the cleaning force will be.

Low angled nozzles develop the most thrust and travel quickly up the pipe. The effectiveness of their
cleaning jets, however, is limited due to the distance the water jet must travel to reach the pipe walls.

High angled nozzles develop less thrust, traveling up the pipe slower. The effectiveness of their
cleaning jets is increased as the distance from the nozzle to the pipe wall is less.

15° angle, note the distance between nozzle and pipe crown for high
thrust and low cleaning power. This angle commonly used for stoppages.

35° angle, note shorter distance to pipe crown for better cleaning with a
little less thrust. This nozzle angle is most commonly used for P/M work.

45° angle, note short distance to pipe crown for best cleaning but little
thrust developed. This angle used for grease and detergent build up.

© - 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Operation - Page 4

374 of 891


--- page 375 ---
CLEANING METHOD FOR GREASE REMOVAL

Grease deposits begin at the crown of the pipe and build down until the flow is restricted to a point
that stoppages frequently occur. Understanding grease deposits is important to effective removal.
Grease consists of hot waste oils from commercial and residential users. Tallow, or animal fats, can
mix with oil deposits, creating a heavy sludge. This sludge is deposited on pipe surfaces as water
cools it and moves it down stream from the source. Grease problems are common in service areas
supporting commercial or industrial food preparation or processing.

The difficulty with grease removal is determined by the thickness of the deposit, the hardness of the
deposit, and the length of the deposit area. It can be one thing to dislodge the grease deposits, it can
be another to avoid stopping the flow downstream as the pieces of dislodged grease become stuck.

1. Determine the area affected by the grease deposit. If possible, begin cleaning operations from
the first section showing little or no evidence of grease build up. This will help keep down
the possibility of dislodged grease from being caught downstream.

vs In known grease areas, a grease nozzle should be used to provide the proper cleaning power.
These nozzles are commonly large bore nozzles providing maximum water flow to provide
strong removal jets and supply enough water to keep dislodged materials in suspension.

3, Set up the hose toward the upstream manhole. In heavy grease removal operation, a lower
manhole roller is recommended over the use of a slipper guide. Slipper guides tend to block
the downstream flow, allowing grease deposits to collect in them, stopping the flow. Lower
roller assemblies to not block the invert and allow free movement of water and materials. If
a slipper guide is used, check the flow rate in the pipe continuously. If a drop in the flow rate
is seen, pull the slipper guide up the hose to relieve the grease build up.

4. Move the nozzle slowly up the line, returning it slowly under pressure. Observation of the
flow will show the effect the nozzle is having. After a couple of passes, the water should be
clear unless harder layers are present.

5. Grease observed in the pipe during cleaning will help determine what the deposit thickness
is. Recent layers of grease will tum the downstream flow into a light brown color, much like
adding cream to coffee. Layers beginning the hardening process will show as small clumps
or bits in the flow. Harder layers typically show as curved sections of grease in the shape of
the pipe itself and be accompanied by strong odors. Extremely hard layers will generally
appear as ‘logs’, long and hard to break up as they pass by. These logs usually indicate a
serious build up the flusher may or may not be able to effectively remove.

6. As explained in the drawing, grease can harden to a point where flushing machines will have
difficulty in removing it. In these instances, a Rodding machine may have to be used to cut
the hardened grease from the pipe walls in manageable pieces. Once removed, flushers can
be scheduled on a regular frequency to remove recent, soft deposits before they can build into
difficult problems.

© - 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Operation - Page 6

375 of 891


--- page 376 ---
CLEANING METHOD FOR SAND REMOVAL

Sand in collection systems can be a variety of different consistencies from loose earth and dirt, to bank
run with rocks, to very fine beach sand. With some minor differences, the removal methods remain the
same with all types of sand or earth debris.

One of the most common mishaps in sand removal is trying to remove too much at one time and getting
the nozzle or the sewer hose stuck in the debris, sometimes referred to as compacting. Compacting
occurs when the nozzle is sent too far up the pipe to remove all the material between the nozzle and the
manhole. Typically, the nozzle will build material behind it as it is pulled back, in some instances causing
a wall of material to build downstream. Usually the nozzle can pull clear of the debris pile, however, if
the material can pack tightly around the hose, it will prevent the nozzle from pulling out. To avoid
potential compaction, and the resulting stoppages that can be caused by this, some basic steps can be
taken to reduce the opportunity for this to occur.

1. Always remove the material from the downstream access, A method for removing the debris,
such as a spoon shovel, handi-clam, or air conveyance, should be available to prevent the material
from going downstream and creating problems.

2. Use a sand trap in the downstream invert to prevent large amounts of debris from getting past the
access. This device will stop most material, causing to fall out of suspension and allow the water
to flow by.

3. Using the proper nozzle can usually be determined by the size of the line. In small lines. of 6" -

10", a combination sand/grease nozzle may be effective. In medium to large size lines of 12" -
24", either a sled or football type sand nozzle should be used. In larger lines these same nozzles
should also be used, however, as the line size increases, the effectiveness of hydraulic cleaners is
diminished and bucket machines may have to be used.

4. Once set up in the pipe with the proper nozzle and hose protection, use the rule of 10's. Travel
approximately 10' up the pipe and return to the manhole. Examine the amount of material that is
pulled out to determine how far the nozzle can safely travel. Ifa large amount returns, clean the
same 10' section a few times to clear it, then push another 10! beyond to remove the next leg.
Though this may take some additional time, the chance of compaction is reduced and the debris
will be more easily controlled.

5. Once enough material has gathered in the manhole, stop operation of the nozzle unti! the materials
can be removed. Under no circumstances should an operator be in the manhole when the flusher
nozzle is working, even if it is quite a distance from the manhole. An unexpected release of water

in the pipe can quickly fill the manhole, or airborne rocks, razors, and other objects can cause
injuries.

6. Using this method, continue the operation until the pipe is cleared. It is not uncommon to need
several tanks of water in a given line section. If a useable hydrant is nearby, a long length of hose
may be used to trickle feed the tank, keeping the water at the same level while working. If using a
slipper guide, be sure materials are shaken out regularly to avoid compaction in the guide itself

© - 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Operation - Page 8

376 of 891


--- page 377 ---
CLEANING METHOD FOR STOPPAGE REMOVAL

Stoppages can be caused by a wide variety of circumstances from hydraulic bottle necks in the. system
to physical objects such as cans or tires. The flushing machine can be used to relieve most stoppages
resulting from grease, debris deposits, or soft solids like rags and papers. In some instances, a
rodding machine may be needed to remove roots or foreign objects from the pipe that flushing
machines cannot easily remove,

As a caution when using flushing machines to relieve stoppages, always operate the flusher from the
downstream, dry manhole. Never attempt to operate from a surcharged manhole. In trying to place
the nozzle in the invert of a surcharged manhole, it is difficult to determine if the nozzle is in the pipe.
When the nozzle is put under pressure it can exit the manhole without warning surprisingly fast,
striking and/or penetrating anything in it’s path, including the operator, and can cause serious injury.

If forced to operate from a surcharged manhole, use long poled tools to insure the nozzle is in the
pipe. Apply minimum pressure of approximately 500 psi, use the long tool to feel if the nozzle is
running up the pipe or up the side of the manhole. This tool may also keep the hose on the pipe floor,
preventing the nozzle from escaping. Again, this is a dangerous practice, and is not recommended.
Most serious injuries associated with flushing machines are related to surcharged manhole operation.

1, Determine the nearest dry manhole from the stoppage. Set up for stoppage removal should
be done from this manhole.

2. A penetrator nozzle should be used to clear the line. This nozzle has a small head designed
to penetrate the material and create an ever widening hole though it.

3. Set up the sewer hose in the pipe, using the proper hose protection and/or easement rollers.
Send the nozzle up the line until the upstream travel stops, indicating the blockage has been
reached.

4. Use the reel controls to back the nozzle up and return to the mass repeatedly. If this fails to

relieve the blockage, a common practice is to physically hold the hose, using gloves for:
protection, pull the nozzle back and abruptly let go, ramming the debris.

5. If the stoppage should clear before water nuns out in the water tank. If the blockage remains,
refill and return, or a Rodding machine may want to be considered. The nozzle may be trying
to move a large rock or other physical object from an invert opening, and lacks the strength
necessary to move it against the head pressure of the water backed up in the pipe system. The
Rodding machine is best designed to provide the strength needed to resolve these blockages.

6. After the stoppage is relieved, the pipe should be cleaned using the proper nozzle to remove
any additional materials that caused the problem. Once cleaned, the line should be proofed
with a proofing tool to be sure the nozzle is not passing under or around another potential
stoppage. Document this proofing test to limit future liability claims.

© - 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Operation - Page 10

377 of 891


--- page 378 ---
HYDRAULIC ROOT CUTTER OPERATION

Flushing machines can utilize hydraulically powered root cutters to remove hardened grease and roots
from sewer lines. Hydraulic root cutters are designed to propel a skid mounted motor up the pipe
while turning a tool at the front end. This tool can be an effective method for material removal,
however, some caution is advised in operation.

Avoid using standard skid mounted cutters in pipes with alignment problems such as dropped pipe
joints. The tool can become lodged in the dropped section and force excavation to be removed.
Some skid assemblies can accommodate dropped joints up to approximately 3", and should be used
in known problem lines, or when faced with an unknown line condition. Be sure the machine footage
meter is operating properly and is reset during use to locate the tool if it becomes stuck.

Use the proper sized skid for the tool being used. Undersized skids will cause opposite end of the
cutter to bounce around in the pipe, potentially causing damage to the pipe and/or cutter.

Use the proper saw blade when cutting roots. Use balanced blades to prevent front bearing wear.
Though interchangeable with Rodding machine saw blades, not every blade will withstand the
tremendous forces generated by the cutter. Use saw blades specifically designed for use on hydraulic
root cutters. Never attempt to handle the operating tool once the cutter is connected to the hose.

L. When removing roots, it is often best to use a size smaller root saw than the diameter of the
pipe to do the initial cutting pass. The cutter can be gaffed up at the far manhole, or returned
to the cleaner to attach a pipe size root saw and skids for the final pass.

A Operate the tool slowly up the line. Due to the rotational speed of the blade, roots can be
thrown against the sides of the pipe, avoiding the cutting teeth. Slow operation allows the
tool to work much more effectively. Operating speed should be a minimum of half that used
with nozzles.

3. When engaging root masses, ease the cutter in and out of the mass using the hose reel
controls. Carefully watch the sewer hose during use. If the hose begins to twist, it indicates
the saw is stuck in the materials, forcing the motor to turn. Withdraw the hose to avoid hose
damage at in the pipe, and free the blade.

4. Use finishing tools, like three blade cutters, to scour the pipe walls after root cutting. Root
. saw blades will leave a small portion of roots in the pipe. The three blade cutter scours the
pipe walls to cut the remaining roots or root ends. This tool requires a very slow travel speed
to get these remaining roots, at approximately 2/3 of the speed the cutter was operated at.

5. For grease removal, use the three blade cutter. Root saw blades become ineffective when
clogged with grease. The three blade cutter provides a long, single edged cutting tool that
with shave off layers of grease. Use a small tool on the initial pass, either gaffing up the tool
at the far manhole or returning it to the cleaner to put a pipe sized tool and skids on. Use a
very slow travel speed to be sure the deposit is broken up enough to pass downstream.

© ~ 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Operation - Page 12

378 of 891


--- page 379 ---
PURGED PLUMBING

Purged plumbing, commonly referred to as “blown toilets", is an increasing problem for may.
agencies. Recent studies of this nuisance problem show there can be several contributing factors.
Increased water pressures used in flushing equipment in recent years, more frequent line cleaning
required by preventive maintenance programs, line obstructions, and structure plumbing can all
contribute to the phenomenon.

Purged plumbing is the result of air volume moving in the pipe during cleaning operations. Most
often this can be felt as a blast of air from the manhole when the cleaning nozzle is working. As
the nozzle moves up the line, nozzle jets create high pressure air behind the nozzle. This is
referred to as positive air pressure. Air in front of the nozzle is pulled downstream. This is
referred to as negative air pressure.

The negative air pressure moving down the pipe will try to pull air from the source of least
resistance. Normally this air is pulled from the line itself, manhole chambers, and/or vents in the
manhole lids. If the pipe is obstructed with water flow, root or grease build up, or has a dip
sealed with water, the cubic foot space available for air movement is reduced or eliminated. The
negative air is then pulled from service connections.

When air is pulled from service connections the house vent will usually allow air to move freely.
Depending on the size of this vent, obstructions that may be present, and the complexity of the

structure plumbing, the air may pull freely or may try to pull water from the traps. Which traps
are affected depends on which trap offers the path of least resistance to the negative pressure.

In toilets, water is drawn from the trap as negative air forms a vacuum. Water is drawn down
from the bowl until it reaches a level that air can move through the trap, venting air to relieve the
negative pressure. Once the nozzle is past the service connection, the water settles and remains at
low level in the bowl and trap, barely sealing the trap.

When the nozzle is pulled back, positive air is now pushed downstream. The same conditions that
caused negative air pressure to pull water from traps'will cause positive air to be pushed up the
service connection. The positive air pressure builds behind the small amount of water in the trap.
This pressure builds to a point it can move the water, and will either push back the water seal and
vent, or can eject water from the trap as the air escapes. In some cases this can be dramatic,
causing water to reach the ceiling.

Research has shown that to help avoid purged plumbing, the best solution is a multiple one. First,
in areas that there are known occurrences of this, remove the upstream manhole to help allow
more air flow in the line. This alone will not solve the problem, however, it will help to reduce
the volume of air that is drawn from service connections. You should be sure there are no crown
obstructions that inhibit this air flow, and if so, they should be removed with equipment designed
to cut roots and hardened grease. Use high angled nozzles, or vortex nozzles that break the
pressure cone of air in the pipe. If by taking these preventive steps the problem persists, video
inspection of the main line and/or service vent may be needed to find physical flaws.

© ~ 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Operation - Page 14

379 of 891


--- page 380 ---
PURGED PLUMBING RESULTS

Typically, purged plumbing does not attect toilets as often as other “P” traps ina building.
Basement or first floor traps in sinks and showers will often provide air relief for the rest.of the
building. Low flush toilets, and other modern designs may have a low enough trap to be.the
first to evacuate water. The results of this range from minor surging to impressive spray that
can reach the ceiling.

As negative pressure from the nozzle pulls air from service
connections, the vent system may be too small in diameter,
clogged, or not properly installed, causing a vacuum that can
pull water down in the toilet.

Negative air pressure pulls water down in the trap |

Water will pull down until the surface level breaks
contact with the top ofthe trap. At this point the vacuum
is relieved, and some water returns to the bowl, barely
covering the top of the trap.

Negative air pressure relieved when
water yeilds to allow air to vent

On the return pass, positive pressure from the nozzle forces
air back into the service connection. If the vent work cannot
accept the volume of air, it seeks the path of least resistance.
Aur builds pressure until the water in the toilet can be forced
from the trap.

Depending on the amount of water in the trap, and the
amount of air pressure, the air can either vent easily, or will
explode the water from the trap.

LW. Positive air builds pressure bhind water in trap, eventually
causing the water to move

© - 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Operation - Page 16

380 of 891


--- page 381 ---
10.

TOP TEN LIST OF FLUSHER DO’S AND DON’TS

Always be aware of traffic and safety concerns. When setting up on the street use cones to define
the work area. Set the cones out at least 20' out from the machine to allow the sewer hose to be
laid out to change nozzles, skids, or other tools. Always wear gloves when handling the hose,
especially when the hose is under load to protect hands from acids and other agents that may be
present.

Inspect the sewer hose regularly. Do not allow use of a hose that has deep cuts in the jacket, or
sections of hose jacket that are peeling off. Watch all coupling and splice fittings for bubbles in
the jacket indicating water is getting between the inner core and outer jacket. Avoid using hose
with many in-line splice connections for extended periods before replacing.

Do not use the sewer hose without a slipper guide or lower manhole roller to avoid damage.
Always use the proper easement roller, both at the remote manhole, and on the machine to ease
the stress placed on the hose.

When performing routine cleaning, use little pressure to jet up the line, when returning turn the
pressure up a little to help the scouring action. Most 8 - 12" pipes can be easily cleaned at
approximately 1200 - 1500 PSI. For tough problems such as hardened grease or large amounts of
debris, a full 2000 PSI may be needed.

Do not attempt to clear a blockage from the upstream, full manhole. Serious injury can result if
the nozzle is not in the pipe and exits the manhole unexpectedly under pressure.

When moving large amounts of static debris, use short distances forward and bring the nozzle
back to the manhole. Trying to pull too much material can bury the hose and nozzle, making it
difficult, if not impossible, to remove, and can cause a blockage.

Under no circumstances should anyone be in the manhole when the flusher is in operation.
Serious injury can result if a blockage is relieved upstream and floods the manhole, or from
airborne debris such as small rocks and razor blades thrown up by the force of the nozzle.

Use the proper tool for the job. Understand how each nozzle in the inventory works, and when it
should be used. Some nozzles may appear to work well when used outside of their design, when
in fact they not be working as well as a nozzle designed for the application.

When operating hydraulic root cutter, use a much slower forward and reverse speed than when
using a nozzle to allow the cutter to work. Always use the same size skids as the blades being
used to avoid damage to the blade, cutter, or pipes.

Maintain the machine and accessories regularly. Do not leave nozzle attached to the sewer hose
overnight, or long periods, as they will corrode and fuse themselves to the coupling. Grease all
reel bearings, and rollers regularly, and check system fluid levels in the pump, engine, and
hydraulic system. Inspect nozzles for wear, replacing them if the orifices become wallowed out,
or they show signs of heavy abrasion wear.

© - 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Operation - Page 18

NOZZLE TYPES AND USE

Determining the proper nozzle for the job can be confusing. The operator cannot see what is
happening in the pipe and how the nozzle is working. Like all tools, nozzles have different
designs for various cleaning applications. These applications fall into five basic catagories:
Preventive maintenance, Stoppage Removal, Crown Obstruction Removal, Invert Obstruction
Removal, and Specialty nozzles.

There are, to be sure, many different types of nozzles that are offered for use with flushing
machines. The best way to determine how a specific nozzle will work for you is to determine
which catagory group the nozzle fits into. If a nozzle does not appear to fit any one group, or has a
narrow specialty use, you may want to consider the use you will get from it versus the cost.

Preventive maintenance nozzles include radial nozzles, typically
drilled at 35°. These nozzles provide a design suitable for use in
routine cleaning and pin pointing of problems in pipes.

Stoppage nozzles include penetrator nozzles, typically drilled at 15°.
These nozzles are designed to penetrate materials and create an ever
widening hole. Stoppage nozzles commonly have heads smaller than
the nozzle shank.

Crown obstruction nozzles include large bore nozzles, typically
drilled at 35° to 45° with some having combinations of both. These
nozzles are designed to produce high volume and direct jets at crown
obstructions to remove them.

Invert obstruction nozzles include either flat sled type, or
football type nozzles. These nozzles have replaceable
orifices at various angles from 1° to 15°. These nozzles are
designed to provide high water volume and direct jets to the
bottom of the pipe.

© - 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Tools - Page 2

385 of 891


--- page 386 ---
LOCATING AND INSPECTION TOOLS

In the same catagory as specialty nozzles, there are many useful specialty accessories used to
facilitate video inspection, problem identification, and underground locating of pipes. While not
used on a daily basis by most regular cleaning crews, these accessories can be tremendous time
savers when working to accurately pin point, or observe underground problems.

Proofing Tool: A proofing tool is used during regular
maintenance to confirm the pipe is clear within 1" of
the diameter. Used to enhance records, this tool is very
effective in finding areas a mechanical cleaner needs to
be used. Also used for non-T'V inspection of new
sewer lines before acceptance to be sure they are not
crushed or have protruding connections.

Self Propelled Camera: The advent of small,
inexpensive TV systems has allowed development of
easy to use skids with a camera installed. These
systems use a special nozzle attached to the skid to do
fast inspection of areas that have suspected problems.
Not to take the place of a mainline camera system,
these small systems can provide a real time look at
most problems in lines up to 12".

Sonde: Location equipment using battery operated
sondes can be used with flushers. These sondes emit a
signal that is traced on the surface with a special
receiver. When strapped to the sewer hose, or skid,
the progress of the nozzle can be monitored, or locate
a specific dig site.

Cable Towing Nozzle: This nozzle is used to pull
video or bucket machine cable when stringing a line.
The nozzle has a built in swivel to allow the cable to

UL - twist without effecting the sewer hose, and should be
ante used whenever pulling tag lines or cables.
© ~- 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Tools - Page 4

386 of 891


--- page 387 ---
REMOTE AND EASEMENT ACCESS

As operators well know, not all manholes are in the street. Construction needs of the time,
development in former open land, and varying construction codes, will ofen result in manholes
set to the side of the road, in back yards, alleys, and easements or right of ways.

Reaching these manholes with the sewer hose can be difficult, especially if the cleaner cannot be
taken off road to reach them. Remote manholes are typically set to the side of the street , within
a few dozen feet. Isolated manholes are commonly found in easement right of ways behind
buildings, or through an undeveloped area.

Easement and lateral rollers to protect the hose and the operator should be used when working in
remote or isolated manholes.

Top manhole rollers should be used at the distant
manhole to allow the hose to pass by the sharp edges of
the rim under load. Accidents involving hose breaks
are most commonly caused by sharp edges cutting into
the hose and holding it while it is being pulled,
eventually cuttin the hose.

Field stake rollers are used to protect the hose from
structure corners, stone or masonary walls, and other
opbjects that would catch the hose and damage it.

Use slipper guides to pass the hose over rough stone,
fences, or binding angles.

Machine mounted easement rollers should be used to take
the lateral stress away from the level wind and footage
meter. These insert to a tube mounted under the hose reel,
and allow the hose to be deployed in line with the cleaner.
Roller sides are quick connect, allowing set up of the hose
before they are attached.

For use on 90° access, use the 90° machine mounted
easement roller. Roller sides are quick connect, allowing
set up of the hose before they are attached.

Articulating, or pivoting machine mounted rollers provide
an unlimited number of working angles, and are extremely
useful in street operation if the cleaner is not aligned with
the manhole. Working up to 180°, the roller sides are
quick connect, allowing the hose to be set up before they
are attached.

© ~ 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE - Flusher Tools - Page 6

387 of 891


--- page 388 ---
HYDRAULIC ROOT CUTTERS

Hydraulic root cutters come in a variety of power configurations and sizes. Much like power
saws used in construction, there are ranges of performance power from low rpm/low torque to
high rpm/high torque. As the rpm and torque rating increase, the cutter provides higher cutting
power with less chance the saw blade will bind in root masses.

For routine maintenance in pipes where roots have been previously removed , a low rpm/low
torque cutter can be used . These cutters typically turn at approximately 400 rpm, generating 300
pounds of torque. Bolt on skids with open ends are most often used, requiring approximately 20
minutes to change skid sizes . Due to the open skid design, caution should be observed when
using in pipes that may have dropped sections, as the cutter could become stuck. These cutters
are less expensive than high performance cutters, but suitable to perform light maintenance work.

For cutting heavy root masses or extensive use, a high rpm/high torque cutter is used. These
cutters typically turn at approximately 850 rpm or higher, generating over 1000 pounds of torque.
A drop in type, ‘closed’ skid design are commonly used, requiring approximately 2 minutes to
change skids sizes during use. Due to the closed skid design, these cutters can be used in lines
with known, or suspected, dropped joints, allowing retrieval up to a 3" drop. These cutters are
usually more expensive than low performance cutters, but they will provide a far stronger cutting
force and potentially longer field life.

Maintenance of these cutters is crucial to long field life. All root cutters need to have the water
removed after use, and oil run through the system to prevent rust build up. As a recommended
practice, after each line section has been completed, the tool should be removed from the hose,
and oil poured into the nozzle. Turn the blade to draw the oil into the cutter and drain water from
the vent. When the water turns to oil from the vent, the tool is lubricated. Be sure not to store the
cutter with water in it for longer than a few minutes to prevent rust from forming internally.

-— Bolt on skids

--- page 389 ---
ROOT SAW TYPES AND USE

Just as carpenters have different saws for different cuts, root saws are varied for different types of
work. Some root saws used with rodding machines can be used on hydraulic root cutters,
depending on the task. While these saws can be interchangable, root saws specifically designed
for use with hydraulic root cutters should be used.

Rodder root saws are not designed to be used with hydraulic root cutters, and may not hold up well
being turned at high rpm’s at high torque, especially in lines where heavy roots exist. Blade
balance is also important, especially with root cutters, as an unbalanced blade can affect bearings
in the front of the tool. Cutting tools and blades designed for use with hydraulic root saws are
typically heavier than most standard rodder tools, designed not to deform while being turned at
high rates of speed and lessen wear.

Standard root saw blade. Typically used as an all purpose P\M saw in lines
that have known or suspected roots. Flat blade allows teeth to cut closer to
the pipe surface.

Concave root saw. Commonly used as an all purpose P/M saw in pipes with
known or suspected roots. Rounded edges keep teeth away from pipe
surfaces and joints in lines that have pipe deflection problems. Rounded
shape adds higher strength to the blade over standard blades for heavy work.

Spiral root saw blade. Most often used in lines having known, heavy root
intrusion. Tapered end engages roots, creating a leading cut in heavy
masses. Light roots are drawn into the saw and cut. This blade is most
effective when used in graduating sizes when working in heavy root masses.

Extra curl root saw. Typically used to grind and cut roots that have
filled the pipe. The spiral pattern creates a multiple cutting surface that
works at the entire face of the root mass. This tool can be very effective
in lines when root s have trapped dirt and other debris in the mass.,
using the flusher to remove the grindings periodically.

© - 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Tools - Page 10

--- page 391 ---
TROUBLE SHOOTING LACK OF PRESSURE

The drop of operating pressure can be a tough problem to solve. It is usually a simple problem,
however. The following check list should help you to identify what has happened to cause lack of
top operating pressure.

1.

Check the nozzle for wear. Most cases this is the culprit. If the nozzle has a forward
orifice, place the stem end of a pop rivet, from the inside, through the opening. Attach the
nozzle to the hose and run the pressure up. If the cleaner gets to full pressure quickly, you
need to replace the nozzle. If not, continue with the next steps.

Check the strainer. When strainer is replaced, check the assembly for suction leaks. A
suction leak can affect the pressure. Ifthe strainer is clogged, clean the strainer and
replace. Try the machine, if it is still not up to pressure, continue with the list.

Check the pump for any leaks. There may be some worn packings, or worn seals allowing
water and pressure to escape. Unless an active water leak is seen, it may not be necessary
to go inside the pump. Before working on the pump, follow the next steps.

Check the belts that drive the pump for wear, or tightness. Many times it is the power
belts slipping. Ifthe belts are glazed, torn, or checked, replace the belt and try the
machine. Ifthe belts are in good shape, continue with the list.

Next check the water control valves to see if there is any blow-by. Disconnect the return
line hose from the water control or ball valve. Pull the lever to divert the water to the
sewer hose. Run the machine, if any water comes through the control valve and out the
open end of the return line, you will need to replace or repair the valve. If no water
appears from the return line, proceed to step 6.

If the pressure relief valve does not dump to ground and is piped back into the tank or
system, disconnect the return or dump line. Run the machine, if water comes out the
dump line below maximum pressure the valve may be stuck open, or need adjustment.

If all of these steps do not tell you where the problem is, it may be in the pump, or motor.
You may need to contact SRECO-FLEXIBLE for further help. Consult the pump manual
for suggested procedures.

© ~ 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Repair - Page 2

391 of 891


--- page 392 ---
PUMP TROUBLESHOOTING - FLUID END

Common causes relating to loss of pressure are usually not in the water pump itself. Clogged
strainers, worn water control valves, worn or ‘stuck’ pressure relief valves, or worn nozzles are
the usual culprits that steal pressure. Refer to the trouble shooting guide before looking to the
pump. Basic troubleshooting of the pump can be done during operation, and should be a
regular field mainternance check. Most common things to look for in the fluid end are;

Valves - Valve damage or wear can affect pressure and flow, and can be difficult to determine
which valve may be causing the problem. Valve damage can sometimes be heard during
operation standing near the pump, or using a mechanic’s stethoscope or small pipe to listen to
each valve. Each valve consists of a seat, moving disc, spring, and retainer, which are subject to
wear. Valve discs can jam open if something large has tried to pass through it.

Valve Covers - The most common source of water leaks on a pump are
the valve access covers, which may need tightening or new ‘O” ring seals.

ao

Gear end

1\
caf
| a

| Wy 7A

J. Cylinders - Ifthe cylinders are worn or scratched, water may
/

be evident under the pump between the fluid and gear ends.

Packing - When packings wear, water may be seen leaking from
the bottom of the pump, usually between the fluid and gear end.

Icing - Cylinders, packing, and valve assemblies can be damaged with a single freeze. To avoid
catastrophic failure, be sure to use the recommended dewatering process for the system, and
remove the small drain plugs (open petcocks if installed) that drain the galleys. Ifice is suspected
in the system, place the machine in a heated area to thaw. If ice is suspected in the sewer hose,
Tun the system without nozzles to flush out the ice.

Air in the Pump - A pump will pull air into the fluid end if it runs out of water, or there is an air
leak in the suction line. A large air ‘bubble’ occurs that will prevent water movement and is
evidenced by a severe pulsation of the sewer hose. Remove the nozzle and run the system up for a
full minute to allow the air mass to move out of the pump. -

Water Purity - Solids suspended in water can erode fluid end components. Most suspended
particles are introduced throught the water tank. Be sure to flush the hydrant before each fill,
check the strainer daily, and clean the sump of the water tank periodically . If plant effluent or
drafting water source is used, secondary straining such as a fine mesh nylon strainer at the tank
fill, may be needed.

© - 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Flusher Repair - Page 4

392 of 891


--- page 393 ---
SEWER HOSE ANATOMY

Most sewer hose used today is constructed specifically for conveyance of pressurized water.
Generally speaking, there are three grades of sewer hose used. Common, high, and specialized
grades. Each grade of hose offers different properties from low cost considerations, to high end
applications.

Common grade hose is typically the least expensive, using layered construction and a
polyethylene inner core. High grade is usually a little more expensive, as layered construction
is fuse bonded to each layer, and a hytrel inner core is used. Specialized grades of hose are far
more expensive, and include extremely high pressure applications, or single material
composition.

Longevity of sewer hose is difficult to predict due to the various conditions found in collection
systems. There are, however, some basic guidelines that can be used to make a fair assessment
of how a particular hose will perform.

Side Wall Thickness: As a general rule, the thicker the walls of a hose, the higher resistance to
cuts and abrasive wear will be seen. Thicker walled hose will tend to resist kinks, especially
where attached to fittings over thinner walled hose.

Construction: Bonded, or fused construction permanently bonds the outer jacket, nylon
reinforcing, and inner core, into one piece. Layered construction can allow the outer jacket to
separate from the nylon jacket and inner core. This difference can be seen when cuts occur in
the outer jacket. Ifthe jacket can be peeled off in larger pieces similar to a banana, it is
probably not bonded. Ifthe jacket cannot be peeled and only small pieces can be pulled off, it
is most likely bonded.

Inner Core: The thickness and density of the inner core determines the elasticity and strength
of the hose. As and example, hytrel tends not to stretch as much as polyethylene. Ifthe hose is
pulled when a nozzle becomes stuck, the potential for hidden damage increases with elasticity.
The thickness of the inner core can affect water volume capacity, and should measure a true 1"
or .75" ID for trailer or truck mounted systems to operate at full capacity.

Bend Radius: The bend radius of a hose determines how tight a bend can be made before a

kink, or permanent bend in the hose occurs. Asa rule, the lower the bend radius, the higher the
resistance to kinking, especially at coupling connections.

Outer jacket

Nylon reinforcing webbing

Wall thickness

HOSE COUPLINGS AND REPAIR

When sewer hose is damaged, couplings are usually needed to perform repairs. While this is not
a difficult process, there are some considerations to be made for successful coupling installation.

When installing new end or splice fittings, be sure to use the proper size fitting to accommodate
the wall thickness of the hose on the machine. Often is best to purchase fittings made by the
hose manufacturer than take a chance on having one fail.

Good hose fittings have internal ribs designed to bite into the hose and prevent it from pulling
out. Hydraulic assisted presses are usually needed for installation, however, field repair kits
using draw down bolts can provide a safe installation if properly used.

Cut the sewer hose a few feet from any damaged areas to insure hose integrity for the coupling to
mount on. Be sure the cut is as square as possible, as uneven cuts may prevent a solid seal to the

coupling. Be sure the sewer hose is free of oil or grease on the outer jacket where the coupling
will mount.

Place the new coupling on the end of the hose, and draw a line on the hose at the end of the

coupling. Ifthe coupling is properly pressed on, the line will be very close to, if not right on the
edge of the coupling.

Avoid using temporary in-line splice couplings unless under emergency conditions. Temporary
fittings are typically larger in diameter than the sewer hose, and are threaded on by hand. Over
time, these fittings can loosen or hang up in the pipe during use, and are not designed to be used
on a permanent basis.

Despite the best efforts to protect the hose, all hose will eventually need replacing. While there
are different parameters used in determining when to acquire new hose between different
agencies, there are a few common considerations; 1. Age of the current hose, 2. Many in-line
splicer couplings (more than 5 in $00‘), 3. Overall length and is it enough to reach between the
farthest manholes, and 4. Overall condition of the hose and number of cuts, peeling sections, etc.

Coupling Types Cut away of coupling
