USING THE PROPER TOOL FOR THE JOB

We have all heard that using the right tool for the job can make all the difference. Some of us learn this
lesson when we try to loosen a stubborn nut on a toilet tank with a few taps of a hammer and discover
porcelain has little to no elastic properties. We usually learn this lesson repeatedly in adult life due to
convenience. It can be inconvenient to stop what you are doing to go get the proper tool, however, as
many of us can attest, the results of not doing this can be far more than inconvenient. The lessons of
using the proper tool for the job is especially true with Rodding Machines. Using the wrong tool for a
given cleaning application can cause extensive rod or tool damage, can damage pipes, can damage the
machine, or hurt the operator. Part of the art of operating Rodders safely and effectively is
understanding the many different tools that can be used and what each is for.

There are four basic categories of tool groups used with Rodding Machines; Boring Tools, Cutting
Tools, Finishing Tools, and Specialty Tools. Each tool group with its own specific design function and
each tool in the group having it’s own specific application.

Boring Tools:

Boring tools perform as their name suggests, they bore into materials, their corkscrew shaped design
separating them from other tool groups . These tools are used to remove stoppages, as piloting tools to
allow larger tools to be used, and to retrieve objects in pipes. Having unique working abilities in their
ability to be turned clockwise and counterclockwise, boring tools are critical to the removal of roots and
grease. These tools operate at low RPMs, typically between 20 and 30 RPM for best effectiveness.

Cutting Tools:

Cutting tools are primarily root saws, shaped or constructed for different uses. Much as there are
different types of saws used to cut lumber, there are different types of saws used to cut roots.
Depending on the type of roots, density, and intrusion level, there is a saw to meet it. Root saws can
only be used clockwise in the pipe, and are most effective when turned at approximately 45 - 55 RPM,
moved slowly in the pipe. Root saws should be preceded by a piloting auger and used in pull back
mode to do their heavy cutting work. This puts less strain on the rods than pushing the tool, and allows
the saw to deliver all of its cutting power to the root mass. .

Finishing Tools:

Finishing tools are the last tools used in cleaning operations. They are designed to remove residue of
materials boring or cutting tools may leave in the pipe. These tools are not sized smaller than the pipe,
as are other tools, they fit snugly to scour the pipe walls free of grease and/or root materials. For best
efficiency, a rotation speed of approximately 60 - 70 RPM should be used, with very slow movement in
the pipe to allow several revolutions per foot. These tools are essential to use in heavy grease and root
control, effectively lengthening the frequency between cleaning.

Specialty Tools:

Specialty tools have special designs which are used in unique cleaning or pipe work situations. From
cleaning tools that penetrate hardened slurry composites, to tools used to pull back cables for cameras
and bucket machines, to finding buried manholes, these tools fill a variety of needs. Typically not usable
outside of their design function, they are highly effective when used as designed.

© 2002 Tilson & Associates, LLC Sewer Maintenance Methods and Tools - Page 9

397 of 891


--- page 398 ---
ROD COUPLING TOOLS

Used for a variety of purposes, these tools are not used to clean pipe, but to work with rods
themselves. To properly attach tools, work with rod couplings, feed through guide hoses, or
retrieve if broken, these tools should be kept with the machine at all times.

The assembly wrench is used to loosen or tighten
coupling nuts. The jog in the handle is to allow
knuckles to pass each other without hitting when
using both the assembly wrench and the assembly
turning handle. ‘The tapered end of the tool is for
use in anchoring the coupling tools are being
attached to, or when the turning handle is not handy.

The assembly turning handle is designed to anchor
the rod coupling through the center hole for leverage
to loosen or tighten nuts on the rod coupling while
removing/connecting rods, or working with tools.
The locking pin moves in and out to lock onto the
coupling and hold it firmly. This tool is much safer
and easier to use than broken rods or other ill fitting
tools to hold the rod coupling against stubborn nuts.

The pull out tool design is to allow rod to turn while
a coupling is pushed during hand rod operations.
Today it is used primarily to set the rod pressure and
robotic system during the machine set up. Far safer
than using a broken rod through the coupling in this
process if the reel is turned, the tool allows the rod
to turn.

The pilot bullet tool is used primarily to guide the
rod through the guide. This tool is be hand
tightened on and retracted into the machine so on
the next use it will pilot its way down the guide hose
and protect the rod coupling threads and the inside
of the guide hose. The tool can also can be used to
push up the pipe to tow back cable or ropes. When
pulling cable, the swivel bullet tool is recommended
to allow the cable to turn without kinking.

The pick up tool is used when a string of rod breaks
in the pipe. Designed to rotate onto a rod and catch
a coupling on the pull back, this is a critical tool to

have on the machine.

Photos courtesy of SRECO-Flexible

© 2002 Tilson & Associates, LUC Sewer Maintenance Methods and Tools - Page 10

398 of 891


--- page 399 ---
BORING TOOLS

The use of boring tools with rodders is essential for most all cleaning work. Boring tools pilot
small holes through roots and grease which allow larger tools to be used until full pipe sized
tools can be pulled through. Boring tools have specific design functions, from stoppage removal
to mineral deposit removal, to piloting for larger tool use. Boring tools should be turned
relatively slowly, around 25 to 35 RPMs, and moved slowly in the pipe. These tools provide the
perfect means to carry the rod to the far manhole where tool changes to other pull tools can be
easily made. Designed to operate clockwise in the pipe, these tools can be turned counter
clockwise to free them from materials if they get snared, or to work their way over misaligned
pipe connections that clockwise rotation will not let them pass.

The squarebar corkscrew is designed to be used on
stoppages. It rips and pulls its way through material
that makes up static blockages, with an open
winding of the blade that allows debris to flush

through the tool as it relieves the blockage.

The auger is used for piloting a hole to the distant
manhole in roots and grease. From this hole, larger
tools can be pulled through the line to clear any
organic material deposits or growth. Extremely
effective in grease when used in graduating sizes to
pipe'size, it can remove this material in small chunks.
Augers are also used as an all purpose tool when it is
not known what is in the line.

The double point corkscrew is used to bite into
fabrics in the pipe and remove them by slowly
twisting and pulling them out. Water will gradually
lubricate the pipe around the material, allowing it to
be pulled out. Other uses include removing
frisbees, cans, and plastic bottles, jugs, and buckets.

The round wire corkscrew is designed to remove
mineral deposits in small pipes of 3" to 8" diameter.
The resiliency of this tool allows it to penetrate and
pick apart lime and other deposits. It can also be
used as a simple traversing tool from one manhole
to the next.

The sand corkscrew is used to traverse pipe
— N A KK ke vA Y YY which has filled with sand or to open pipes
after floods. The tool allows water to follow
using the rear threads and allow water flow or
a cable to be attached for bucket machine use.

. Photos courtesy of SRECO-Flexible
© 2002 Tilson & Associates, LLC Sewer Maintenance Methods and Tools - Page 11

399 of 891


--- page 400 ---
ROOT SAWS

For most any kind of root cutting work necessary in pipes, there is a corresponding root saw.
Root saws range from heavy cutting with heavy blades to light curtain root cutting with very
thin blades. Each root saw is designed for a specific task, much as wood working saws are
designed for specific work. Root saws generally need to be turned relatively quickly to
operate as designed, about 50 - 60 RPMs. They should also be pulled as opposed to pushed in
the pipe to reduce torque damage to rods and to cut more effectively in the same plane.

The standard root saw, or flat blade as they are sometimes called, are used
for heavy cutting work. The flat edge of the blade allows the teeth of the

saw to travel closer to the pipe to cut roots that are pushed to the side by the
force of the cutting action.

The concave root saw uses a shaped, or curved, blade for strength and to
keep the teeth of the saw away from pipe edges. This is the most commonly
used root saw for heavy cutting work due to their strength and ability to work
in sensitive pipes without scarring the inside with the cutting teeth.

The Fine blade root saw uses a very thin steel blade with a reinforced back
to keep its shape. The thin blade cuts fine roots such as curtain roots and
new growth, and does not push them off to the sides like heavier steel tooth

root saws.
* >

Neg a = P w

A \ The spiral root saw uses a blade which has an auger shape to it to create a

js \ taper that engages very thick root structures and draws the rest of the
3X - ki blade into the cut. The tool is effective in light roots as the roots can be
\ at drawn into the saw and cut in several places, unlike standard root saws

. A which cut in a single plane.

The extra cur] root saw uses an extra wrap of the blade to provide a full
face cutting surface for working on willow and other full pipe mass root
structures. Very effective as a push cutting saw, though caution of over
torque has to be observed during use.

The tandem root saw is used when pull cutting cannot be easily done,
such as in a long run without enough rod, or blocked upstream access.
The second blade prevents the front root saw from trying to turn and
climb to the top of the pipe. The second blade can be a fine blade saw
to provide a surgical cut after the heavier cutting blade has passed.

Photos courtesy of SRECO-Flexible
& 2602 Tilson & Associates, LLC Sewer Maintenance Methods and Tools ~ Page 12

400 of 891


--- page 401 ---
SCOURING TOOLS

Scouring tools, sometimes referred to as finishing tools, are designed to scour pipe walls after
cleaning operations to remove hair roots, grease and other debris root saws and boring tools can
miss. Made at or slightly larger than the pipe size, these tools compress slighly as they are pulled
into the pipe. They should be rotated quickly, about 60 to 70 RPMs and pulled back slowly for
full effect. Especially useful in completely removing hardened grease and avoiding large chunk
removal when used in graduating sizes up to pipe size. This controls the way grease is cut out
and can avoid stoppages. As a regrowth preventer, finishing tools scour roots from the sidewalls
of the pipe, cutting exploratory roots away which lengthens the time between return maintenance
calls.

The three blade cutter is used in grease and root
applications. The tool is designed to be pushed
if necessary and is a choice tool in lines that
serpentine and do not allow conventional tools
to be used due to misaligned pipes.

The curved blade cutter is used primarily in
grease applications, the tool is tapered to allow
increasingly larger bites to be taken when
being pulled back slowly. The short cutting
edge can get under hardened deposits of grease
and pry them free. Not necessarily
recommended for root removal work due to the
limited cutting surface, it can be used if no
other scouring tool is available.

The four blade cutter is used primarily on root
masses, especially those of very fine root matter
such as willow or chinese elm trees. The thin
roots of these trees can be difficult to cut,
however, the very thin cutting blades of these
tools can be very effective in cutting away this
light root matter.

. Photos courtesy of SRECO-Flexible
© 2002 Tilson & Associates, LLC Sewer Maintenance Methods and Tools - Page 13

401 of 891


--- page 402 ---
SPECIALTY TOOLS

There are a variety of specialty tools used with rodding machines which are designed for unique
tasks or problems. Typically these tools are not used outside of these functions, but when used
for the specific application they were designed for, these tools can provide very good results.

The spear point is used for a variety of things. It is
designed to dislodge materials in pipes like eggshells,
fine broken glass, and other materials that tend to lay
flat and are not easily moved with flushing machines
unless disturbed. It can be used as a stoppage tool, but
is not as effective as a square bar corkscrew in that job.

The sand leader is made to move on top of sand in a
pipe to dig at it and allow flushing equipment to
remove it. The curved sides tend to let the tool remain
on top of the material and is frequently used to thread
camera or bucket machine cable between manholes.

The loop tool is a simple tool designed to pull cable
or rope between manholes. Its cousin the swivel loop
is primarily for cable to allow the cable to twist when
being pulled so rod is not twisted in the process.

The turn type porcupine is used when scale deposits
such as concrete or paving have adhered to the pipe
walls. Pulled back and forth in the pipe and turned,
the heavy bristles can pry off stubborn scale. Caution
should be used in being sure the pipe is the same size
the entire section.

The lateral locator is used to help find buried
manholes. Placed in the pipe at the 6/12 position and
not rotated, the tool will expand when it enters a
manhole, giving exact footage to the structure. This
tool is frequently used in pipes running at full flow
which cameras cannot see manhole structures as they
pass through them.

5 Photos courtesy of SRECO-Flexible
© 2002 Tilson & Associates, LLC Sewer Maintenance Methods and Tools - Page 14

403 of 891


--- page 404 ---
HYDROGEN SULFIDE (H2S) IN MANHOLES

Hydrogen sulfide is a naturally occurring gas from the biological breakdown of organic materials.
When bacteria and organisms breakdown organic material, waste by products are produced in the
form of hydrogen sulfide. H2S has a distinctive odor of rotten eggs, and is heavier than air so it is
found low in the manhole if present. Above 200 parts per million, hydrogen sulfide will deaden
the sense of smell, so detectors are the only safe means of testing for this gas. The following
chart shows health effects at various levels of H2S.

5 ppm: Moderate odor, easily detectable by smell
10 ppm: Eye irritation can begin, bloodshot eyes may indicate presence of gas
30 ppm: Very strong odor of rotten eggs
100 ppm: _—_ Loss of smell in 2 - 15 minutes, breathing irritation resulting in coughing
300 ppm: _ Rapid loss of smell, difficulty breathing without coughing
500- 700 ppm: Unconsciousness within a short time, possible death in 30 minutes
700-1000 ppm: Rapid unconsciousness. Breathing stops
1000-2000 ppm: Instant unconsciousness, involuntary muscles cease, death in a few minutes
43000 ppm: Lower explosive limit

If hydrogen sulfide is present, it is most likely to be found;

Down low in the manhole

Where there is poor ventilation like pump stations, wet pits, or deep manhole structures
Where flow is stopped, either a stoppage, or area that organics settle out and decay

In large lines that are flowing slow and nearly full

At discharge points of long force mains due to organics clinging to pipe walls

Hydrogen sulfide is strong enough to cause death by itself, or by causing unconsciousness leading
to death by falling or drowning. Remember, sense of smel! will be lost when H2S is present in
amounts that can cause death or unconsciousness, you will not be able to smell it.

Respect hydrogen sulfide gas, and take the proper steps to detect and disperse it;

Use knowledge and experience to suspect it in the system

Use gas detecting equipment to test manholes and other confined spaces before entry
Use a blower to vent all confined spaces entered, H2S may become present after entry
Use a safety harness when working in manholes

Do not attempt a rescue attempt if proper self contained breathing apparatus and entry equipment
are not available. One half of confined space fatalities are. would be rescuers who thought they
would not be overcome by the same gasses someone else was. Call in the emergency and wait for
trained personnel to arrive. Open manhole covers nearest the accident manhole, and start blowing
air into the effected manhole to disperse the gas.

© - 1997 SRECO-FLEXIBLE Field Safety - Page 4

404 of 891


--- page 405 ---
HAZARDOUS ATMOSPHERE REVIEW

Among hazardous atmospheres, there are three primary groups;

Lack of oxygen - carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide
Hazardous atmospheres - hydrogen sulfide, industrial wastes
Explosive atmospheres - methane, petro-chemicals

Test and ventilate underground or confined space structures before entry to avoid accidents.
Follow these basic rules;

Common Places Found

Lack of Oxygen Hazardous Atmosphere Explosive atmosphere
Down low near the bottom Down low near the bottom Methane - top of structure
Sealed structures Large pipes running full Fuel fumes - down low
Meter vaults Wet pits, and pump stations Near swamps or old dumps
Non-vented manholes Long force main ends Near industrial areas

Symptoms of Exposure

Lack of Oxygen Hazardous Atmosphere Explosive atmosphere
Difficulty breathing Smells of rotten eggs Smells like fuel
Ringing in ears Difficulty breathing Difficulty breathing
Dizziness or nausea Dizziness or nausea Dizziness or nausea
Coughing Coughing Coughing
Drowsiness Unable to think clearly

IF ANY OF THESE SYMPTOMS OCCUR - GET OUT!!

Do not attempt a rescue of a victim if:
You are alone, call for help immediately - seconds count

You do not have proper emergency air equipment or do not know how to use it
~ You do not have a safety harness or other confined space entry equipment

REMEMBER!

% of all fatalities in confined space accidents are would be rescuers believing they would not be
affected by the same conditions as the victim, or not knowing what the conditions were.

Don’t be a dead hero and an ineffective rescuer

City of San Mateo
Environmental Services
Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)

SOP NO.:: d

DIVISION: Uvtilities

~”

PROCEDURE TITLE: Confined Spaces

INTRODUCTION

This SOP is a guide to use when it is needed to work in a confined space. A confined
space is defined by Cal-OSHA as the concurrent existence of the following condi-
tions: (1) existing ventilation is insufficient to remove dangerous air contamination
and/or oxygen deficiency which may exist or develop; and (2) ready access or egress
for the removal of a suddenly disabled employee is difficult due to the location
and/or size of the opening(s). Dangerous air contamination is defined as an atmos-
phere presenting a threat of causing death, injury, acute illness or disablement due
to the presence of flammable, explosive, toxic or otherwise injurious or incapaci-
tating substances. Included in this definition are:

1. Sanitary sewer lift stations.

2. Sanitary sewer and storm manholes.

3. Water, electrical and pump station vaults.
4. Tanks, stationary or portable.

5. Pits, which either have covers or have insufficient natural ventilation to allow
for a safe atmosphere.

~ 6. Excavation or construction trenches.

‘OPERATING PROCEDURE

Preentry
1. Entry is not allowed until the atmosphere within a confined space is found to
be safe. All air tests should be conducted by qualified persons who have
received specific training on the use of air monitoring equipment.
whe |

409 of 891


--- page 410 ---
Bi

In every confined space area, air testing should include, but not be limited to,
oxygen deficiency, toxic gases and the presence of flammable or combustible
gases.

If monitoring for carbon monoxide, levels must remain below 35 ppm. When
not using SCBA equipment, the oxygen level must be above 19.5 percent and
not exceed 23 percent. Lower explosive limit (LEL) must be maintained below
10 percent. Hydrogen sulfide level must be maintained below 10 ppm.

When using SCBA equipment, the oxygen level should not be below 16 percent
and not exceed 23 percent. Hydrogen sulfide level should not exceed the
Cal-OSHA requirement of 50 ppm. LEL should be below 10 percent. When
using SCBA equipment at the sewer lift station, spike readings lasting more
than 10 seconds must be acted upon and evacuation of the confined space must
begin. If the monitor clears to below allowed levels within 10 seconds, work
may proceed.

No smoking will be permitted within 25' of the confined space.

Procedures

1.

The confined space shall be checked with an approved air monitoring device by
inserting a probe in the confined space prior to entry. If an alarm sounds
during the monitoring process or the employee feels there could be an unsafe
condition, do not enter until it is made safe. If toxic gas levels are below

50 ppm and LEL is below 10 percent, the space may be open for ventilation, but
not entered. Once this process has been completed and preentry conditions are
met, entry can be made. A gas monitoring device probe shall be lowered into
the confined space and kept inside during the entire maintenance process
when entry is required. If an alarm should sound and acceptable levels are
exceeded, secure the area and notify the supervisor immediately. The follow-
ing areas shall be referred to as confined spaces and be assigned by type as
follows:

a. Sanitary sewer and storm manholes—Permitted Confined Space.
b. Sewer lift stations—Permitted Confined Space.

c. Water tanks—Permitted Confined Space.

d. Water valve pits—Nonpermitted Confined Space.

e. Water meter vaults or pits—Nonpermitted Confined Space.

f. Trenches—Nonpermitted Confined Space.

A

410 of 891


--- page 411 ---
If any employee encounters a situation which does not fit this list precisely, or
if there is a question concerning whether or not it is a confined space, assume it
to be a Permitted Confined Space. Under no circumstances is a manhole or any
cover of an enclosed area to be opened without first testing the space for toxic
gases, combustibles, or the lack of oxygen.

Classification Permitted Confined Space

The procedures specified in Title 8, Article 108, Section 5159, will be followed,
including setup and use of all required equipment. The exception to Section 5159
will be that the person or persons in the confined space need not wear approved
respiratory protective equipment (SCBA) if the space is constantly ventilated and the
air quality continually tests at a safe level. Entry into the confined space is by permit
only. The confined space entry permit or work permit is a written authorization
that certifies that all existing hazards have been evaluated and necessary precautions
have been taken to ensure the safety of each worker. Permits must be dated, signed
and posted. Supervisors are responsible for the evaluation and the posting of the
permit.

1. An approved safety harness and safety line are required for entry into a
Permitted Confined Space. The line shall be attached to either the hoist on the
tripod or some other fixed object. At the sewer lift station screen room, the
rescue plan shall consist of the two standby employees equipped with SCBA
equipment and a stretcher to remove an employee not wearing a safety
harness.

2. Atleast two employees, currently certified for CPR/first aid, shall stand by on
the outside of the confined space ready to give assistance in case of emergency.
One employee shall be continually monitoring the atmosphere in the confined
space. At least one additional employee, who may have other duties, shall be
within sight or maintain radio communication with the standby employee.
An effective means of communication between workers in the confined space
and the standby worker must be maintained.

3. The standby employee shall have an SCBA available for immediate use.

4. Work involving the use of flame, arc, spark or other source of ignition is
prohibited within a confined space which contains dangerous air contamina-
tion due to flammable and/or explosive substances.

5. If the air quality becomes unsafe as measured by an approved air monitoring
device, the space will be immediately evacuated. Ventilate the area for five
minutes and wait for air quality to improve. If air quality does not improve,
SCBA equipment will be needed.

411 of 891


--- page 412 ---
If the employee in the confined space becomes incapacitated due to the unsafe
atmospheric conditions, a standby employee shall immediately notify the
person within sight. This second person shall call Communications either
through the nonemergency radio or by dialing 9-1-1.

The standby employee shall immediately begin lifting the fallen employee out
of the confined space. The second standby employee will assist in removing
the victim from the confined space and begin to administer CPR and/or first
aid.

Classification Nonpermitted Confined Space

1.

2

The entire lid or cover shall be removed, and the space shall be ventilated.

If the atmosphere tests acceptable, the employee need not wear the harness nor
be attached to the hoisting device.

Employee may now enter the space to perform work. Entry into this space is
acceptable by ladder or hoist or by any other appropriate device or piece of
equipment. The atmosphere shall be continually monitored while the
employee remains in the confined space.

If the air quality becomes unsafe, as measured by the approved air monitoring
device, the employee shall immediately evacuate the confined space.

If the employee in the confined space becomes incapacitated due to accident or
injury and the atmosphere still tests acceptable, rescue will only be performed
by Fire Department personnel or under the direction of a medical technician.

If the employee in the confined space becomes incapacitated due to the unsafe
atmospheric conditions, the standby employee shall immediately notify the
person within sight. This second person shall call Communications either
through the nonemergency radio or by dialing 9-1-1.

Any rescue can only be attempted after atmospheric conditions have been

- made safe or with the use of SCBA equipment, if available at the site. The

standby employee shall immediately begin lifting the fallen employee out of
the confined space. The second standby employee will assist in removing the
victim from the confined space and begin to administer CPR and/or first aid.

Definitions

Ls

Qualified Person: The Qualified Person is the person designed to serve as the
on-site monitor during confined space operations. This individual must
understand the use of safety equipment and assure compliance with the safety
procedures developed for the confined space operation. This person shall be

-4-

412 of 891


--- page 413 ---
capable of specifying necessary control and/or protective action necessary for
workers’ safety. Additionally, the Qualified Person must be trained in the use
of combustible gas /oxygen deficiency and toxic gas meters and in CPR/first aid.
All City of San Mateo Utilities Department employees engaged in con-
fined space entry, either in the active or standby roles, are required to fulfill the
training requirements of the Qualified Person.

Standby Person: As with all other personnel involved in confined space entry
operations, the Standby Person is thoroughly trained in the confined space
entry procedures and the use of safety and monitoring equipment. Standby
Person should be stationed at the access opening of any confined space while
such space is occupied for any reason. At all times, the Standby Person must
maintain visual contact with every occupant in the confined space, where the
construction of the space permits, or should have continuous knowledge of the
activities and well-being of every occupant of the confined space.

MAINTENANCE (Equipment, Tools)

1

Barriers and warning signs must be placed at or near the entrance to the
confined space.

Any electrical service or equipment connected to a confined space should be
locked in the “off" position and tagged if such service presents a hazard to
personnel entering the confined space. Follow SOP that pertains to lock-out,
tag-out procedures.

When unsafe conditions are detected by air monitoring equipment or by other
means, the work area should be ventilated and made safe before entry, as
determined by follow-up monitoring. Low-pressure type air movers (blowers)
or fans should be used to purge the confined space prior to personnel entry.
Refer to the SOP for the air monitoring equipment of gases and vapors.

SAFETY PRECAUTIONS

In general, personnel authorized to enter a confined space should be provided with
and use the following equipment:

i.

es

Safety harness—Full harness to be worn whenever feasible.
Lifeline—2,000 Ibs. test strength.

Portable ladder or other approved means of entry and exit provided such
means would not obstruct the access opening.

Several means of illumination equipment which are explosion-proof.

413 of 891


--- page 414 ---
Nonsparking striking, chipping, hammering, or cutting tools and equipment in
a confined space where an explosive or flammable air conditions exist.

Other personal protective equipment (i.e., eye protection, chemical-resistant
gloves) should be taken into consideration by the work environment, the work
being performed and skin absorption potential of the chemicals involved.

Air-driven power tools (where flammable liquid is present) whenever feasible
should be used.

Cylinders of compressed gas should never be taken into a confined space.

City of San Mateo
Environmental Services

GAS ALERT MICRO 5 PID

Follow safety rules at all times. Your life and the lives of your fellow

1)

3)

workers depend on it.

Always “quick check” your gas detector or continuously
monitor immediately before use each time at the work site.
Even if your company practice requires you to test your
equipment at the beginning of each shift, or at the work center,

anything can happen to your equipment en route from job to
job.

Test the atmosphere in a manhole before removing the cover
whenever possible, i.e.: through the cover puller access holes.

Never enter a manhole without first testing for combustible
gases and purging the atmosphere within the manhole with a
power ventilator. Then ensure continuous power ventilation
after you enter, and all the while you are working in a manhole.

if gas is detected and/or your unit alarms while you are working in
manhole, leave the manhole immediately. Do no re-enter until the
cause is determined and corrected.

Remember, it only takes a second for an accident to happen.

Qupwicorpyard\Sewers\SOPs\SS Manhole Monitoring Procedures.doc

Page 5 of 6

418 of 891


--- page 419 ---
After every attempt has not succeeded to restore the space to normal limits for gas
concentrations, the supervisor will work with local authorities to evacuate or correct or
remedy the problem, as necessary.

Note: If there is no hole in the manhole lid, create a work order to change out the lid,
after the scenario has ended. Give the work order to a lead worker.

Employee Responsibility

Employees must follow all City procedures to ensure personal safety and the safety of others
when responding to high/low gas alarms. Failure to do so may result in serious injury or fatal
consequences. Employees not sure of work environment shall contact a lead worker before
proceeding. It is each employee’s responsibility to operate the gas detection testing equipment
safely. Employees will properly maintain, inspect, test, and ensure the gas detection testing
equipment is within the calibration standards at the time of use.

Management Responsibility
Management is responsible for providing the proper equipment and training for employees and

for ensuring that employees follow the established procedures and guidelines. Management will
coordinate emergency activities with local authorities.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
SANITARY SEWER MANHOLE MONITORING PROCEDURES

Policy

Safe manhole monitoring procedures are required for all employees and contractors who open
manholes within the San Mateo service area,

Purpose

To ensure employee safety, establish procedures and responsibility for opening sanitary system
manholes.

Equipment

Gad detector

Manhole hook

Pry bar

Spark Resistant Sledge Hammer (when lid is stuck and no LEL is present)

Procedure

Testing

© Setup traffic control and secure the area.

e Using a multi-gas detector, place the tip of the hose into a vent hole of the space to be
tested Gust the tip of the probe — do not push probe into dustpan). if there is no vent hole,
crack the lid slightly using a pry bar and then prop the lid open just enough to insert the
gas detector hose into the space. Observing the meter on the detector, note the reading
for a minimum of 30 seconds. If no alarm sounds, the employee may remove the
manhole cover using a manhole hook. If the lid is stuck, a sledge hammer should be used
to break the seal before straining with a hook. If an alarm sounds, do not remove the
lid.

e Assess upstream and downstream structures (2 structures in each direction) using testing
protocol. If the readings are satisfactory at either of these locations, follow ventilation
procedures. Used forced air purge. DO NOT use a blower in the exhaust mode to purge
an LEL condition.

e If high readings continue, do not open the manhole. Contact your lead worker or
supervisor.

Q\pwicorpyard\Sewers\SOPs\SS Manhole Monitoring Procedures.doc Page 1 of 6
420 of 891


--- page 421 ---
e Remain at site until a lead worker or the supervisor arrives.

e When there is a question of an environmental impact, the employee will notify Source
Control. The designated Source Control Inspector will contact the customer, when
appropriate.

Carbon Monoxide (CO)

Below 25% - No alarm
Remove cover and proceed with work.

25% and above — Alarm
De not remove manhole lid; retest.

1) Ifan alarm condition exists, notify a lead worker.
2) Assess upstream and downstream structures (2 structures in each direction) using same
testing protocol. If the readings are satisfactory at either of these locations, follow

ventilation procedures.

3) Ifreadings continue to be unsatisfactory, select a structure with lowest reading levels;
position yourself up-wind and remove lid allowing for natural ventilation.

4) Ifnatural ventilation does not clear space, install a blower to power ventilate.
Hydrogen Sulfide Gas (H2S)

0-9 ppm — No alarm
Remove cover and proceed.

10-49 ppm — Alarm
Do not remove manhole lid; retest.

1) Assess upstream and downstream structures (2 structures in each direction) using same
protocol. Ifthe readings are satisfactory at either of these locations, follow ventilation

procedures.

2) Select structure with lowest level; position yourself up-wind and remove lid allowing for
natural ventilation.

3) Ifnatural ventilation does not clear space, install a blower to power ventilate.
Procedures for high LEL > 10%, Oxygen > 23.5% & H,S >50 PPM

1) Call (650) 522-7350 (lead worker) and ask for the COp fire extinguisher to be delivered.

Q:\pw\corpyard\Sewers\SOPs\SS Manhole Monitoring Procedures.doc Page 2 of 6

421 of 891


--- page 422 ---
2) Meet with the reporting employee to verify the readings and continue to monitor the
space.

3) Wash down lid and the surrounding area with water to dampen the spark potential.

4) While continuing to monitor the space, use a 20-lb CO2 extinguisher, place the nozzle in
the lid vent hole or under the raised edge of the lid, and discharge the extinguisher gas to
insert the space.

5) Leave a gas detection device in place to monitor the space continuously, ensuring that the
CO> gas saturates the space, lowering the explosive potential (LEL).

6) When the manhole atmosphere becomes stabilized, stand up-wind of the structure and
immediately pull the lid to begin natural ventilation.

7) Remaining up-wind, retest the space. If the space cannot be naturally ventilated within
normal limits, install a blower to force ventilate the space. DO NOT USE EXHAUST
MODE TO REMOVE LEL.

8) Commence work after the space reaches normal limits.

If an employee cannot clear the condition by venting the up- or downstream-structure, he or she
will immediately contact a lead worker. All employees who open manhole lids shall follow
these procedures and guidelines.

e When there is a question of an environmental impact, industrial release or spill, the
employee will notify the Source Control Inspector. The designated Source Control
Inspector will contact the industrial customer, when appropriate.

Sampling of Manhole

Oxygen
19.5% - 23.5 % - No alarm
Remove cover and proceed with work.

Oxygen-Deficiency
Below 19.5% - Alarm and no other Alarms on the meter
Remove manhole lid; retest.

Oxygen-Enriched Atmosphere
Above 23.5% - Alarm

Do not remove manhole lid; retest.

1) Ifan alarm condition exists, notify a lead worker or supervisor.

Q:\pwicorpyard\Sewers\SOPs\SS Manhoie Monitoring Procedures.doc Page 3 of 6

422 of 891


--- page 423 ---
High oxygen levels accelerate combustion, which could also result in an explosive
condition even with low LEL readings. Use caution to avoid sparks and other ignition
sources, move vehicles and do not allow smoking in the area.

2) Assess up- and down-stream structures (2 structures in each direction) using testing

protocol. If the readings are satisfactory at either of these locations, follow ventilation
procedures.

3) Ifhigh readings continue, do not open the manhole. Contact your supervisor.

4) Remain at the site until a supervisor arrives.

5) When there is a question of an environmental impact, the employee will notify the Source
Control Inspector. The designated Source Control Inspector will contact the customer,
when appropriate.

Lower Explosive Limit
(LEL) Below 10% - No Alarm

Remove cover and proceed with work.

(LEL) 10% and above ~ Alarm
Do not remove manhole lid; retest.

1) Ifan alarm condition exists, notify a lead worker or supervisor.

13. OUTGOING RIM TO INVERT:
Code
Description
Enter in feet (to the nearest tenth of a foot) the
distance between the rim level of the manhole
(top of manhole lid) and the invert (bottom of
channel).
1
2
Solid (concealed pick holes)
Vented (pick holes & vent holes
allow rain in)
3
Gasketed (gasket present to
prevent rain inflow)
4
5
Bolted
Inner Cover (a secondary lid is
installed under top cover)
Locking (requires special tool to
unlock and remove lid)
14. OUTGOING GRADE TO INVERT:
Enter in feet (to the nearest tenth of a foot) the
depth between the grade (ground) and the invert
(bottom of channel).

Storm Drain Cleaning
CLEAR
BLOCKAGE
 Vacuum visible signs of sewage spill
(record volume of initial spill vacuumed).
 Flush storm drain with water (3x the
amount of the recorded spill volume).
 Ensure signs of spill are removed.
 Return flush water to sanitary sewer.
YES
Did spill reach storm drains?
NO
 Vacuum/remove visible signs of sewage spill (solids, grease, TP, etc).
 Set up means to return water to sanitary sewers (berms, etc.)
 Spray disinfectant (wait 10 minutes) & broom area.
AREA CLEAN UP
 Flush area affected by spill with water (use at least 3x the amount of the recorded spill volume)
ensuring water is returned to sanitary sewer.
 PHOTOGRAPH AREA AFTER CLEAN-UP
 Estimate total gallons of spill and gallons recovered. Estimate spill start and end times and duration
of spill. FILL OUT SPILL FIELD FORM completely. Return all forms and pictures to office.
 PERFORM CCTV as soon as possible or within 24 hours of spill - submit burned DVD.
 Information reviewed between Crew, Leadworkers, Supervisors and Sr. Project Manager.
DOCUMENTATION
& REPORTING

City of San Mateo

SPILL EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLAN
EASEMENT ACCESS PROCEDURE
Exhibit C
Sewer Maintenance SOP
EASEMENT ACCESS PROCEDURES
AFTER RECEIVING A SEWER COMPLAINT FROM CITY HALL OR DISPATCH…

Maintenance crew arrives at address and knocks on door.
IF RESIDENT IS HOME:


Inform resident that there is a need to inspect the sewer system located in the sewer easement on
their property.
Proceed to yard and perform required maintenance activities as neatly as possible. Try not to
disturb landscaping.



Replace manhole cover and remove all signs of activities, equipment, debris, etc.
Ensure gate(s) are shut on the way out.
Inform resident(s) of the condition encountered, work completed and if there is any necessary
follow-up. If so, provide an anticipated date and time when crews need to return.
If necessary, identify the location of the upstream and/or downstream manhole(s) in the
underground map book. Proceed to that residence and start process again.

If resident refuses entry:

Hand the resident the “Access Required” door hanger and allow them a moment to read and
understand the City ordinance that residents must provide unobstructed access to public mainlines
and manholes located on private property. If necessary, hand resident manager’s business card
and wait for resident to call.
If resident STILL refuses entry:

Return to yard and ask supervisor to mail an “Access Ordinance” letter to the resident. The letter
should provide a date and time upon which crews will return and require access.
Daytime: IF NO ONE IS HOME AND YARD IS ACCESSIBLE:

Fill out the “Access Required” door hanger explaining that there was a need for sewer crews to
access the public sewer mainline/manhole on the property. Leave on the door knob(s) of doors
expected to be used by returning residents.


Go to gate and call out (make noise) in case there is a dog in the yard.
Enter yard and proceed with maintenance activities as neatly as possible. Try not to disturb
landscaping.

After activities are completed, replace manhole cover and remove all signs of activities,
equipment, debris, etc.


Ensure gate(s) are shut on the way out.
If no one has returned, complete information on the door hanger providing the resident with
information on the condition encountered, work completed and if there is any necessary follow-
up and when crews are anticipated to return.
Daytime: NON-EMERGENCY- - IF NO ONE IS HOME AND YARD IS NOT ACCESSIBLE:

Fill out the “Access Required” door hanger explaining that there is need for sewer crews to access
the public sewer mainline/manhole on the property. Provide a date and time upon which crews
will return and expect access (gate unlocked, dogs kept inside, etc.) in order to service the
mainline of manhole.

Return at specified date and time.
If yard is still not accessible:

Return to yard and ask supervisor to mail an “Access Ordinance” letter to the resident. The letter
should provide a date and time upon which crews will return and require access.
If yard is YET AGAIN not accessible:

SAMPLE DOOR HANGER

Call communications and/or a code enforcement officer and request an escort onto the property.
Daytime: EMERGENCY - - IF NO ONE IS HOME OR UNABLE TO GET A RESPONSE &
YARD IS NOT ACCESSIBLE:


Call communications and inform them that there is a sewer emergency and crews are going into
the yard of a residence. This is important in case resident calls police about an intruder.
Fill out the “Access Required” door hanger explaining that there was a need for sewer crews to
access the public sewer mainline/manhole on the property. Leave on the door knob(s) of doors
expected to be used by returning residents.


PROCEED TO ATTEND TO THE EMERGENCY. (Crews may need to jump the locked fence).
After activities are completed, replace manhole cover and remove all signs of activities,
equipment, debris, etc.


Ensure gate(s) are shut on the way out.
If the resident has not returned, complete information on the door hanger providing the resident
with information on the condition encountered, work completed and if there is any necessary
follow-up and when crews are anticipated to return.

Call communications again to inform them you are leaving the site.
Nighttime: EMERGENCY - - IF NO ONE IS HOME OR UNABLE TO GET A RESPONSE &
YARD IS NOT ACCESSIBLE:


Call communications and inform them that there is a sewer emergency and crews REQUEST
THE PRESENCE OF AN OFFICER (ASAP).
Fill out the “Access Required” door hanger explaining that there was a need for sewer crews to
access the public sewer mainline/manhole on the property. Leave on the door knob(s) of doors
expected to be used by returning residents.

AFTER ARRIVAL OF AN OFFICER, PROCEED TO ATTEND TO THE EMERGENCY.
Do not attempt to gain access prior to the arrival of an officer.
After activities are completed, replace manhole cover and remove all signs of activities,
equipment, debris, etc.



Ensure gate(s) are shut on the way out.
If the resident has not returned, complete information on the door hanger providing the resident
with information on the condition encountered, work completed and if there is any necessary
follow-up and when crews are anticipated to return.

SPILL SERVICE CALL FORM
When a call is received by a representative of the City of San Mateo regarding a spill
complaint, or when a City representative witnesses a sewage discharge, the
following information should be recorded:
Date:
Time Call Received:
Received By:
Phone #:
Caller’s Name:
Caller Address:
Location of Spill: Estimated
Time spill began:
X-St.
NOTE: A City Representative could be any City employee, a police officer or dispatcher, fire fighter, or
administrative staff. The Sewer Division is to ensure that all representatives of the City understand the
urgency in contacting the Sewer Division directly after receiving a spill complaint and the importance of
collecting contact information and the estimated start of spill time.

INVESTIGATION AND DOCUMENTATION FORMS
SPILL VOLUME CALCULATION
The purpose of this worksheet is to capture the data and method(s) used in estimating the volume of a spill.
Since there are many variables and often unknown values involved, this calculation is just an estimate.
Additionally, it is useful to use more than one method, if possible, to validate your estimate.
Check all methods and tools that you used:
Remember to take photos!






Visual estimate
Measured surface area and volume
Duration and flow rate
Estimated daily use per capita upstream
Meter @ Pump Station
Other (use notes to explain)

barrel(s)

X 55
Total Volume Estimated
Using Visual Method
Measured Volume Method (this may take several calculations as may have to break down the odd shaped spill to
rectangles, circles, and polygons)
If the entire spill is settled in one area, calculate the volume of spill in feet (L' X W' X D') and convert to gallons (X7.48 for
gallons in a square area, and X.785 for gallons in a circular area). It is important when guessing depth to measure, if
possible in several locations and use an average depth.
1. 
Draw a sketch of the spill in the space provided on next page
2. 
Draw shapes and dimensions used for calculations
3. 
Use correct formula for various shapes (see table below)
Spill Shape
Rectangle
Volume Calculation Formula
L x W x Dx7.48
Volume Result
Circle
3.14 x R² x Dx0.785
Show formula used
Polygons

City of San Mateo
Public Works Department
Environmental Services Division
SPILL EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLAN
INVESTIGATION AND DOCUMENTATION FORMS
USE THIS SPACE TO DRAW A SKETCH OF THE SPILL SHAPE AND DIMENSIONS
Duration and Flow Rate Method
Start date and time:
End date and time:
Total spill duration:
Subtract line 1 from line 2. Show time in minutes
Average flow rate in GPM:
Use photo chart to estimate flow rate
(account for diurnal patterns if long duration)
Total volume estimate:
minutes x gpm
Upstream Connections Method
If you are dealing with a spill that has been running into a storm drain, you must estimate the gallons by: the amount of the
spill times the number of upstream connections on the receiving line (200 gal. per household per 24 hr) and estimate the time
that the flow has been occurring. Each residence contributes about 240 gallons per day or about 10 gallons per hour.
Multiply the number of residences by 10 and by the number of hours. This gives you the number of gallons.
EXAMPLE A: If you have a line with 6 houses on it and it has been spilling for 24 hours :
6 houses x 200 gallons per house per 24 hours = 1,200 gal.
EXAMPLE B: If you have 60 houses on a line that has been overflowing for 4 hours :
60 houses x 10 gallons per house per hour x 4 hours= 2,400 gal.
Pump Station Method
If the flow is coming from a pump station, use the previous day's (same weather) flow and pump capacity to estimate the flow.
Additional Notes (attach extra pages if needed):

CALCULATION AND ESTIMATION METHODS
Sewer Maintenance Spill
Calculation and Estimation Methods
Outlined in this document are three methods that are most often employed for estimating the
volume of sanitary sewer spill. The City staff preparing the estimate should utilize the most
appropriate method for the sewer spill in question and use the best information available.
Method 1: Eyeball method
The volume of small spills can be estimated using an “eyeball estimate”. To use this method
imagine the amount of water that would spill from a container listed on the table below. A jug
contains 1 gallon, a bucket contains 5 gallons, and a drum contains 55 gallons. If the spill is
larger than 55 gallons, try to break the standing water into 55 gal drums and then multiply by 55
gallons. This method is useful for contained spills up to approximately 220 gallons.
Size of
container
Water jug
Bucket
How many
of this size?
Size Multiplier
Total Volume
Estimated (gal)
(gal)
X 1
X 5
55 gal drum
X 55
Total volume
estimated
Method 2: Measured Volume
The volume of most small spills that have been contained can be estimated using this method.
The shape, dimensions, and the depth of the contained wastewater are needed. The shape and
dimensions are used to calculate the area of the spills and the depth is used to calculate the
volume.
Common Shapes and Dimensions
Steps for Volume Calculation
Step 1
Step 2
Step 3
Step 4
Step 5
Sketch the shape of the contained sewage (see figure above).
Measure or pace off the dimensions.
Measure the depth at several locations and select an average.
Convert the dimensions, including depth, to feet.
Calculate the area in square feet using the following formulas:

--- page 491 ---
City of San Mateo
Public Works Department
Environmental Services Division
SPILL EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLAN SPILL
CALCULATION AND ESTIMATION METHODS
Rectangle: Area = length (feet) x width (feet)
Circle:
Triangle:
Area = diameter (feet) x diameter (feet) x 0.785
Area = base (feet) x height (feet) x 0.5
Step 6
Step 7
Multiply the area (square feet) times the depth (in feet) to obtain the
volume in cubic feet.
Multiply the volume in cubic feet by 7.5 to convert it to gallons
Method 3: Duration and Flowrate
Calculating the volume of larger spills, where it is difficult or impossible to measure the area and
depth, requires a different approach. In this method, separate estimates are made of the
duration of the spill and the flowrate. The methods of estimating duration and flowrate are:
Duration
The duration is the elapsed time from the time the spill started to the time that the flow was
restored.
Start Time: The start time is sometimes difficult to establish. Here are some approaches:
1. Local residents can be used to establish start time. Inquire as to their observations.
Spills that occur in rights-of-way are usually observed and reported promptly. Spills that
occur out of the public view can go on longer. Sometimes observations like odors or sounds
(e.g. water running in a normally dry creek bed) can be used to estimate the start time.
2. Changes in flow on a downstream flowmeter can be used to establish the start time.
Typically the daily flow peaks are “cut off” or flattened by the loss of flow. This can be
identified by comparing hourly flow data during the spill event with flow data from prior days.
This method will likely only be effective with consistent weather.
3. Conditions at the spill site change over time and can be used to establish the start time.
Initially there will be limited deposits of toilet paper and other sewage solids. After a few
days to a week, the sewage solids form a light-colored residue. After a few weeks to a
month, the sewage solids turn dark. The quantity of toilet paper and other materials of
sewage origin increase over time. These observations can be used to estimate the start time
in the absence of other information. Taking photographs to document the observations can
be helpful if questions arise later in the process. This method is valid for spills that have
been occurring for a long time and may be used in conjunction with either of the above
methods.
4. It is important to remember that spills may not be continuous. Blockages are not usually
complete (some flow continues). In this case the spill would occur during the peak flow
periods (typically 10:00 to 12:00 and 13:00 to 16:00 each day). Spills that occur due to peak
flows in excess of capacity will occur only during, and for a short period after, heavy rainfall.
End Time: The end time is usually much easier to establish. Field crews on-site observe the
“blow down” that occurs when the blockage has been removed. The “blow down” can also be
observed in downstream flowmeters.
Flow Rate
The flowrate is the average flow that left the sewer system during the time of the spill.
There are three common ways to estimate the flowrate:
491 of 891

--- page 492 ---
City of San Mateo
Public Works Department
Environmental Services Division
SPILL EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLAN SPILL
CALCULATION AND ESTIMATION METHODS
1. The San Diego Manhole Flowrate Chart: This chart, included as at the end of this
appendix, shows sewage flowing from manhole covers at a variety of flowrates. The
observations of the field crew can be used to select the appropriate flowrate from the
chart. If possible, photographs are useful in documenting basis for the flowrate estimate.
2. Flowmeter: Changes in flows in downstream flowmeters can be used to estimate the
flowrate during the spill.
3. Counting Connections: Once the location of the spill is known, the number of upstream
connections can be determined from the sewer maps. Multiply the number of
connections by 200 to 250 gallons per day per connection or 8 to 10 gallons per hour per
connection.
For example: 22 upstream connections * 9 gallons per hour per connection
= 198 gallons per hour / 60 minutes per hour
= 3.3 gallons per minute
Spill Volume
Once duration and flowrate have been estimated, the volume of the spill is the product of
duration (hours or days) and the flowrate (gallons per hour or gallons per day).
For example: Spill start time = 11:00
Spill end time = 14:00
Spill duration = 3 hours
3.3 gallons per minute x 3 hours x 60 minutes per hour
= 594 gallons

SPILL SIGNAGE AND POSTING
PROCEDURES
POSTING OF CONTAMINATED WATERS IN CONNECTION WITH SPILL
This requirement applies to sanitary sewer overflows where 10,000 gallons or more reached
a waterway or are expected to reach public creeks, drainage channels, and/or waters that run
adjacent to or through City Parks and are accessible to the public, and/or the Marina Lagoon.
1. 
Follow spill emergency response procedures for spill assessment, and containment.
2. 
If sewage entered surface waters and was not fully recovered, refer to Sewage
Watershed Map (page 22) and determine area of impact. Note name of watershed,
names of waterways and beaches (if applicable) on spill field form.
3. 
Spill Response Crew shall assign a crew to post contaminated water advisory signs at all
pre-determined locations downstream of spill location in the identified watershed(s).
Crew shall follow the Preferred Specific Locations identified on pages 23 through 25.
Signs shall be posted in conspicuous locations to avert potential human water contact.
Park locations where waters are completely fenced and inaccessible to the public will not
receive signage.
4. 
If impacted watershed drains in to the Marina Lagoon, the large beach closure signs shall
be utilized at Aquatic Park (1097 Seal Court) and Lakeshore Park (1500 Marina Court).
5. 
Leadworker, PW Supervisor or Sr. Project Manager shall inform Director of Parks and
Recreation (522-7404) and Parks Manager (522-7423) of spill address/location, date,
time and volume of spill, and the name of the impacted Sewage Spill Watershed.
6. 
Warning signs should remain posted until the risk of contamination has subsided to
acceptable background levels. For beaches, this will be determined by the San Mateo
County Department of Environmental Health. For all other waterways, re-sampling must
be performed by crews.
For Questions or further assistance,
Contact County Recreation Water Quality Program
Phone 363-4798

SPILL SIGNAGE AND POSTING PROCEDURES
PARK SIGNAGE POSTING LOCATIONS
Follow these noticing procedures for SSOs where more than 10,000 gallons reached (or
are likely to reach) waterways that run adjacent to or through City Parks and are
accessible to the public.
1. Identify the location of the spill on the Sewage Spill Watersheds, Spill Notifications
Boundaries Map. Note the Name of the watershed. ALSO - Note whether the Watershed is
part of a drainage system that reaches the Marina Lagoon (these watersheds are encircled
in red on the map).
2. Find the name of the Watershed below and proceed to post “Danger, Raw Sewage” signage
at the locations specified.
3. Remember to also post Beach Closure signs at Lakeshore Park, Aquatic Park,
Bayside/Joinville and Tidelands Park.
Edgewood Creek Spill Watershed
There is no public access to any waterways in this watershed. PW Supervisor to contact and coordinate
actions with the City of Burlingame.
Poplar/Bayfront Spill Watershed
Ryder Park
Post one sign downstream of water feature, adjacent to the wood deck.
San Mateo Creek Spill Watershed
Gateway Park
rd
th
Post signs at the waters edge at the 3 Avenue and 4 Avenue entrances to the Park. Attach a third sign
to an A-Frame and place it at the top of the stairs in the middle of the park which lead down to the creek.
Proceed to posting Arroyo Park (page 19).

Laurelwood Park (3471 Glendora Dr.)

(creek on the right drains from Belmont). Post one sign near the culvert on the left side. Continue down
the path and post another sign on the left side at the path leading down to the creek. Further down there
is a fork in the path; stay to the left and post again at the creek access. Then, proceed to Post at Marina
Lagoon locations (page 20).

two locations along the walking path.

Post a sign at the boat launch.

WATER QUALITY MONITORING PROGRAM
WATER QUALITY MONITORING PROGRAM
INTRODUCTION
This Water Quality Monitoring Program supplements the description of City response activities
and standard operating procedures to be utilized in the SERP, in the event a sanitary sewer
spill exceeds 50,000 gallons and enters surface waters. This program is reviewed periodically
and may be updated as necessary.
SAFETY
Be aware of safety issues and do not subject personnel to unsafe conditions in order to
comply with this Water Quality Monitoring Plan. Sampling will not be conducted if there are any
concerns regarding field crew safety. These concerns may include heavy rain events, which
compromise access points through flooding and swift currents. Thunderstorms will also be
avoided when lightning is occurring. Employ the buddy system as required to maximize
employee safety when sample collection is required.
ESTIMATION OF SPILL TRAVEL TIME
The following methods are recommended to estimate spill travel time and direction:
• Method 1: Use a velocity probe (such as a Global Water FP111-S Flow Probe). To
determine the rate of flow in the surface water or
• Method 2: Visual ft/sec measurement. This may be done by observing or dropping floatable
debris in the surface water and timing how long it takes to travel over a measured distance
(e.g., 100 feet). Include sections in the surface water where there are bends, bottlenecks, or
other characteristics that may slow down the flow. If the first measurement is uncertain, this
estimate may be performed three to five times, and the values averaged to determine an
estimated travel time.
Either method will provide a means to estimate the distance traveled and identify where the
spill may be headed within the waterway.
504 of 891

--- page 505 ---
City of San Mateo
Public Works Department
Environmental Services Division
SPILL EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLAN
WATER QUALITY MONITORING PROGRAM
WATER QUALITY SAMPLING PROCEDURES
•
In the event a spill reaches a surface water or (flowing) drainage channel tributary and
the volume is estimated as greater than 50,000 gallons, samples are required within 48
hours of first knowing of the release. The purpose of water quality sampling is to
determine the nature and extent of the impact of the spill. Note: Water quality sampling
should not be given precedence over stopping the spill or protection of public health.
•
When sampling a spill, collect a minimum of three samples as conditions allow. One
sample shall be located 100 feet upstream of the point where sewage entered the
waterway. The second sample shall be taken at the discharge location. A third sample
shall be taken 100 feet downstream of the point where sewage entered the waterway.
When possible, collect samples from the middle of the waterway where the water is
actively flowing.
•
•
Sample for Total Coliform, E. coli, enterococcus, and Ammonia as a minimum. Conduct
additional sampling for pH if practical.
Additional follow-up samples may be required by the County Health Department to
confirm the surface water quality has returned to baseline conditions. The timing and
location of samples shall be determined through discussions with the County Health
Department. Follow-up samples may be used to determine if posting of warning signs
should be discontinued (if signs were posted).
•
Collaboration with the County Health Department should continue until closure is
obtained.
WATER QUALITY SAMPLING EQUIPMENT
The following list describes equipment that should be stocked and readily available for each
water quality sampling event.
•
•
•
•
Personnel protective equipment including latex/nitrile gloves and eye protection
3 – 120 mL sterile plastic containers for Bacteria sample collection.
3 – 250 mL plastic containers preserved with H SO for Ammonia analysis.

4
3 – 250 mL plastic containers (for collecting the sample and transferring to the preserved
container for ammonia. Also can be used for checking pH, if desired)
•
•
•
1 – Sample Collection Tool (extension pole with attachment to hold sample bottle)

Chain of Custody forms for WWTP Lab, Caltest Labs, and County Health Lab,
Ensure that there are adequate quantities of sample containers-kits if there are more
than three sample locations.

--- page 506 ---
City of San Mateo

SPILL EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLAN
WATER QUALITY MONITORING PROGRAM
SAMPLE COLLECTION PROCEDURE
Note: Collect the UPSTREAM sample first.
I.
Bacteria Sample Collection – Dip sample bottle into water, preferably mid-stream
1. Using the 120-mL sterile plastic sample bottles, one sample must be filled at a point
approximately 100 feet upstream of where the spill entered the receiving water, a
second bottle filled at a point 100 feet downstream of where the spill entered the
receiving water, and the third sample collected at the point where the spill entered
the receiving water. Total coliform, E. coli and enterococcus can be analyzed at the
lab from each sample container.
2. Immediately before sampling, don latex/nitrile gloves. Using a pen (that doesn’t
smear in water) record on the bottle label (1) the sample ID*, (2) date, (3) time of
sample collection, and (4) the sampler’s initials.
*Sample ID = Manhole#–UP (upstream) or Manhole#-DWN(downstream)
Example for upstream Sample ID:
431:06-UP
Example for downstream Sample ID: 431:06-DWN
Example of discharge point Sample ID: 431:06-PT
3. Carefully open the sample bottle without touching the inside of the lid or bottle.
4. Face upstream (or upgradient) using the extension pole or by hand, submerge the
sample container and fill it under water, if possible, without collecting surface or
bottom debris. Pour off excess sample volume so that the container is filled to the
100-mL fill line. Secure the lid and dry the outside of bottle with a paper towel.
5. Immediately place the filled & labeled sample container inside the cooler with ice or
cold packs.
6. Repeat Steps 1 through 5 for each bacteria sample location. Proceed to Section III
(after collecting ammonia samples if necessary).
II.
Ammonia Sample Collection – Do NOT Dip Sample Bottle into Water
Use Secondary Container
1. One 250-mLplastic ammonia sample bottle (containing sulfuric acid as preservative)
must be filled at a point 100 feet upstream of where the spill entered the receiving
water, one sample collected at a point 100 feet downstream of where the spill
entered the receiving water, and a sample where the spill entered the receiving
water. USE THE UNPRESERVED SAMPLE CONTAINER (does not contain
sulfuric acid) TO COLLECT THE SAMPLE AND TRANSFER THE CONTENTS TO
THE PRESERVED SAMPLE CONTAINER. Sampling personnel must wear
gloves and safety glasses while collecting samples for ammonia because of
potential contact with sulfuric acid, which is highly corrosive.
2. Use a pen that does not smear when wet to record on the bottle label (1) the
sample ID*, (2) date and (3) time of sample collection, and (4) the sampler’s initials.
506 of 891

--- page 507 ---
City of San Mateo
Public Works Department
Environmental Services Division
SPILL EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLAN
WATER QUALITY MONITORING PROGRAM
*Sample ID = 
Manhole#–UP (upstream) or Manhole#-DWN(downstream)
Example for upstream Sample ID: 
431:06-UP
Example for downstream Sample ID: 431:06-DWN
Example of discharge point Sample ID: 431:06-PT
3. Remove the lid from the clean / empty / unused 250-mL plastic secondary container
(does not contain sulfuric acid).
4. Face upstream (or upgradient) using the extension pole or by hand, submerge the
secondary container and fill it under water, if possible, without collecting surface or
bottom debris.
5. Carefully open the ammonia sample bottle containing sulfuric acid.
6. Slowly transfer sample from the secondary container into the 250-mL ammonia
sample bottle preserved with sulfuric acid. Secure the lid so that acidified sample
does not leak out of the bottle and dry the outside of bottle with a paper towel.
7. Immediately place the filled & labeled sample container inside the cooler with ice or
cold packs.
8. Repeat Steps 1 through 7 for each ammonia sample location.
III.
Chain-of-Custody & Sample Delivery
1. Depending on which lab the bacteria samples will be delivered to, complete the
appropriate Chain-of-Custody (COC) form for the bacteria samples (either the
WWTP Lab or County Health Lab); include the Sample IDs, Date & Time of Sample
Collection and Sampler’s Name & Signature. Note the approximate upstream and
downstream distance in the “Notes” field on the COC if the samples were collected
from a distance other than 100 feet.
The COC is a legal document and must be complete.
2. Specify on the COC the analyses requested for each sample. Each sample should
be analyzed for: Total coliform, E. coli, and Enterococcus. Each COC is pre-filled
for three samples; add additional lines if more samples were collected.
3. Complete the Caltest Lab COC form for all samples that will be analyzed for
ammonia. Include the Sample IDs, Date & Time of Sample Collection and
Sampler’s Name & Signature. Note the approximate upstream and downstream
distance in the “Notes” field on the COC if the samples were collected from a
distance other than 100 feet.
The COC is a legal document and must be complete.
4. Deliver bacteria samples to the WWTP Laboratory during normal business hours
(Mon – Sun 6:30 a.m. to 3 p.m.). If after business hours, deliver to the County
Health Lab.
5. Ammonia samples will be picked up by Caltest from the WWTP Lab. A sample
refrigerator labelled “Caltest Pickup” is in the sample receiving room of the WWTP
Lab. Place the labelled ammonia samples in the refrigerator. MAKE SURE THE
507 of 891

--- page 508 ---
City of San Mateo
Public Works Department
Environmental Services Division
SPILL EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLAN
WATER QUALITY MONITORING PROGRAM
SAMPLE LABELS AND COC ARE COMPLETE. Place the CalTest COC in the clip
on the refrigerator door. Call Caltest for a pickup (707-258-4000). The courier will
sign the COC when they pick up the samples and leave a copy of the signed COC
in the clip.
6. If unable to deliver the bacteria samples to the WWTP Lab, they can be delivered to
the San Mateo County Public Health Laboratory at:
225 W. 37th Ave, Room 113. Hours of operation are Mon – Fri 8am to 5pm.* Have
the Lab Analyst sign the COC form & make a copy.
7. Place the signed COC forms (or photocopies) in the spill file.
SAMPLING COLLECTION BEST PRACTICE
•
Collect all grab samples approximately 3’ - 6” below the surface (or if shallower, as close
as possible to this depth) to avoid sampling debris or scum from the surface.
•
Collect the sample in a safe manner in the middle of the flow, against the direction of
water flow.
•
•
•
Photo-document the spill locations.
Leave approximately one inch of head space in individual sample bottles. Do not overfill.
Once the lid is opened for the individual sample bottle, do not touch the inside surface of
the bottle or lid.
•
•
For the sample bottles that contain a preservative, take care to keep the preservation
material in the container.
Immediately place all sample bottles on ice.
SAMPLING TIME CONSTRAINTS
Bacteria samples have a 6-hour (chilled) regulatory holding time. Samples will not be
analyzed if the holding time has been exceeded. The WWTP Lab and County Lab need
about 30 minutes to set up the tests.
Ammonia samples have a 28-day regulatory holding time. Samples must be maintained at
< 6oC (on ice or refrigerated) from the time of collection until receipt by the analytical laboratory.
*If the WWTP Lab and County Public Health lab is Closed…
If the WWTP Lab or County Health Lab will be opening within 5 hours from the time the samples
were collected, keep samples in a refrigerator or in the cooler containing the cold packs/ice and
immediately deliver samples as soon as either Lab is open for business.
Otherwise, call the County Health Lab’s After Hours Contact List (starting with #1 –
Office/Pager/Cell) and arrange to meet a lab analyst. They have agreed to meet us at the lab to
receive samples during off-hours.
Spill Sampling Laboratory Contact Information is on the following page.

Sacramento, CA 95814
Subject:
Technical Report for Sanitary Sewer Spill of 50,000 Gallons or More
Event ID: XXXXXX
This submittal comprises the Sanitary Sewer Spill Technical Report (“Report”) that is required
by State Water Resources Control Board (“SWRCB”) Order No.WQ 2022-0103-DWQ
(“Order”). The Order requires each enrollee to submit a Spill Technical Report in the California
Integrated Water Quality System (“CIWQS”) online Sanitary Sewer System Database within 45
calendar days of the spill end date for any spill in which 50,000 gallons or greater is discharged
to surface waters.
This Report comprises the following sections:
1. Causes and Circumstances of the Spill
2. District’s Response to Spill
3. Water Quality Monitoring
4. Spill Impact Evaluation
1.0

ultimate destinations:

the discharge volume and, if applicable, the recovered spill volume:

EXHIBIT 7.10A
City of San Mateo, Regulatory Compliance
Required Best Management Practices for
Food Service Facilities
Grease Removal Devices and Maintenance / Records
 If you generate grease, you will need a grease removal device (GRD).
A GRD is either a grease trap or a grease interceptor.
 To stop pipes from plugging, keep your GRD in continuous operation and clean.
-
-
At a minimum, clean the device every six months; or when the combined thickness of the floating fats,
oil and grease, and solids layers exceeds 25% of the total depth of the device.
 Keep a record of GRD cleanings in a logbook or binder for the Environmental Compliance Inspector to review:
-
-
-
-
Document the date
Who performed the cleaning
How much waste was removed
Where the waste was deposited
 If you hire a contractor to clean the GRD:
-
Confirm the work was performed as agreed
-
Keep the receipts in a folder as proof of service
Restaurant Operating Practices
 Greasy, dirty, water from cleaning the exhaust FILTERS should be drained into the sink connected to the
grease trap or interceptor - or disposed of offsite by your contractor.
-
Water from cleaning the exhaust HOODS is too dirty and should be disposed of offsite by your contractor.
 Waste oil should be collected in a tallow bin and disposed of by a grease-waste hauling company.
 To save money on GRD cleaning costs, use dry clean-up methods such as dry wiping with rubber scrapers
and paper towels. These practices reduce the solids and grease load to the GRD:
Scrape off excess food from plates and cookware into the trash.
Wipe the excess grease with paper towels and dispose of the towels in the trash.
Wash plates, pots, pans and utensils.
 Keep the ingredients in the bowl, not on the floor! To prevent spills:
-
-
Cover large containers when transporting
Securely store liquid materials away from floor drains
 To prevent solids from clogging pipes, keep strainers or screens over floor drains.
Storm Drain Protection
 It’s against the law to drain any water into a storm drain from washing floor mats or other equipment. Storm
drains lead directly to local creeks and the bay, and are for rain water only. Instead, pour dirty water in the
mop sink or an area connected to a sanitary drain.
 Don’t throw mop water out the back door! It is illegal to dump mop water to a storm drain.
Put it into a sink or sanitary drain instead.
 Keep it clean! Cover your dumpsters and clean your trash enclosure, tallow bins, or barrels to prevent trash
and grease waste from getting washed into the storm drain.
 Don’t rinse spills into the storm drain. Use dry clean-up methods (absorbents/kitty litter for oil spills) or a
wet-dry vacuum to clean outdoor spills.
524 of 891

--- page 525 ---
City of San Mateo, Regulatory Compliance
Municipal Code Applicable to
Food Service Establishments
Chapter 7.38 Sanitary Sewer Use
7.38.160. Requirements for Food Service Facilities
(a) The owner of every commercial, industrial, or institutional food service facility, shall install or cause to be installed a
pretreatment system for grease removal which meets or exceeds minimum sizing requirements of the Uniform Plumbing
Code, by no later than January 1, 2001. The Director shall approve final sizing.
7.38.100 Pretreatment of Industrial Waste
(c) Food service facilities shall have a pretreatment system for grease removal designed to serve all drains
that may discharge grease into the sewer, including floor drains.
7.38.120 Specific Discharge Prohibitions
(h) Oil and grease shall not be discharged into the sewer system if average concentration of floatable oil and
grease exceeds 20 mg/L, defined as that which is subject to gravity separation at a temperature of 20 degrees
Celsius and at pH 4.5; nor shall the total oil and grease concentration exceed 200 mg/L.
7.38.170 Operation and Maintenance of Pretreatment Systems
(a) Pretreatment systems shall be inspected and maintained by the user as needed to ensure continuous
efficient operation.
(c) A record of all inspection and maintenance activity shall be kept by the user at the facility showing the
date of inspection or maintenance, the activity completed, and the disposition of the removed contents,
filters cartridges, or other components.
(d) Failure to operate or maintain the pretreatment system in a way so as to ensure optimum efficiency, or
failure to keep records of pretreatment system maintenance shall constitute a violation of this chapter.
7.38.210 Waste Discharge Permit
(a) The Director shall require each significant industrial user to obtain a waste discharge permit.
(b) The Director may require other users to obtain waste discharge permits as necessary to carry out the
purposes of this chapter.
7.38.390 Best Management Practices
(a) Where best management practices (BMP’s) have been established or adopted by the Director for an
activity, operation, or facility which may cause a prohibited discharge to the sanitary sewer or storm drain
systems, persons undertaking such activity or operation, or owning or operating such facility shall comply
with such requirements as may be specified by the Director in waste discharge permits, compliance
directives, printed material, or verbal notice. (Ord. 2006-1 § 1; Ord. 2003-7 § 1)
Chapter 7.39 Stormwater Management and Discharge Control
7.39.090 Discharge of Pollutants
(a) Non-stormwater discharges to the City storm drain system are prohibited.
7.39.110 Illicit Discharge
(a) It is prohibited to establish, use, maintain, or continue illicit drainage connections to the City storm drain
system, and to commence or continue any illicit discharges to the City storm drain system. This prohibition
applies to connections made in the past, regardless of whether made under a permit or whether permissible
under the law or practices applicable or prevailing at the time of the connection.
7.39.120 Reduction of Pollutants in Stormwater
Any person engaged in activities, which will or may result in pollutants entering the City storm drain system shall
undertake all practicable measures to reduce such pollutants.
