assumption is number of bedrooms plus one. 
It should be noted that rents vary significantly across housing type and are dependent on factors such as building class, 
size, size of units, and number of units.
Of the properties listed on CoStar, the largest share of rental housing consists of properties of 2-5 rental units (42 percent), 
followed by properties of up to 10 units (37 percent). There is no data on single-family homes. These smaller properties 
are largely older buildings, with the majority built before 1990. Properties of 50-200 units make up 5 percent of the total 
and are markedly newer. 
These characteristics are notable because rents vary significantly between buildings of different sizes and ages – rents for 
units in older buildings are, on average, lower than units in newer multi-family developments. Ownership type also varies; 
630 of 891

--- page 631 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
there are more individual owners among properties with fewer units, while larger numbers of units tend to be owned by 
corporate entities. This variation is important to consider when discussing the policies in this report, as different segments 
of the rental market will respond differently to their implementation. 
Evictions
Staff met with legal service providers serving San Mateo clients to obtain data on eviction cases and general trends in 
caseloads over the last five years. These organizations provide services prior to formal eviction proceedings and legal aid in 
unlawful detainer (UD) cases. All reported a significant increase in caseloads. For example, Project Sentinel, a non-profit 
that provides services in fair housing, dispute resolution, and support to tenants facing eviction in San Mateo County, 
found a 24% increase in their caseload, from 346 cases in 2018 to 428 cases in 2023. Legal Aid Society of San Mateo 
County reported that their caseload on UDs in 2019 went from 1,120 to 1,520 in 2023, a 35% increase. 
This trend can be attributed in part,  to the termination of COVID-19 pandemic-related rental assistance and eviction 
moratoria. At the same time, all organizations stated that AB 1482 (summarized in Table 3 below) has significantly 
curtailed “no-cause” evictions. The primary reason for most eviction cases is nonpayment of rent.
A study of court records of eviction filings in San Mateo County published in February 2025 by Mills Legal Clinic at Stanford 
Law School supports these observations. AB 1482 appeared to contribute to a significant drop in “no-cause” evictions, with 
the vast majority of evictions due to alleged nonpayment of rent. San Mateo (city), with 141 filings, is one of the four San 
Mateo County jurisdictions with more than 100 UDs in 2019 and 2023 combined, along with Daly City, East Palo Alto, and 
Redwood City. 
Even with comprehensive data on court filings, the extent of residential displacement due to “soft” or “functional” 
evictions – cases where displacement occurs, but the formal case never reaches the courts – is unknown. The Stanford Law 
School study also does not provide details such as the type of housing (single-family dwelling, small or large multi-family, 
older or newer stock), geographic concentrations of eviction filings within San Mateo, or the extent to which evictions 
disproportionately impact people of color or those of lower income. 
Outreach and Community Engagement
In parallel with this research, staff also conducted outreach and community engagement to consult with both tenants and 
property owners in fall and winter 2024 and 2025; and two community workshops were held through the Community 
Relations Commission on November 20, 2024 and January 15, 2025. A variety of public comments were received from 
community stakeholders on potential anti-displacement and tenant protection strategies and programs. A summary of the 
public comments received in both community workshops can be found in the meeting minutes from the Community 
Relations Commission in Attachment 1.
In December 2024, staff facilitated five focus group meetings with community stakeholders to gather more input on how 
housing issues and current policies impact tenants and property owners. Input received included the following: 
•
Support for policies that increase likelihood that renters can successfully relocate within or in close proximity to 
San Mateo; in particular, increasing all forms of relocation assistance to three months’ rent. 
•
Support for a rental registry to address a lack of San Mateo-specific housing data that could facilitate policymaking 
in this area. 
•
Opposition to a rental registry due to concerns about privacy and administrative burden on property owners and 
managers to meet extensive reporting requirements.
•
Support for a policy that would limit no-fault evictions during the school year.
•
Emphasis on differences in the ability of larger versus smaller property owners and managers to keep apprised of, 
and comply with, complex state and local laws.
•
Emphasis on the effect of increased regulation on property owners who tend to own smaller, older, and relatively 
more affordable housing, and who are facing steep increases in costs (insurance, utilities, etc.).
631 of 891

--- page 632 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
•
Overarching concern about ability of renters to remain in their San Mateo community.
•
Support for education and outreach on current tenant protections and available resources and emphasis on 
providing education and outreach in multiple languages.
•
Emphasis on the limited supply of rental units impacting rent prices.
Current Protections in State and Local Law
There are many state laws that address various aspects of the rental market and that set the existing policy framework for 
tenants and landlords. Some relevant policies include:

Policy Alternatives for City Council Consideration
The table in Attachment 2 describes various options for the City Council to consider to implement the relevant Housing 
Element policies and to respond to the findings described in this report. The table identifies each Housing Element policy, 
briefly describes the problem it is intended to address, discusses potential benefits and costs, and lists jurisdictions with 
some version of the policy. Policy alternatives are discussed below, with additional details included in Attachment 2. 
Relocation Assistance
AB 1482 introduced the requirement of relocation assistance for no-fault, just-cause evictions, which include owner move-
in, substantial renovation or demolition, a requirement to vacate the unit related to habitability, or withdrawal of the unit 
632 of 891

--- page 633 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
from the rental market. This provision only applies to tenants in covered units who have lived in the unit for one year or 
more. The purpose of expanding relocation assistance to tenants who have been in the unit for less than one year is to  
provide the same level of protection afforded by AB 1482 to all tenants evicted for these reasons. Policy alternatives to 
consider include the amount of relocation assistance provided and the qualifying length of tenure (e.g., no minimum, 
minimum 3, 6, 9 or 11months).
Right to Return
While AB 1482 addresses relocation assistance, there is no right to return provision for tenants evicted for the reasons 
stated above. SB 567 provides for the right to return only in cases of substantial renovation where the work is not 
completed (or never commences). While some of the City’s policies include a right to return, they do not address most no-
fault evictions described in AB 1482, and do not apply to demolitions. The purpose of a right to return policy is to provide 
tenants an opportunity to return to their unit in all of these cases.  
Strengthened Enforcement Penalty Structure for Unsafe or Substandard Units
As noted above, the City’s existing ordinance on relocation assistance for tenants in unsafe or substandard units that are 
temporarily or permanently displaced due to City Code Enforcement activities is from 2019. Since adoption, as the 
ordinance has been utilized, there have been some functional issues and policy limitations that have been identified. 
Housing Element Policy H.3.4(c) was included to improve the intended function of the ordinance, including the provision of 
relocation assistance and the appeals process. 
Specifically, for tenants whose temporary displacement becomes permanent, the ordinance does not offer additional, or 
cumulative assistance beyond three times the US Department of Housing and Urban Development Fair Market Rent (FMR). 
This is an important gap to address because of the high cost of living in San Mateo, where three times FMR may be 
insufficient even in cases of short-term displacement. Based on City implementation of the ordinance, allowing for greater 
relocation benefits if a temporary relocation becomes permanent would avoid significant finance impacts to tenants who 
are no longer able to return to their unit.  With regards to the processing of an appeal related to a relocation finding,  
Council provided earlier direction to explore alternatives, including the use of a hearing officer. 
Rent Registry 
The purpose of a rent registry is to collect data specific on the rental housing stock in a jurisdiction. The level of specificity 
that a rent registry provides exceeds that of any other data sources, including commercial real estate databases or local 
sources such as business license records. For example, a rent registry can provide accurate information on the number of 
rental units, rents charged, and vacancy rates, track unit turnover by category, and track evictions. 
To determine what data is currently available, staff reviewed the City’s internal business license tax database and CoStar; 
some of these results are discussed elsewhere in this report. Both sources help to determine the approximate number of 
rental properties citywide, but the data is aggregated, provided as averages, and incomplete (no data on single-family 
homes). While valuable sources of information, they require several assumptions and are best used to identify broad 
trends in the local rental market. 
Several Bay Area jurisdictions have proposed or instituted rent registries. These include the following:
•
East Palo Alto: The City of East Palo Alto selected a firm via an RFP process in 2024 and expects to implement a rent 
registry by mid-2025. The city is leveraging its existing Rent Stabilization and Just Cause for Eviction program, which 
regulates approximately 2,500 of the city’s rental units and includes full-time staff.
•
Foster City: (pending) A Rental Registry was presented to the Foster City City Council in October 2024 to apply only to 
the city’s Below Market Rate (BMR) units. Through this rental registry, the city intends to gather additional data to 
improve the auditing and monitoring process and ensure that tenants and BMR property owners are compliant with 
the regulations. This is a pilot program.
633 of 891

--- page 634 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
•
Half Moon Bay: The City of Half Moon Bay adopted a rent registry ordinance in February 2024 and began registrations 
in June 2024. This was followed in fall 2024 by implementation of a new rent stabilization program. The stated purpose 
of the rent registry was to identify how many rental units exist in Half Moon Bay and to track year-over-year changes, 
including displacement of residents. The rent registry website can be viewed here.  
•
Concord: The City of Concord has an established Rent Registry program for the past four years that collects data on 
rent amount, property address, size of unit, number of bedrooms and bathrooms, lease terms, and parking spaces. All 
rental units are required to be registered, including single-family homes. The city releases an annual report each year 
on its Rental Registry Webpage. 
•
Palo Alto: In October 2024, the City of Palo Alto launched a Rental Registry Program for rental properties with three or 
more units. The stated purpose of this registry is to better understand the rental landscape in Palo Alto and help 
inform future Council decision-making (see link).  
While rent registries provide a reliable source of rental information, jurisdictions must weigh this benefit against the costs 
of establishing and maintaining the registry. Cities must invest in an RFP process to identify a firm that can create the rent 
registry and budget for significant costs associated with registry set-up and maintenance, annual consultant and software 
fees, and city staff time. Some cost recovery is possible with a citation system, but this also requires staff time. Below are 
some examples of rent registry budgets and estimates:
•
East Palo Alto budgeted $150,000 for program set-up, with an additional $150,000 on an annual basis.
•
Half Moon Bay requires $30,000 annually to maintain its rent registry contract, plus one half of a permanent staff 
position and a full-time temporary position. 
•
Concord’s program has an average budget of $81,000 per year for the rent registry software. Their program runs 
on a cost-recovery model using Rent Registry fees to cover the software and personnel. 
•
Through General Funds, Palo Alto budgeted $443,256 over four years to cover software services, one full-time 
staff position, and other additional services. Palo Alto has the intention to switch to a partial cost-recovery model 
after the first year.
Emergency Rental Assistance
Receiving an eviction notice due to non-payment of rent can lead to a household becoming homeless. For this reason, 
several jurisdictions in San Mateo County have implemented their own emergency rental assistance program to help 
renters avoid missing a month’s rent, utility payments, or security deposits. The household limit for this assistance ranges 
from $4,000-5,000. Jurisdictions such as South San Francisco, Daly City, Foster City, Half Moon Bay, Redwood City, and 
Brisbane use either General Funds or Housing Successor Funds to make this form of assistance available. The average 
funding for this is $84,000 annually. The jurisdiction has the option to independently administer the program, or contract 
with a nonprofit service agency. Payments are issued directly to the property owner.
City Council Direction
Based on the information provided in this agenda report and the policies in the City’s Housing Element, staff is seeking 
Council direction on the following questions to guide how the City proceeds with implementation.
1.
Given the commitment in Policy H.3.4(a) to extending relocation assistance for no-fault evictions for those with 
tenure less than one-year, which policy alternative would the City Council like to pursue, i.e., qualifying length of 
tenure and amount of compensation?
2.
Would the City Council like to pursue a policy related to right-to-return as described in Policy H.3.4(b), and if so, 
which alternative should be pursued? 
3.
Given the commitment in Policy H.3.4(c) to strengthening the enforcement penalty structure of the City’s existing 
ordinance on relocation assistance for tenants displaced by code enforcement activities, what changes would the 
City Council like to pursue in an amended ordinance? 
634 of 891

--- page 635 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
4.
Would the City Council like to pursue a rent registry, a different model for data tracking, or rely on existing data 
sources to achieve the goals of Policy H.3.6? 
5.
Are there other policy alternatives the City Council would like to pursue based on the findings in this report? 
6.
Does the City Council wish to prioritize any of these items as staff continues with implementation?
Next Steps
Based on the direction provided by the City Council, staff will begin drafting the necessary code amendments, policy 
language or program proposal(s), as necessary, and return to the City Council for further approval of each item. Due to the 
differing nature of the various policy alternatives, depending on the feedback City Council provides, these items may be 
addressed in groupings, or as standalone items. Staff is working towards implementing all actions related to Policies H 3.4, 
H 3.6, and H 5.4.1 by the end of 2025.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The impact on the General Fund and other housing funds will depend on the direction the City Council provides in this 
study session. Code amendments to reflect updates to an existing ordinance or to introduce a new ordinance will have 
minimal impact, but there may be costs associated with implementation. As noted above, a rent registry – or any data 
collection mechanism – will have associated upfront and ongoing costs. Finally, a rental assistance program could be 
supported out of a restricted Housing fund, such as the Housing Successor Fund (F255), with no impact to the General 
Fund.  Staff will address the budgetary impact of each item when returning to the City Council for further direction or 
approval.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This study session is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical 
change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)

All meeting noticing requirements were met.

2. Community Needs Workshop for 2025-26 Community Development Block Grant Annual Action Plan 
The Community Relations Commission determined community priorities in preparation of the 2025-26 Annual 
Action Plan for the Community Development Block Grant Program. 
 
Public Comment on this item:  
Public comment was received from 9 members of the public and broadly included the following recommendations 
for CDBG funding priorities:  
• Continue to fund legal services organizations to assist the anticipated growing number of evictions.  
• Fund programs to stop the “gap” when the tenant cannot pay a month’s rent to avoid evictions for 
nonpayment, rather than “penalizing” landlords 
•  Make available programs to assist tenants to apply for housing programs such as Section 8. 
• Create more housing options for low-income housing for seniors and individuals with mental or physical 
disabilities.  
• Continue funding for repairs/maintenance on sidewalks to mitigate damage done by trees to sidewalks 
and trip hazards. 
•  Continue to prioritize affordable housing under CDBG.  
 
3. Community Workshop on Anti-Displacement and Tenant Protection Policies 
Staff provided a presentation on the current tenant protection policies and the City’s strategies to address 
displacement, a goal in the Housing Element.  
 
Public Comment on this item:  
Public comment was received from 18 members of the public on this item. Comments included the following broad 
areas: 
• Increasing relocation benefits to three months for all evictions.  
• Lowering the limit of rent increases to 3-5% to help prevent displacement, especially of teachers, first 
responders, and essential workers. 
• Removing the “expiration date” on tenant protection laws. 
• Consider that the cost of living in San Mateo is very high, and tenants are living in overcrowded 
conditions.  
• Consider more protections against unsafe living conditions - individuals living in units with mold, 
infestations, or other repair needs.

From:

Sent: Monday, March 31, 2025 11:40 AM

To: Rachel Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>

Cc: Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; Christina Horrisberger <chorrisberger@cityofsanmateo.org>
Subject: Thoughts related to 4/21 study session

Hi Rachel,

As we approach the date of the study session devoted to AB 1482 expansion, I wanted to share a few
more thoughts.

1) Right to return. When we met with one of the council members recently, s/he asserted a strong
belief that SB 567 had created the right to return after a substantial remodel. Of course this is
not true. SB 567 created the right to return if and only if the owner failed to initiate or complete
the planned remodel, in which case the tenant could return at the previous rent. It did not
create a right to return after the remodel was complete. No doubt you know this, but s/he was
so emphatic that I wanted to touch on it.

2) Data registry. With regard to the rental registry, the housing element states a preference for
pursuing this as part of a regional process, such as one that might be undertaken by 21
Elements. I emailed Josh Abrams of Planning Collaborative and learned that the creation of
rental registries is not part of the 21 Elements’ upcoming work plan for 2025/2026 or beyond.
To tie the rental registry to a nonexistent regional process would seem the equivalent of
putting it in a drawer to die.

3) Relocation assistance.

e When members of the “Our Home San Mateo” coalition met with you, we expressed
our objection to means testing as part of increasing relocation benefits to three months.
The primary reason for our objection is that it provides an incentive for landlords to
discriminate against the very people who are most in need of help. I wanted to take the
opportunity to underscore this since it is such a terrible backfire. However, regardless of
their income brackets, tenants are forced by an eviction to shell out much more than one
month. To shift the burden to the tenants seems especially unfair in light of the fact that
these are discretionary evictions usually done when the landlord sees an opportunity
for economic gain.

642 of 891


--- page 643 ---
2

We also argued for the increase to three months on the basis of internal consistency
within city policies. Currently, the city’s demolition and condo conversion ordinances
require three months of relocation assistance, whereas the requirement for Ellis Act,
substantial remodel and owner move-in evictions is only one month. From a public
policy perspective, this doesn’t make sense. When this same council member expressed
skepticism about the proposed increase to three months, we then asked whether the
condo conversion and demolition provisions should be reduced to one month. Of
course that is not what we want, but it underscores the disjunction that currently exists
among various city policies and the need to bring them into line.
Thank you for listening and for taking these thoughts into account. 
Best regards, 
Karyl Eldridge 
Vice Chair of One San Mateo

party to a real estate contract via written or verbal communication.

From: 
Sent: Thursday, April 3, 2025 2:05 PM 
To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; akohjikian@cityofsanmateo.org; City Attorney's Office 
<CityAttorneysOffice@cityofsanmateo.org>; Rachel Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Subject: Opposing Proposed Housing Element Policies – A Plea for Fairness 
Dear Honorable Members of the San Mateo City Council, 
I’m writing as a longtime resident and small property owner in San Mateo to respectfully oppose the proposed 
expansion of tenant protection policies being presented at the upcoming study session on April 21 (I will be 
out of the country then, so am writing instead). 
As a retired individual, the rental income from my property is not just helpful — it is essential to my livelihood. 
Like many small landlords in our community, I am not a large corporation with deep pockets. I rely on this 
income to make ends meet, and I do my best to treat my tenants fairly and with respect. 
It is disheartening to see policies continually shift in ways that assume landlords are the problem. Proposals like 
requiring relocation payments for tenants who have been in a unit for less than a year, or adding layers of 
bureaucracy like a rental registry, place significant burdens on small property owners — especially those of us 
who are retired and on fixed incomes. 
I understand the importance of protecting tenants from displacement, but why must that responsibility fall solely 
on the shoulders of housing providers? The ability to provide homes for our own children, or to make necessary 
improvements to aging properties, should not come with financial penalties. When the laws become so heavily 
weighted in one direction, it discourages people like me from continuing to offer housing at all. 
We are not the enemy. Most of us are responsible, compassionate landlords who value the tenants we serve. We 
want to be part of the solution, not treated as part of the problem. 
Please consider the unintended consequences of these proposals — especially for seniors, retirees, and families 
trying to build a future in San Mateo. I urge you to oppose these policy expansions and take a more balanced 
approach that respects both tenants and housing providers alike. 
Thank you for your time and consideration. 
Sincerely, 
Cindee Graham 
Retired Millbrae Resident & San Mateo Housing Provider

--- page 645 ---
1
 
From: 
Sent: Thursday, April 3, 2025 1:37 PM 
To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Subject: Opposition to Proposed Tenant Protection Measures 
I am writing to express concerns regarding the proposed tenant protection measures, specifically the 
expansion of tenant protections, creation of a rental registry, and recommendations for policy changes 
on eviction and remodel-related tenant displacement. While the intent behind these measures is to 
ensure fairness and security for renters, there are several reasons why these proposals may not be 
beneficial in practice, and could have unintended consequences. 
1. Expansion of Tenant Protections and Relocation Payments:
Extending AB 1482 provisions to require tenant relocation payments for no-fault evictions for
tenants with less than one year of tenure raises significant concerns. While the idea is to provide
more protection for tenants, this provision may discourage landlords from offering rental
agreements to new tenants. Landlords could be hesitant to rent to new tenants due to the
increased financial burden and potential risk associated with having to pay relocation expenses if
a no-fault eviction is necessary. This could lead to fewer available rental units and a decrease in
housing supply, ultimately making housing even less affordable and accessible in the city.
Additionally, the financial impact of paying relocation fees for tenants who have only been in
place for a short time could disproportionately affect smaller landlords, who may struggle to bear
these costs.
2. Creation of a Rental Registry:
The creation of a rental registry to monitor rents, evictions, and tenant displacement citywide,
while seemingly beneficial for transparency, would likely impose significant costs on both the city
and property owners. The proposed system would require substantial taxpayer funds to support
staffing, the creation of new bureaucratic infrastructure, and the use of consultants. These
resources could be better utilized in addressing more immediate issues such as increasing
housing development or providing direct financial assistance to vulnerable populations.
645 of 891

--- page 646 ---
2
Furthermore, the increased administrative burden on landlords—particularly small property 
owners—could lead to unnecessary complexity and compliance costs, which may be passed on 
to tenants in the form of higher rents. 
3. Recommendations Regarding Substantial Remodel Exemptions and Right to Return: 
Requiring landlords to provide documentation when using the substantial remodel exemption and 
instituting a "Right to Return" policy for tenants displaced due to demolition or remodeling may 
create additional legal and logistical challenges. Landlords would face increased paperwork and 
bureaucracy, which may lead to delays and more complex processes for property improvements 
that are often necessary for maintaining the condition and safety of buildings. In some cases, 
these additional requirements could disincentivize investment in necessary upgrades and repairs, 
further exacerbating housing quality concerns. Additionally, implementing a Right to Return policy 
could limit landlords' ability to make timely decisions about their properties and negatively impact 
the efficiency of housing markets, leading to potential underutilization of spaces or even reduced 
housing stock if developers are discouraged from undertaking substantial remodels or new 
constructions due to regulatory uncertainty. 
4. Strengthened Enforcement and Penalty Structure for Landlords: 
While it is essential to ensure landlords follow the law, further strengthening enforcement penalty 
structures could disproportionately impact smaller landlords who may not have the resources to 
navigate complex regulations. The potential for increased penalties could create a chilling effect, 
where landlords become overly cautious and, as a result, reduce the number of rental units 
available or increase rents to cover potential penalties. Additionally, this might incentivize some 
landlords to exit the rental market altogether, which would reduce overall rental housing 
availability, further exacerbating affordability challenges. 
In conclusion, while the proposed measures aim to address important issues related to tenant 
protections, they could inadvertently reduce the availability of rental housing, raise costs for both 
tenants and landlords, and create significant administrative and regulatory burdens. Instead of imposing 
additional restrictions and costs, we encourage the city to explore other solutions that foster 
collaboration between tenants, landlords, and the city to promote affordable housing, improve housing 
quality, and ensure fair treatment for all parties involved. 
Thank you for considering these perspectives. 
 
Karen Vega

Subject: Study of Displacement Protections for Tenants

I’d like to speak at the study session relating to displacement protections for tenants. I’m a property
manager and an attorney practicing landlord/tenant law.

Please help me by giving me the facts on the following:

How many tenants in the city have lived there one year or less?

How many rental units are demolished or substantially remodeled each year?

How many rental units are there in the city?

How many units were there 1,2, 3 and 5 years ago?

How many housing providers are there in the city?

How many were there 1,2,3 and 5 years ago?

How many tenants in the City are evicted each year?

How many evictions in San Mateo County are evicted due to demolition or substantial
remodeling?

9. How much will it cost the city each year to staff a displacement protection department?

What is the Tenant ProtecƟon Act in San Mateo County? 
 
The Basics: 
 
New laws provide an Annual Rent Cap (5% + Consumer Price Index) and Just Cause ProtecƟons for residenƟal 
tenancies Rent Cap will take eﬀect on 1/1/20 with a rent roll back to March 15, 2019 Just Cause ProtecƟon 
requires at least 12 months of tenancy These new laws do not apply to all housing. 
 
According to a new report from Stanford Law School’s Community Law Clinic, evicƟon cases in San Mateo 
County – where rents are among the highest in the naƟon – have climbed past pre-pandemic levels. In 2023, a 
total of 1,510 evicƟon cases were ﬁled – a 35% increase from 2019. And early data from 2024 suggest the 
trend is conƟnuing. The numbers echo naƟonal paƩerns, the report says. 
 
“EvicƟons in San Mateo County, 2019-2023,” conducted on behalf of the clinic’s clients Community Legal 
Services in East Palo Alto (CLSEPA) and the Legal Aid Society of San Mateo County (Legal Aid SMC), analyzed 
evicƟon cases from the last full year before the pandemic through the ﬁrst full year aŌer the expiraƟon of 
COVID-era evicƟon moratoria. It oﬀers a rare and in-depth look at evicƟon paƩerns in the county where the 
law school’s Community Law Clinic represents low-income tenants. The top line ﬁnding: There has been an 
upƟck in evicƟons in San Mateo County, with the vast majority based on nonpayment of rent. 
 
The ﬁndings reinforce the need for increased rental assistance, according to Juliet Brodie, director of the 
Community Law Clinic. “We call it a housing crisis, but really it’s a rent crisis,” she says. “People aren’t being 
evicted because they refuse to pay rent. They’re being evicted because they can’t aﬀord to. The gap between 
wages and rents just keeps geƫng bigger.”

Subject: Oppose new rent control measures in San Mateo

Dear respected city officials,

| am writing to express my opposition to the city of San Mateo adding new rent control
restrictions.

We are small, private, mom and pop property owners. We purchased two small
apartment buildings as an investment toward our retirement and have 10 total

units. These properties have taken a significant amount of time to generate income, 5-7
years, due to the high purchase costs and large mortgages. In addition to expected costs
associated with loan interest, repairs, updates, vacancies, recuperation from Covid, and
generally high cost of living in San Mateo, we are right now experiencing new skyrocketing
costs associated with water, energy, new San Mateo business taxes, balcony inspections,
and insurance premiums.

Market rents are not rising faster than our costs! Enough is enough.

Our buildings are already subject to California AB1482, which imposes a rent cap and
protects tenants rights, including relocation costs and eviction protections.

Please do not enact additional constraints on housing providers. Small property owners
cannot sustain more costs that will further exasperate our ability to provide quality
housing. Higher burdens on housing providers result in less resources to maintain our
buildings which, over time, degrades our neighborhoods.

As a San Mateo resident, | applaud efforts toward building more housing in the city. More
housing is critical to solving the housing crisis.

Alicia Woodfall-Jones

--- page 653 ---
From:

Sent: Tuesday, April 8, 2025 3:16 PM

To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; City
Attorney's Office <CityAttorneysOffice@cityofsanmateo.org>; Rachel Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>

Subject: April 21 Study Session - Support for Strong Tenant Protections

Good afternoon,

My name is Amanda Chang and | am a staff member of Urban Habitat. | was born and raised in San
Mateo County and am a voter in District 1. | am writing to strongly encourage the city council to adopt the
policies recommended by the Our Home San Mateo campaign.These policies include: 1) Just cause for
eviction from Day One, 2) Prohibition of no-fault evictions during the school year, 3) Right to return after a
substantial remodel, 4) Increased relocation assistance, and 5) Creation of a rental data registry.

The above policies are concrete ways the council can act to protect families from the trauma of evictions
and increase the stability of their lives. Furthermore, passing these policies will bring San Mateo closer to
meeting its regional climate and housing goals as well as help unlock millions of dollars in regional funds
for the city through the Transit-Oriented Communities Policy.

In 2022, the Metropolitan Transportation Commission (MTC) adopted the Transit-Oriented Communities
(TOC) Policy to ensure neighborhoods around transit stations and along transit corridors are places that
not only support transit ridership, but are places where Bay Area residents of all abilities, income levels,
and racial and ethnic backgrounds can live, work, and access services, such as education, childcare,
and healthcare.

San Mateo is a TOC Area, which means it is eligible for One Bay Area Grant (OBAG) program
transportation funding. The next round of funding is slated to begin in 2026 and will make available
hundreds of millions of dollars that the city relies on to maintain its public transit, roads, highways,
sidewalks, and bike lanes. In pursuit of compliance with this policy, San Mateo has already received over
$600,000 in funds from MTC to implement zoning changes and housing production policy development.
While San Mateo is already on its way to TOC compliance for zoning and housing production, TOC areas
are also required to adopt at least two tenant protection policies that go beyond state law.

To help jurisdictions pass effective tenant protections, MTC will soon be releasing model ordinances for
a number of the policies Our Home San Mateo has recommended. These model ordinances can help
reduce staff research time and make sure the City is staying in-step with other cities and counties in the
Bay Area.

653 of 891


--- page 654 ---
2
With powerful support from both the community and the region, there is no better time than now for San 
Mateo to pass the strongest tenant protections it can. 
 
Thank you for your consideration, 
 
Amanda

Amanda Chang (all pronouns)

From: marylene notarianni

Sent: Tuesday, April 8, 2025 5:34 PM

To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; akohjikian@cityofsanmateo.org; City Attorney's Office
<CityAttorneysOffice @cityofsanmateo.org>; Rachel Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>

Subject: COMPLETELY OPPOSE TO INCREASE TENANTS PROTECTION - Let me tell you why?

Dear All,

I fully understand the intent to protect tenants, especially in cases where
certain property owners may overstep by imposing excessive charges, rent
hikes, or displaying disrespectful behavior. However, such instances are not
the norm, and the majority of property owners act with integrity. While it’s
important to maintain a balance, I believe the current regulations already
provide ample protection to tenants.

That said, I must express my firm opposition to the proposal of offering compensation, particularly for
tenants who are "not at fault." Allow me to share my personal experience. My business has a 70%
decline this year, and when my landlords decided to sell their property, I was given a 60-day notice. In
response, I followed the law and gave my own tenant a 60-day notice to vacate my property.
Throughout this process, I was always respectful to my tenants. For example, I allowed them to move
in six days before the lease contract began without charging them rent. Additionally, I did not raise the
rent for two years. I even moved into another rental property last year to accommodate them, which
incurred the cost of two moving expenses.

In the end, my tenant vacated the property on the final day of his 60 days notice, at 5:00 PM. A
week later, he requested compensation for being "not at fault." I found this request to be
highly unfair, especially considering the tenant's financial position. While his request was
within the legal framework of San Mateo County, I felt like an abuse of the system, particularly
since he was financially stable and had returned to his father’s estate in Tiburon.

As property owners, we are the ones paying the property taxes, taking on the risks of purchasing and

maintaming the property, and shouldering the costs of any damage or special assessments, particularly
with the current challenges posed by SB326 and rising fire insurance premiums. Increasing the

655 of 891


--- page 656 ---
2
relocation compensation for "No Fault" cases would be highly unjust, and this type of rule is not even 
existing in Europe and in France. 
For these reasons, I strongly urge you not to increase the compensation fee to one year for tenants 
who are "Not at Fault." The existing provisions should suffice, and imposing such a burden would be 
especially detrimental for those of us who are already financially struggling. I am completely opposed 
to this proposal. 
Thank you for your attention.

From:

Sent: Wednesday, April 9, 2025 9:00 AM

To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; City
Attorney's Office <CityAttorneysOffice@cityofsanmateo.org>; Rachel Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>

Subject: Support Tenant Protections in San Mateo

Hello San Mateo Councilmembers, City Attorney, and policymakers,

As the Council considers ways in which to fulfill its commitment to expand tenant protections under AB 1482, Tenants
Together encourages you to make changes that will meaningfully enhance housing security for renters in the City of San
Mateo. As California’s statewide coalition for renters’ rights since 2008, we are well-versed in local and state tenant
law and policy. We wholeheartedly support the policy proposals brought by the Our Home San Mateo coalition in
advance of the April 21t meeting.

Renters in San Mateo comprise nearly half the population, and face some of the highest home prices and rents in the
nation. One in four pays more than 50 percent of their income on rent, leaving very little for other essential needs that
include food, health care, transportation, utilities and child care. The prospect of an economic recession and the
potential removal of federal supports like Medicaid, housing vouchers and SNAP benefits will only put more San Mateo
working families who rent at risk of eviction and homelessness.

Many of those most affected are workers whose contributions are essential to the functioning of the community—child
care workers, paramedics, home health aides, elementary school teachers, security guards, and retail clerks. If we
continue to hemorrhage the people who fill these critical jobs, the future vitality of San Mateo is at risk.

In the interest of protecting the well-being of San Mateo renters, and the future of the city itself, Tenants Together
encourages the city to adopt the following commonsense policies that have been proven to prevent homelessness in
scores of jurisdictions across California:

Just cause for eviction from Day One

Prohibition of no-fault evictions during the school year
Right to return after a substantial remodel

Increased relocation assistance

Creation of a rental data registry

The above measures are concrete ways the council can act to protect families from homelessness and are critical to
fulfilling the obligations set forth in San Mateo’s housing element. Tenants Together encourages the council to move
with an appropriate sense of urgency to move them into law.

In solidarity,

Shanti Singh (she/her/ella)

From:

Subject: Opposing San Mateo Study Session on Rent Control

Good Afternoon Members of the San Mateo City Council, Mayor Newsom, City Manager Khojikian, City
Attorney Rasiah, and Housing Manager Horst,

My name is Rachel Salomon and | founded a property management and real estate brokerage company
about 18 months ago in San Mateo County. Our clients, who are owners of rental properties in the
county, are not wealthy, but have been able to build a net worth that is almost exclusively in their local
realestate. In some cases, these owners are not even cash flow positive on their properties because
their mortgages and property taxes are already so high, compounded by the fact that the homes are older
and need significant capital improvements to keep them habitable and safe for their tenants. In all
cases, Our Current client base owns only one rental property. Expanding on the tenant rights/protections
under AB1482 to include increased property taxes, relocation payments for No-fault evictions, and
mandating reporting will drive up the holding costs for those rental properties which will ultimately 1)
hurt the tenants who are renting the properties, and 2) motivate more owners to sell their properties
resulting in a decreased supply of rental units in San Mateo County.

lam empathetic to those who are directly impacted by the high market rents, and I'm also aware that
more and more residents of California are renting their primary place of residence. We believe a more
effective solution to affordable housing is not only to increase the supply, but to incentivize real estate
investors and developers to build more housing. We have seen time and again in the Bay Area,
developers and corporations get tax incentives to bring in jobs, but never to bring in housing, causing a
huge imbalance for the populations servicing those jobs. Increasing regulations on landlords will
ultimately make housing less affordable because the rental supply will decrease when landlords decide
it is no longer beneficial to own and operate rental properties from too many regulations. Tenants will be
forced to move farther away from their jobs simply to find rental housing, regardless of the cost.

What the city should be considering is howto increase the number of rental units. specifically with

and condominium construction. Adding more regulation and increasing bureaucracy will only hurt the
people at the bottom who are ultimately the people you are setting out to help.

PMI Redwood Realty has informed its clients of this meeting and what is proposed. Additionally,
representatives from our company will be attending the presentation on 4/21 to learn more about the

info@sustainablesanmateo.org
www.sustainablesanmateo.org

San Mateo City Council
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403

April 11, 2025

Dear Mayor Newsom and Councilmembers,

Sustainable San Mateo County (SSMC) appreciates the opportunity to provide
input on renter protection measures being proposed by Our Home San Mateo.
Their efforts to prevent displacement and keep San Mateo renters in their homes
align with our mission to promote long-term sustainability and equity in the region.

As shown in our Sustainability Dashboard, approximately half of San Mateo
households are renters. Renter protection solutions—such as just cause for
eviction, limits on no-fault evictions, the right to return after remodels, relocation
assistance and a rental data registry—can help prevent displacement, increase
transparency and promote more resilient communities. SSMC’s Sustainability
Ideas Bank highlights several local policy tools that support renter stability.

While SSMC does not take formal positions on specific city ordinances, we provide
research and promote solutions that align with our mission. Renter protections are
a key strategy for addressing the housing insecurity that disproportionately affects
low-income households and essential workers—those most likely to face long
commutes if displaced, thereby increasing vehicle miles traveled and undermining
local climate goals.

We commend the City of San Mateo for exploring policy approaches that
acknowledge the intersection of housing, equity and sustainability. SSMC
welcomes continued dialogue and remains a resource for data-driven strategies
that support a more resilient San Mateo.

Sincerely,

yeh Y=

Sarah Hubbard
Executive Director
Sustainable San Mateo County

Our federal tax identification number is 48-1265207. SSMC is a 501(c)(3) organization with tax-exempt status.

Subject: Support for Renter Protections

Dear City Council Members,

| am a longtime San Mateo resident and | am concerned about keeping San Mateo a place where
people of all income levels can thrive. So as you consider ways in which to expand protections
under AB 1482, | encourage you to make changes that will meaningfully enhance housing security for
renters in the City of San Mateo.

According to multiple sources, renters in San Mateo comprise nearly half of our population, and with
the exceedingly high rents in the city, many of them lead highly tenuous lives. One in four pays more
than 50 percent of their income on rent, leaving very little for other essential needs that include food,
health care, transportation, utilities and child care. Furthermore, there is strong reason to expect that
in the coming four years, they will be subjected to takeaways that make life increasingly difficult for
them. Already there is talk of assaults upon Medicaid, housing vouchers and SNAP benefits, all of
which are vital supports for people of lower income. In an environment such as this, where the needs
of our more vulnerable community members are so readily compromised, there is ever more reason
to keep renters from being subjected to the cost and trauma they face when evicted from their homes.

Stable and secure housing is fundamental to the ability of families to thrive. Please support the
proposals set forth by the coalition of organizations called “Our Home San Mateo” to help increase
housing security for renters in a regrettably insecure time.

Thank you for your consideration of these important proposals from the Our Home San Mateo
coalition.

on the housing security of renters in San Mateo. Specifically, (name of organization) supports the policy
proposals that have been set forth by the “Our Home San Mateo” coalition.

The City of San Mateo is known to have some of the highest housing costs—both home prices and
rents—in the entire nation. Furthermore, the city’s housing element says that rents increased 75% ina
period of 10 years. In the face of increasing rents and wages that nowhere near keep pace, many San
Mateo renters live paycheck to paycheck, struggling to keep food on the table, pay their utility bills,
clothe their children, and make the rent. They live in a highly fragile state and are at risk of being
displaced.

community—child care workers, paramedics, home health aides, elementary school teachers, security
guards, and retail clerks. If we continue to hemorrhage the people who fill these critical jobs, the future
vitality of San Mateo is at risk.

In the interest of protecting the well-being of San Mateo renters, and the future of the city itself, |
encourage the city to take bold action to keep renters in their homes.

and the cost to maintain them is extremely high, and rises each year with the cost of labor
and materials rising exponentially. Additionally, the cost of homeowners insurance has risen
and is projected to rise even more in the future with all of the fires that have occurred in
California over the past half decade. The guidelines that have been proposed will ultimately
increase my holding costs by requiring specific reporting that will likely be annual if not more
frequently, expecting homeowners to pay for databases and staff salaries with increased
property taxes, and require that | pay for my tenants to relocate if | want to take back control
of my property to make necessary upgrades and improvements to my aging property.

If the goal of these proposals is to create a better tenant experience or make renting a home
more affordable, then the proposed measures are falling woefully short. They may be
addressing some issues in San Mateo, but the ultimate problem is that there is simply not
enough housing supply for everyone who works here. The city should be looking for ways to
address this shortage, and not punish the landlords that are currently supplying

housing. Actions that would benefit the broader community might include:

1) Removing the red tape around construction to increase the supply of homes.

2) Incentivise owners of single family rental homes to convert properties into multi-family.
3) Woo developers who clear the path for more homeownership.

4) Require that new apartment developments meet the needs of families by requiring more
units be at least 3 bedrooms.

Subject: High Cost of Rent in the City of San Mateo

Dear San Mateo City Councilors,

One out of every four renter residents of San Mateo pays more than half of their take-home pay on rent, leaving very
little for other essential needs that include food, health care, transportation, utilities and child care that is a necessity for
job holders often holding down two jobs in order to get by. These families are on the edge of potential homelessness,
and "camping" on city property and in the parks.

When | was a fresh-faced young man some decades ago, out on his own for the first time, just out of college and moving
into my very first studio apartment, | recall that either by news report on NPR or perhaps in another source of advice
that | read regarding personal budget matters, the advice was that it would be best that you would spend no more that
30% of you take-home pay for rent (or mortgage). If you did so, you'd have sufficient funds to pay for groceries, utilities,
other bills, and a little in your pockets to spend on having fun -- and some money to save in the bank for "rainy days."
The policies that are your responsibility and more specifically the rules that you are in the perfect position to enact
simply in need to protect these vulnerable residents from being evicted, and having no roof over their heads and having
children unable to attend school.

Were | in your position of authority, | might assuage the concerns of those landlords only a small number of apartments
by perhaps setting some limits in the rules. Perhaps it would be something akin to if they own four single-family units
(not apartment complexes), those landlords would be able to receive some dollar amount of recompense, funded by
being taken out of city parking meter revenues.

Thank you for your time and consideration.

Cordially,

Pat Willard

Founder, Director,

Peninsula Anti-Racism Coalition

From:

From:
Sent: Tuesday, April 15, 2025 8:18 AM

Cc: zdah|@cityofsanmteo.org <zdahl@cityofsanmteo.org>
Subject: San Mateo Study April 21

| suggest that the Council should be informed of the following facts before making its decision relating to
new landlord/tenant legislation:

In 2023 there were approximately 70 eviction cases filed relating to tenants in the City of San Mateo. Of
these only 7.4% or 5.18 were “no-fault” actions. Of these only 20.1% or 1.04 went to judgment. (Source:
Stanford Law School Study).

“No fault” evictions include the categories of (1) owner intending to take the rental off market, (2) owner
intending personally to move in or to have a family member move in, or (3) owner intending to demolish

or significantly remodel the unit. (California Civil Code Section 1946.2(b)(2)).

The city cannot say how many rental units were demolished or significantly remodeled in 2023. See
Development Director Zachary Dahl’s April 11 e-mail response to my questions.

683 of 891

2
Mr. Dahl responded that there are approximately 20,000 rental units in the City but he has no records of 
how many owners of rental units or property managers there are. 
How many owners/managers is the City ready to inconvenience and tax with a new ordinance requiring 
registration and fees, and how much is the City ready to increase taxes to pay for new staffing to 
administer and enforce new regulations?  Will the citizen/taxpayers be best served by a new ordinance 
that may possibly prevent fewer than 1.04 tenants from being evicted each year? 
Timothy S. O’Hara 
Realtor ® Property Manager and Attorney 
 
P.S. I am not a resident of the city and do not manage any properties in the city.  For many years I have

Subject: United Way Bay Area Support for One San Mateo Tenant Protections Policies

Dear San Mateo City Council and Staff,
My name is Jess Hudson and |! am the Public Policy Manager for United Way Bay Area.

On April 21 the city council will consider options for fulfilling its commitment to expand protections under AB
1482. | am writing on behalf of United Way Bay Area to encourage the council to adopt policies that will have a
meaningful impact on the housing security of renters in San Mateo. Specifically, United Way Bay Area supports the
policy proposals that have been set forth by the “Our Home San Mateo” coalition. These policies include:

e Just cause for eviction from Day One

e Prohibition of no-fault evictions during the school year
e Rightto return after a substantial remodel

e Increased relocation assistance

e Creation of a rental data registry

The City of San Mateo is known to have some of the highest housing costs—both home prices and rents—in the
entire nation. Furthermore, the city’s housing element says that rents increased 75% in a period of 10 years. In the
face of increasing rents and wages that nowhere near keep pace, many San Mateo renters live paycheck to
paycheck, struggling to keep food on the table, pay their utility bills, clothe their children, and make the rent. They
live in a highly fragile state and are at risk of being displaced.

Residents who experience a no-fault eviction are entitled to just one month's rent under AB 1482, but we know that
residents will require first and last month's rent and security deposit in order to move into a new unit- assuming
one is available within the city in their price range. This leaves residents vulnerable to periods of homelessness or
housing instability, increased their risk of losing their job, and the loss of community ties like neighbors and school
placements for children. A 2017 UC Berkeley Study of San Mateo County renters found one in three San Mateo
residents experienced some period of homelessness or marginal housing in the two years following their eviction
and experienced multiple negative impacts to their safety, wellbeing, and employment opportunities.

Many of those most affected are workers whose contributions are essential to the functioning of the community—
childcare workers, paramedics, home health aides, elementary school teachers, security guards, and retail clerks.
If we continue to hemorrhage the people who fill these critical jobs, the future vitality of San Mateo is at risk.

In the interest of protecting the well-being of San Mateo renters, and the future of the city itself, United Way Bay
Area encourages the city to take bold action to keep renters in their homes. Strong anti-displacement policies

instability and the poverty risk outcomes that are associated with it.

From:

<rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>; City Attorney's Office <CityAttorneysOffice@cityofsanmateo.org>;

Subject: Proposed Tenant Protections

April 15, 2025

Mayor Newsom and Members of the City Council:

We ask that you not move forward with any of the expanded tenant protections being
proposed by staff as they are very one-sided and will have a punishing effect on
housing providers in this community who are already laboring under a very challenging

financial environment.

Our costs for repairs are skyrocketing due to the demand for labor and

materials. Moreover, many of us will likely be required to spend tens of thousands of
dollars to convert our buildings to electric power in the near future to comply with
recently adopted mandates which will not save us much, if any, money as our

residents are the ones who consume the majority of the power on our properties to

1

687 of 891


--- page 688 ---
2
heat their residences, warm their water, and prepare their meals.  Finally, and very 
significantly, our insurance costs are skyrocketing and the coverage provided is 
shrinking.  This translates into us needing to spend much more money on premiums 
and incur higher expenses that were previously covered by our insurers.  
Tenant protections are burdensome and add to the expense of providing 
housing.  Expanding the tenant protections provided by State law will only amplify the 
challenges faced by housing providers and will not increase the supply of much 
needed low-income housing.
Most housing providers will agree that the high cost of housing is indeed a vexing 
problem that needs to be resolved.  To place the financial burden of resolving this 
problem on one set of constituents is both unfair and unwise.  
Instead, we ask that you stop this assault on those of us who provide housing and 
instead work with all the stakeholders in the housing community to craft a solution that 
takes into account the needs of all.
Brian and Ann Ponty
San Mateo Housing Providers

From:

Subject: "Tenant Protection"

Dear City Manager and City Council,

Seriously, we need to drop the use of "tenant protection" and start using "tenant/landlord" collaboration if we are to
come to a solution.

Tenants enter into a "partnership" through a residential lease which is their agreement with the terms and conditions
specified. If terms and conditions are not met, it is a breach of contract.

The unprecedented Pandemic addressed the need for tenant protection as a PUBLIC HEALTH MEASURE to stop the
spread of a killer virus, Covid-19.

Further extension of this temporary, emergency executive order (SM County and Federal Health Departments) is an
abusive use of AB 1482.

The use of the phrase"tenant protection" connotates a negative image which is a one sided picture of the situation. If
you want to use this phrase, then, there has to be similarly, "landlord protection", (primarily, LOSS of income to pay the
mortgage, property insurance, utilities-water, garbage, repair, book keeping, and management bills, AND the risk of

facing foreclosure due to non payment of mortages through loss of rents).

Please consider dropping the use of these types of phrases, "tenant protection". This is negative terminology and
depressive.

There are not mass evictions happening like the mass deaths that occurred during Covid-19 that actually required
"protective measures."

Doris Nishimoto, D.D.S.

April 14, 2025
Dear Council Members,

On behalf of the 40+ interfaith leaders in the Peninsula Solidarity Cohort, we thank you for your
service to the community of San Mateo. At this time of uncertainty and instability in our country and
around the world, local leadership matters more than ever to secure safety and stability for our
residents. As you consider expanding tenant protections under AB 1482, we urge you to support
proposals that will increase housing security and reduce the trauma of eviction and displacement.

As faith leaders serving a wide range of communities within our county, we know that people are
hurting and afraid. Already the City of San Mateo has some of the highest housing costs in the
country, with rents increasing by some 75% in ten years, far outpacing wage increases. Nearly half of
San Mateo residents rent their homes, including many essential workers who are forced to spend so
much on rent they have little left for other basic needs like food, health care, clothing, transportation,
utilities and childcare. In one of the wealthiest counties in the country, it is unconscionable that so
many of our residents live so close to the edge, chronically at risk of eviction and displacement. And
now, with the federal government threatening to strip away Medicaid, housing vouchers and SNAP
benefits, low-income residents are at even greater risk.

691 of 891


--- page 692 ---
2
Secure and stable housing is critical for the well-being of individuals and families, and it’s critical for a 
thriving community as well. Out of our shared concern for the well-being of our beloved San Mateo 
community and all who dwell in it, we urge you to support the proposals delineated by the “Our Home 
San Mateo” coalition.   
Together in service to our community,

From:

Sent: Wednesday, April 16, 2025 11:13 AM

To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>
Ce:

Subject: Tenant Protections, April 21 Study Session Agenda

April 16, 2025

Mayor Robert Newsom

Deputy Mayor Loraine

Council Member Cwirko-Godycki
Council Member Fernandez
Council Member Diaz Dash

Subject: San Mateo Climate Action Team supports Tenant Protections for our city’s renters.
Dear Mayor and Council

We join many other organizations and individuals in San Mateo in asking the City Council to adopt
strong policy enhancements, in the form of an ordinance that will provide greater security to tenants.

Housing is a climate issue, it is a right, and because there are insufficient numbers of housing units in
San Mateo, our workers, teachers, care givers, and so many others are forced to endure long,
tortuous commutes.

We recognize the capital investment of property owners, that many scrimp and save just to be able to
create income property, and that many property owners have very limited holdings. But we also
recognize that an even greater burden is placed on renters forced to pay a major portion of their
income on housing.

Housing is a climate issue!

We support these five key components of tenant protection:

1. Just cause protections from day one. Current law provides a small amount of such
protection to tenants, but only after most of them have been tenants of the property for more
than 12 months. The cities of San Jose, Hayward, and Alameda have adopted day one
protections.

2. Prohibit no-fault evictions during the school year. The life of most children revolves around
their school activities. The trauma caused by loss of connection to friends, adult mentors, and

1

693 of 891


--- page 694 ---
2
activities, especially in the middle of the school year, is unnecessary. If the no fault eviction 
could wait until the end of the year, children will be better protected. 
3. Provide the right to return after substantial remodel. Current law allows a landlord to evict
a tenant for “substantial remodel” of a property, but no right to return, even at a higher
rent.  We support policy changes that create a right to return at a reasonable rent.
4. Increase relocation payments to the tenant, after just cause eviction for substantial
remodel. The City already requires three months’ relocation assistance for condo conversions
and demolitions. Please create consistency, which has the added benefit of providing the
displaced tenant an adequate compensation for moving, which is most often very expensive.
5. Create a rental registry to generate data and track compliance. A rental registry is a
valuable asset for the Community, Staff and the Council. More than 30 years ago, a group in
San Mateo County developed a means of measuring how cities in the County are doing in
many areas – RHNA numbers, energy usage, climate action plans, and transportation. Taking
data to the Cities, those of us involved in the benchmarking process were able to show
successes, and where cities could put more emphasis. A rental registry is a vital addition to the
successful implementation of Tenant Protections. While AB 1482 created a set of renter
protections, there currently is no way to track whether those renter protections are being met.
Please direct staff at your April 21 Study Session to prepare for consideration a strong Tenant 
Protection Ordinance that includes the five points above. 
Thank you 
Robert Whitehair, Co Leader 
Michelle Hudson, Co Leader 
San Mateo Climate Action Team 
694 of 891

From:

Sent: Wednesday, April 16, 2025 3:38 PM

To: Rob Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Nicole Fernandez <nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>; Adam Loraine
<aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>; Lisa Diaz Nash <Ildiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Danielle Cwirko-Godycki
<dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org>

Ce: Clerk <cityclerk@cityofsanmateo.org>; City Mgr <citymgr@cityofsanmateo.org>; City Attorney's Office
<CityAttorneysOffice@cityofsanmateo.org>

Subject: SUPPORT for expanding AB 1482

Dear Mayor Newsom and Members of the San Mateo City Council,

As the study session approaches at which the council will discuss various options for expanding AB 1482, I am
writing on behalf of One San Mateo, which is a member of the coalition called “Our Home San Mateo,” to
express our interest in your taking meaningful action to expand AB 1482 and fulfill the city’s obligation to
Affirmatively Further Fair Housing.

When the California legislature adopted Assembly Bill 686 in 2015, it affirmed the obligation of public agencies
to Affirmatively Further Fair Housing, an obligation that was originally articulated in the Fair Housing Act of
1968. Further, AB 686 named the Department of Housing and Community Development as the agency
responsible for oversight and enforcement of AFFH. In April of 2021, HCD issued a 94-page memo with
guidelines for the implementation of AB 686. This memo asserts, in no uncertain terms, that anti-

displacement measures are integral to the fulfillment of obligations under AFFH, laying out a wide range of
anti-displacement policies that would qualify in satisfaction of this. It mentions, among many others, rent
control, just cause for eviction, relocation assistance, and tenant right to counsel.

In its recently certified housing element, the City of San Mateo committed to at least one such policy. Policy
H3.4 states that the city will “Extend AB 1482 provisions to require tenant relocation payments for No Fault
evictions for those with tenure less than one year.” While this is significant, One San Mateo, along with the
other organizations in the “Our Home San Mateo” coalition, maintains that this one policy would be
insufficient to satisfy the city’s obligations under AFFH. We have set forth five other proposals that we believe
would enable the city to make a more fulsome response to the requirements under AFFH.

Those policies are:

1. Starting just cause for eviction on Day One
2. Prohibiting no-fault evictions while the tenants’ children are in school

695 of 891


--- page 696 ---
2
3.
Providing the right to return after a substantial remodel, with a cap on the allowable rent
increase
4.
Increasing relocation assistance for No Fault evictions associated with substantial remodel,
owner move-in and Ellis Act withdrawals to three months, in line with the city’s demolition and
condo conversion policies
5.
Creating a rental data registry
The objective of AFFH is to help reverse the discriminatory impacts of past housing practices and to foster the 
creation of inclusive communities. These are goals we believe the council would readily sign onto. In the 
interest of furthering them, One San Mateo encourages the council to act with an appropriate sense of urgency 
to move the above policies into law. 
Sincerely, 
Karyl Eldridge 
Vice Chair of One San Mateo 
*Wire Fraud is Real*.  Before wiring any money, call the intended recipient at a number you know is valid
to confirm the instructions. Additionally, please note that the sender does not have authority to bind a
party to a real estate contract via written or verbal communication.

Re: Tenant Protection Ordinance Updates Proposed by Our Home San Mateo - Support
To the Honorable City Council,

The League of Women Voters is a non-partisan, grassroots organization founded over 100 years
ago to protect democracy and help people participate in governmental decisions that affect their
lives. We advocate for public policies only when we determine that they comply with a formal
League position addressing a particular issue. Positions require thorough and objective member
study and consensus.

The national League’s position on meeting basic needs calls on us to “support programs and
policies to prevent or reduce poverty and to promote self-sufficiency for individuals and families
. .. [including] policies to provide a decent home and a suitable living environment for every
American family.” That’s why this League endorses the policy recommendations related to
tenant protections developed by the Our Home San Mateo coalition. Each of the five proposed
changes was carefully designed to target realities caused by the affordable housing crisis we all
acknowledge, including factors that result in the tragic disruptions associated with “no fault”
evictions, exorbitantly high rents, and importantly, the inadequacy of data collection processes
needed to make effective policy decisions.

We urge you to comply with the City’s Housing Element’s commitment to update pertinent
ordinances by adopting and implementing those recommendations.

Thank you for your efforts to obtain community input on this matter and for this opportunity to
offer our views.

Respectfully,

Tina Doede, President

444 Peninsula Ave. #1 San Mateo, CA 94401

April 16, 2025 
 
Mayor Rob Newsom Jr. 
Deputy Mayor Adam Loraine 
Council Member Lisa Diaz Nash 
Council Member Danielle Cwirko-Godycki 
Council Member Nicole Fernandez 
 
Dear Honorable San Mateo City Council Members: 
 
On April 21, the City Council will consider options for fulfilling its commitment to expand 
renter protections. I am writing on behalf of Public Advocates to urge the City Council to adopt 
policies that will have a meaningful impact on the housing security of renters in San Mateo. 
Specifically, Public Advocates supports the policy proposals that have been set forth by the “Our 
Home San Mateo” coalition. These changes are focused on preventing evictions and helping to 
keep San Mateo renters in their homes. 
 
Public Advocates is a nonprofit law firm and advocacy organization that challenges the systemic 
causes of poverty and racial discrimination by strengthening community voices in public policy 
and achieving tangible legal victories advancing education, housing, transportation equity, and 
climate justice. Our organization has deep expertise in renter protections and other local anti-
displacement policies.  
 
The City of San Mateo is known to have some of the highest housing costs—both home prices 
and rents—in the entire nation.  Furthermore, the city’s Housing Element says that rents 
increased 75% in a period of 10 years. In the face of increasing rents and stagnating wages, many 
San Mateo renters live paycheck to paycheck, struggling to keep food on the table, pay their 
utility bills, clothe their children, and make the rent. They live at great risk of displacement. 
 
Eviction is so much more than a forced move. It can be a life-changing event that seriously 
harms families, undermining their economic security and housing stability for years to come. 
Data shows that eviction negatively impacts health outcomes, and eviction can be especially 
traumatizing for children. Often when families are forced to move, children abruptly lose their 
connections to neighborhoods and must switch schools, disrupting learning.  
  
In the interest of shielding families from the negative effects of eviction, Public Advocates 
encourages the City Council to adopt the “Our Home San Mateo” policy proposals, as follows: 
  
1. Just cause for eviction from Day One 
2. Prohibition of no-fault evictions during the school year 
3. Right to return after a substantial remodel  
4. Increased relocation assistance 
5. Creation of a rental data registry 
  
701 of 891

--- page 702 ---
The above measures are concrete ways the City Council can take action to protect families from 
eviction, displacement, and homelessness and to increase housing stability. Public Advocates 
encourages the City Council to move with urgency to enact these policies.  
We have expertise in state and local landlord-tenant law and have drafted just cause for eviction 
ordinances for jurisdictions across the Bay Area. Please contact me with any questions about 
how these policies work in practice or why they are so urgently needed – Public Advocates 
would be happy to provide technical assistance on these policy proposals or answer any legal 
questions. 
Thank you. 
Sincerely, 
Suzanne Dershowitz 
Senior Staff Attorney 
Public Advocates

Subject: Support for Our Home San Mateo's policy proposals

Dear San Mateo City Council and Staff:

On behalf of Community Legal Services in East Palo Alto (CLSEPA), I would like to encourage the city
council to adopt the policy changes set forth by the “Our Home San Mateo.” These changes are
focused on preventing evictions and helping to keep San Mateo renters in their homes.

Eviction is so much more than a forced move. It can, in fact, be a life-changing event that does
families serious harm, undermining their prospects for years to come. It is known to impact both
physical and emotional health, and for families with limited resources, the costs involved can
exacerbate challenging financial circumstances.

Often when families are forced to move, families and children abruptly lose their connections to
neighborhood, church and school. Their whole support system can collapse. Eviction often correlates
with a drop in school performance.

In the interest of shielding families and individuals from the negative effects of eviction and providing
stability from day one, CLSEPA encourages the council to adopt the “Our Home San Mateo” policy
proposals, as follows:

1. Just cause for eviction from day one to provide all families with stable housing from the
beginning of their tenancy

2. Prohibition of no-fault evictions during the school year

3. Right to return after a substantial remodel

4. Increased relocation assistance
5. Creation of a rental data registry to provide data to the City to continue to improve stability
in the rental housing market

evictions and increase the stability of their lives. CLSEPA encourages the council to move with an 
appropriate sense of urgency to move them into law. 
Best regards,

Subject: Housing Providers Need Your He Staff Proposals Rent Control

Mayor Newsom and Members of the City Council:

We ask that you not move forward with any of the expanded tenant protections being
proposed by staff as they are very one-sided and will have a punishing effect on
housing providers in this community who are already laboring under a very challenging
financial environment.

Our costs for repairs are skyrocketing due to the demand for labor and

materials. Moreover, many of us will likely be required to spend tens of thousands of
dollars to convert our buildings to electric power in the near future to comply with
recently adopted mandates which will not save us much, if any, money. Finally, and
very significantly, our insurance costs are skyrocketing and the coverage provided is
shrinking. This translates into us needing to spend much more money on premiums
and incur higher expenses that were previously covered by our insurers.

Tenant protections are burdensome and add to the expense of providing

housing. Expanding the tenant protections provided by State law will only amplify the
challenges faced by housing providers and will not increase the supply of much
needed low-income housing. Such protections are pushing out of existence small
rental property Owners (e.g. 4 units or less).

Most housing providers will agree that the high cost of housing is indeed a vexing
problem that needs to be resolved. To place the financial burden of resolving this

problem on one set of constituents is both unfair and unwise.
1

705 of 891


--- page 706 ---
2
Instead, we ask that you stop this assault on those of us who provide housing and 
instead work with all the stakeholders in the housing community to craft a solution that 
takes into account the needs of all.
Sincerely,
Steven Runchey, Housing Provider

Dear Council Members,

| am writing to express my support for stronger protections for renters and to show my support for a
comprehensive rental registry in the city of San Mateo.

Renters make up 48% of our population and deserve safe, stable, and dignified housing. Unfortunately,
without transparency and accountability, tenants often face challenges such as substandard living
conditions, unjust evictions, and difficulty contacting landlords for essential repairs.

Itis so hard to understand how the city of San Mateo can be supportive of all its residents when it does
not have records or details of the renter’s situations.

Arental registry is a crucial step toward protecting renters’ rights. It simply requires landlords to register
their properties and provides the city the capability to ensure better enforcement of housing codes,
create accountability for negligent landlords, and generate reliable data to inform housing policy.

| urge the San Mateo City Council to consider the long-term benefits of a rental registry and to continue
prioritizing policies that protect renters' rights and promote housing stability in our community.
