Subject: Letter on Rental Protections in San Mateo

Dear City Council,

In order to maintain San Mateo’s character as a supportive location for middle class families, change, in
the form of enhanced rental protections, is necessary.

The core values outlined in the City of San Mateo’s charter require consideration for diversity and
inclusivity. Nearly half of the city’s residents are renters. Keeping these citizens safe from unjust eviction
is paramount to the stability and continuity of their lives, as well as the lives of their children.

Housing security for renters, in the form of predictable increases and the expectation of renewal, should
be considered a fundamental right and a key goal of the Council. This plea is echoed by a large coalition of
nonprofit organizations. Housing in San Mateo should not be simply a good to be purveyed to the highest
bidder and at the whim of speculating landlords.

This council has an uncommon ability right now to champion fifty thousand San Mateo citizens. Please
undertake this effort with the goal of embracing these renters as an increasing percentage of the
population and equally deserving of establishing themselves in San Mateo. The cost to buy is beyond most
people, native and new. This should not inevitably lead to their political and social disenfranchisement in
the form of not having any right to call their long term residence their permanent home.

Kind Regards,

Kara Cox

Item #10 — No New Laws!

From Michael Anthony <a

via votervoice.net
Date Fri 4/18/2025 3:00 PM
To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Clerk McTaggart,

| am disappointed that city staff is fast-tracking a series of new laws to regulate privately owned rental housing in
the city.

| provide a much-needed resource in your city — rental housing. Yet, my voice is secondary to the voices of the
activists, amplified by city staff, who are demanding new laws.

City staffs report highlights that the No. 1 cause of renter displacement is nonpayment of rent. Your focus should
be directed toward programs and public funding that would help renters who face economic emergencies — not
the new regulations staff is promoting.

Please recognize that housing providers experience economic challenges as well. It is disheartening that staff's
report does not acknowledge the hardships housing providers also endure. Whether they are financial, regulatory
or legal hardships, we too need assistance from the city and do not need new punitive laws imposed on us.

We went through hardships during the COVID-19 pandemic, and not once did the city ask if we needed help.

| ask that you direct city staff to be fair and take a neutral approach. Do not proceed with new regulations until you
assess what problem you are trying to solve and evaluate what existing laws can be used to address those
concerns without adopting new laws that will continue to make it harder and costlier for us to provide rental housing
to the city’s renters.

Sincerely,

Michael Anthon

Gh Outlook

Vote NO on AB 1157

From Zarine Batliwalla ee

Date Fri 4/18/2025 8:56 AM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Voters and economists alike have warned about the dangers of rent control — and voters have
rejected it at the ballot box again and again. Propositions 10, 21, and 33 were all defeated by more
than 20 percentage points.

Now AB 1157 ignores the will of the voters and threatens to drive even more rental housing out of the
market. Slashing the rent cap to just 2% plus inflation will force many small property owners to sell or
convert their properties, displacing tenants and worsening the housing shortage.

Rental housing providers didn't create California's housing crisis. Penalizing them with punitive rent
caps only makes the situation worse.

Research from institutions like Stanford University and the California Legislative Analyst's Office has
shown that strict rent control reduces housing availability, discourages new construction, and

ultimately harms renters.

Please vote No on AB 1157.

photo Zarine Batliwalla

w.photo

716 of 891

From Rustard Mustard <r

Dear Honorable Mayor and City Council,

physically and emotionally damaged by living in squalor in San Mateo. I have seen
conditions that you would never, ever allow yourself to live in. I have seen children
covered with bedbug sores on their entire bodies, homes that were infested with

stoves that didn't properly work, rodent droppings and so forth.

places for you to see and people to meet. I invite you to tour the reality and I

will be your tour guide.

regulated.

lM Outlook

URGENT A Thoughtful Approach to Protecting San Mateo’s Housing Ecosystem

Date Mon 4/ 12:42 PM

Dear Mayor Newsom, Councilmembers Diaz Nash, Lorraine, Cwirko-Godycki, Fernandez,
and City Staff, and the entire City of San Mateo renters.

Please keep this confidential.

As a real estate professional for 21 years with nd a long-time
advocate for responsible housing policy, | want to express my support for the City of San
Mateo’s exploration of expanded tenant protections, increased transparency through a
rental registry, and strengthened accountability measures for landlords utilizing
substantial remodel exemptions.

While | am fully aware of the concerns raised by SAMCAR and the California Apartment
Association, | believe it is essential that we do not lose sight of the broader context: we
are in the midst of a historic and on-going housing crisis. The cost of living in San Mateo
continues to outpace wage growth, and more and more residents—many of whom are

working families and people of color—are finding themselves displaced or priced out of
the communities they call home.

As a minority voice in this industry, | feel compelled to point out that the influx of
corporate and institutional investments into single-family housing has tilted the market in
favor of large-scale operators, diminishing opportunities for first-time homebuyers and
distorting neighborhood dynamics. In a climate where corporate ownership grows
unchecked, tenant protections are not just justified—they are necessary.

| commend the City for seeking tools to ensure transparency, accountability, and fairness.
A rental registry is not about overregulation; it’s about data-driven policy-making.
Requiring documentation for substantial remodel exemptions ensures the integrity of
these rules and protects tenants from being pushed out under false pretenses. Relocation
assistance and right-to-return policies are humane responses to involuntary
displacement, helping stabilize our communities as | fear things are only going to get
worse.

This isn’t about vilifying landlords or ignoring the legitimate concerns of housing
providers. Rather, it’s about rebalancing the equation to preserve the dignity, security,
and future of renters while continuing to incentivize responsible property ownership. |

720 of 891


--- page 721 ---
respectfully urge you to continue exploring these policies and more with a lens toward
long-term equity and sustainability without losing sight of diversity.

Thank you for your leadership and consideration.

Sincerely,

April 21, 2025
San Mateo City Council
San Mateo City Hall
330 West 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403
Re: Anti-Displacement and Tenant Protection Policy Proposals - Housing Leadership Council of
San Mateo County
Dear Members of the San Mateo City Council,
The Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County (HLC) works with communities and their leaders
to produce and preserve quality affordable homes.
On behalf of HLC, I am writing to encourage you to adopt the policy changes set forth by our partners at
“Our Home San Mateo.” These changes are focused on preventing evictions and helping to keep your
San Mateo renters in their homes.
Eviction is so much more than a forced move. It can be a life-altering event with serious and lasting
impacts on families—undermining their economic security, mental and physical health, and overall
well-being. For community members with limited resources, the costs of eviction can push them over a
financial cliff.
Eviction can be especially traumatizing for children. Often, when families are forced to move, children
abruptly lose their connections to neighborhood, church, and school. Their entire support system
collapses. Research shows eviction is linked to declines in academic performance and an increased risk
of involvement in the juvenile justice system.
In the interest of shielding families from the negative effects of eviction, HLC would like to encourage
you to adopt the following “Our Home San Mateo” anti-displacement and tenant protection policy
proposals:
1. Just cause for eviction from Day One
2. Prohibition of no-fault evictions during the school year
3. Right to return after a substantial remodel
4. Increased relocation assistance
5. Creation of a rental data registry
722 of 891

--- page 723 ---
You have an opportunity to create a meaningful, lasting impact on the lives of your community members.
By adopting the proposed measures, you will be taking clear, tangible steps to protect San Mateo
families from the devastating effects of eviction and foster greater stability in your community. We urge
you to support these vital protections for your community members.
Sincerely,
Ken Chan
Senior Organizer
Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County
Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County
2905 S. El Camino Real, San Mateo, CA 94403 • (650) 242-1764 • hlcsmc.org

| provide a much-needed resource in your city — rental housing. Yet, my voice is secondary to the voices of the
activists, amplified by city staff, who are demanding new laws.

City staffs report highlights that the No. 1 cause of renter displacement is nonpayment of rent. Your focus should

the new regulations staff is promoting.

report does not acknowledge the hardships housing providers also endure. Whether they are financial, regulatory
or legal hardships, we too need assistance from the city and do not need new punitive laws imposed on us.

We went through hardships during the COVID-19 pandemic, and not once did the city ask if we needed help.

| ask that you direct city staff to be fair and take a neutral approach. Do not proceed with new regulations until you
assess what problem you are trying to solve and evaluate what existing laws can be used to address those
concerns without adopting new laws that will continue to make it harder and costlier for us to provide rental housing
to the city’s renters.

Oh Outlook

Opposing added Rent Control in San Mateo

Date Mon 4/21/2025 3:09 PM

To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; akohjikian@cityofsanmateo.org
<akohjikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; City Attorney's Office <CityAttorneysOffice @cityofsanmateo.org>; Rachel
Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>

To:

Citv

Manager Alex Khojikian,
City

Attorney Prasanna Rasiah,
Housing

Manager Rachel Horst, and
City

Council members Rob Newsom (Mayor),
Lisa

Diaz Nash,

Adam

Lorraine,

Nicole

Fernandez

Gentlepersons:

Further rent control measures are not necessary in San Mateo. We have a state law for rent
control and an online calculator showing the rate at which

rent can be increased in each of California's 58 counties. It ls County Specific. There is a
maximum of 10% regardless of the cost of living and the allowable rate has hovered around
8%.

The cost of doing business has increased phenomenally, due for the most part to expenses for
which we have no control. All of the utilities have asked for and been granted rate increases.
PGE, has asked for and been granted rate hikes in one year, Recology has just about doubled
in 10 years, now it's $42 or $47 per can in South San Francisco, Comcast has just raised it's
business and residential rates. The water department - the costs for waste water now exceed
the cost for clean water. San Bruno stopped including this fee on the water invoice and now
includes it on the tax bill. There is that 10% cap on writing off taxes paid. Another loss.

There is also the topic of maintenance and repairs and permits for same. The costs for
plumbers, painters, electricians. has gone way up. Costs increased. If the average cost paid
before was $300-$500, now | have found that it's $700-$1000.

Also, new fees and license requirements have been implemented around the state. South San
Francisco now requires rental providers to obtain a business license. That's another $300+

Then we can look at tenants, payment of rent, security deposits and damages to the property.
Security deposits are now capped at the equivalent of one month's rent. So if you have an
apartment that you rent for $2,000 and the tenant backs into your garage door - that can cost
you $1000 if it's a partial repair. You repair the garage door because, aside from the esthetics,

728 of 891


--- page 729 ---
gaps will allow rodents to enter and that will lead to the health department and/or Vector control.
Then the tenant rolls into it again, another $1000 - you've maxed the security deposit.

By the way, the tenant rolls into the door because, contrary to the lease (so written to prevent
precisely this), parks right up to the door.

You can't ask them to replenish the deposit if you'd raised the rent in the same year because
you'd then be exceeding the maximum annual percentage for an increase. If nothing else, you
could end up in court to "discuss".

This was not a hypothetical!

A property owner can live with the state mandated rent control in it's present form. Implementing
rent control as outlined, you are facilitating the demise of the small property owner. A property
owner cannot be expected to subsidize California's renters, and yet, that is the direction to
which property owners are being forcibly herded.

The reality is that costs everywhere have skyrocketed. Measures like these should be fairly
considered. You Must Consider Both Sides.

The tenant is not always right. You cannot lump all tenants or all property owners in the same
box.

Just like many other issues raised in your meetings there are already measures in place to
address bad behavior whether on the part of the landlord or the tenant.

Adding more controls to rentals in San Mateo will create more and unnecessary problems and
delays.

Please do not implement San Mateo specific rent control.

Rita Fontana

729 of 891


--- page 730 ---
City of San Mateo Council Members: Concerning New Tenant Landlord Laws

Council members, | have reviewed proposed changes affecting my rentals in California.

| am opposed to the proposals; they will negatively affect the business.

| believe these changes are hostile, poorly thought out and lack understanding of the basic

business principles, chiefly economic reality.

In my opinion, the rental registry is the lesser of the concerns because if the changes
in “just cause” and additional “relocation” proposal pass a large number of rentals
will become economically unsustainable and the rental registry will be a colossal

failure.

Let’s look at the basic balance sheet for the average landlord. The cashflow probably
looks great to most folks but the net profit is an entirely different story.

Income: Monthly rental charges. Full tenancy for a year is not realistic, there are repairs to
be made after a tenant leaves.

Costs: Property Tax, Property Insurance (if you can acquire it), painting costs after a tenant
moves out, State of California Tax, Federal Tax, Gardening fees, legal expenses, CPA costs,
repair and cleanup losses can often be greater than the rental deposit allowed by AB 1482

and nearly impossible to collect on.

Considerations:

1. The life expectancy for most appliances is less than 8 years.
2. Property insurance in California is becoming increasingly more expensive if

available.
3. Inthe past 4 years nationally the inflation rate exceeded 22% based on the Bureau

of Labor Statistics, the Bay Area exceeds this figure.
4. Many landlords use rental income to augment their retirement income and already

face aconomic/health challenges.

730 of 891


--- page 731 ---
5. Some tenants will sublet without the landlord’s permission and pocket the
undeclared income.

6. Belongings let behind cause large losses for the landlord.

7. Anon-contested eviction generally takes about 45 days, while a contested one
around 60-75.

8. The rental registry will add costs to the landlord and likely be passed along to the
tenant if they are excessive. This includes inspections which are difficult if not
impossible under AB 1482.

9. The rental registry will likely end up in litigation due to shortcomings/data risks for

the landlord and tenants.
10. Data collected by the rental registry will be shared with Federal agencies.

Gary Laughlin

FW: Subject: Opposition to Proposed Rent Control Regulations — Study Session April 21, 2025

From Rachel Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org >
Date Thu 4/17/2025 3:52 PM
To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Please see below.

From: Omar Galinlo [rrr

Sent: Thursday, April 17, 2025 3:36 PM

To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil @cityofsanmateo.org>; akohjikian@cityofsanmateo.org; City Attorney's

Office <CityAttorneysOffice @cityofsanmateo.org>; Rachel Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>

Cc: Maria Manzo Sa
Subject: Subject: Opposition to Proposed Rent Control Regulations — Study Session April 21, 2025

Omar Galindo

April 17, 2025

San Mateo City Council
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403

Subject: Opposition to Proposed Rent Control Regulations — Study Session April 21,
2025

Dear Honorable Mayor and City Council Members,

As a rental housing provider and long-time member of the San Mateo community, | am writing
to express my strong opposition to the proposed changes to the City’s rental housing
regulations, scheduled for review during the upcoming study session on Monday, April 21, 2025.

While | understand the Council's intent to address housing concerns and tenant protections, the
proposed amendments—particularly the immediate application of “just cause” protections from
the start of a tenancy, the increase in relocation assistance for no-fault terminations, and the
implementation of a rent registry—present serious concerns for local property owners like
myself.

| currently own and manage the following rental properties in San Mateo:

1. Immediate Just Cause Protections:
Under California law (AB 1482), the 12-month period before “just cause” protections apply

732 of 891


--- page 733 ---
allows landlords to make informed decisions about tenants during a reasonable trial period.
Eliminating this window removes a critical tool for property owners to ensure tenants are a good
fit, and may discourage future investment in rental housing within San Mateo.

2. Increased Relocation Assistance:

While | support fair and reasonable relocation compensation in genuine no-fault circumstances,
raising these requirements substantially above state mandates creates additional financial
burdens on landlords, particularly small-scale providers. These policies can lead to difficult and
unintended consequences, such as deferred maintenance or reduced housing availability,
which ultimately harm both landlords and tenants.

3. Rent Registry Requirements:

Mandatory submission of rent roll data introduces ongoing administrative obligations that are
both intrusive and unnecessary. Most landlords are already in compliance with applicable state
law and local codes. Arent registry adds complexity without offering meaningful insight or
benefit, especially for mom-and-pop landlords already stretched thin by compliance and
maintenance costs.

These proposals signal a shift toward overly punitive policies that disregard the challenges
faced by responsible landlords. As a member of the rental housing community, | take pride in
maintaining my properties and treating tenants with fairness and respect. | urge the City Council
to reconsider this approach and work collaboratively with property owners to create policies that
balance tenant protections with the viability and sustainability of rental housing providers.

Thank you for your attention to this matter. | respectfully ask you to oppose the adoption of
these regulations and instead pursue balanced solutions that encourage housing investment
while supporting tenants.

Sincerely,

Omar Galindo and Maria G. Manzo
San Mateo Property Owner & Housing Provider

733 of 891

oh Outlook
Tenant Protections

Fern ae

Date Fri 4/18/2025 2:30 PM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Council,

This letter is to express my opposition to tenant protection policies you are proposing. Here's
why:

1. The people of San Mateo have already expressed their opposition to expansive rent
control beyond State Rent Control AB 1482. 60% of San Mateo voters voted against
Measure Q rent control several years ago and 62% recently voted against State Rent Control
eposmon 33. Are you just not listening/remembering or are you marching to your own
drummer‘?

2. What is the motive for setting up a rental registry and charging landlords for setting up
a bureaucracy to implement it? The obvious one is to install punitive/restrictive rent price
caps beyond the AB1482. All this will reduce the landlord’s income even more. If this is not the
motive, then there are many other free sources to obtain rental information without setting up a
landlord funded bureaucracy.

Nearly 46% of rental housing units are small rental properties of 1-4 units. Over 70% of these
are owned by individuals and managed by the same owners. These small Mom and Pops rely
on this investment not only for current income, but also like a 401K, for current or future
retirement income. They are entitled to make a reasonable profit for hard work to help them get
there. If they don’t, properties will be sold, taken off the rental market, limited new rentals added
and money put into other more profitable investments.

Mom and Pops are already facing challenging maintenance costs. The average home in San
Mateo was built in 1965. 48% of renter-occupied units are 55-95 years old. These units are
increasingly in need of costly maintenance/replacements and exacerbated with high inflation
rates and maintenance costs that continue to exceed that. Costs are becoming very significant
such as conversion from gas to electric appliances, energy efficient windows, roofs, plumbing,
sewer pipes, foundations, fences, concrete/hardscape, furnaces, electrical wiring/panels,
painting, insulation etc. added to property taxes and mortgage costs. Also, the wild fire crisis in
California is causing rental property insurance costs to escalate for all areas statewide to make
up for high localized property loses.

737 of 891


--- page 738 ---
3. The rental registry is a bad idea from so many points of view besides the financial
burden talked about in #2. Sharing rental information can raise privacy issues for both tenant
and landlord. This information could drive up prices if landlords see neighboring units paying
higher prices.

It’s going to be an added administrative burden upon landlords along with all the other State
laws that require many pages of documentation. Some landlords may not comply, unaware,
forget, leading to incomplete data. If the number of rental laws get too onerous, landlords will
become so overwhelmed with the administrative burden that they will sell and exit the rental
market and thereby avoid adding new rental properties. Trying to enforce this will be very
difficult for the city. Simple solutions to complicated problems never work.

Respectfully,

Gary lsoardi

Tenant Proposals

For

Date Mon 4/21/2025 8:05 AM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

| am a small Housing Provider (1-4 units) and | oppose the proposed rental restrictions:

San Mateo Voters Have Already Spoken

The people of San Mateo have already spoken against expansive rental restrictions beyond AB1482
with measure Q several years ago with a 60% vote against and recently state proposition 33 with a
62% vote against.

Small Housing Providers Represent A Significant Share of Rental Units
46% of rental units are small Housing Providers, ma and pops
Over 70% of those are managed by small Housing Providers

Rental Buildings Are Aging Significantly
Average house in San Mateo was built in 1965.
48% of San Mateo renter-occupied units are 55-95 years old

Aging Rental Buildings Are Causing Significant Maintenace Costs

Because of the aging, maintenance costs are increasing significantly- Gas to electric appliances,
sewer laterals, electrical panel upgrades, roofs, galvanized to copper plumbing, foundations,
earthquake retrofit, hardscape, energy efficient windows, furnaces etc. And maintenance costs are
far exceeding the already high inflation rate. Added to that are escalating statewide insurance costs
due to wild fire crisis plus mortgage and property taxes.

Rental Housing Proposals will worsen Negative Cash Flow

Alot of small Housing Providers already face negative cash flow (i.e. expenses exceed rents) fora
long time until it turns positive. And Small Housing Providers hope it will turn positive before
retirement. Rentals are like a retirement 401K plan.

Rent Registry Will Morph Into Punitive Rent Restrictions
The rent registry can only be looked at as an eventual punitive rent reduction bureaucracy with
added Housing Provider costs and greater reduction of income for landlords.

Financial Instability, Over Regulation Will Cause Reduction in Rental Inventory

What all this means is financial instability for small Housing Providers. It will also force rental unit
sales, discourage new rental purchases, encourage investment in better financial opportunities and
reduce rental inventory. Added discouragement will be the burden of the numerous state and local
laws and regulations that will become too onerous to deal with for the small Housing Provider.

There Are Other Options

739 of 891

The city should redirect its focus to supporting programs that provide rental assistance assistance
through community funded solutions and invest in robust outreach that educate renters and housing
providers on the laws already available for land-lord tenant issues.

but it does not apply there. Is this some backroom dealing going on to get these proposals
implemented?

Item #10 — No New Laws!

Date Mon 4/21/2025 2:30 PM
To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Clerk McTaggart,

As a small business owner, this is an attack on my livelihood. This is NOT San Francisco, and is a much smaller
city. Please do not force these requirements on small business owners like me.

| am disappointed that city staff is fast-tracking a series of new laws to regulate privately owned rental housing in
the city.

| provide a much-needed resource in your city — rental housing. Yet, my voice is secondary to the voices of the
activists, amplified by city staff, who are demanding new laws.

City staffs report highlights that the No. 1 cause of renter displacement is nonpayment of rent. Your focus should
be directed toward programs and public funding that would help renters who face economic emergencies — not
the new regulations staff is promoting.

Please recognize that housing providers experience economic challenges as well. It is disheartening that staff's
report does not acknowledge the hardships housing providers also endure. Whether they are financial, regulatory
or legal hardships, we too need assistance from the city and do not need new punitive laws imposed on us.

We went through hardships during the COVID-19 pandemic, and not once did the city ask if we needed help.

| ask that you direct city staff to be fair and take a neutral approach. Do not proceed with new regulations until you
assess what problem you are trying to solve and evaluate what existing laws can be used to address those
concerns without adopting new laws that will continue to make it harder and costlier for us to provide rental housing
to the city’s renters.

Sincerely,

J. Ga

City of San Mateo Council Members Concerning New Tenant-Landlord Laws

Dear City Council Members,

lam writing to express my deep concern regarding the recently proposed tenant-Landlord
Laws that are under review by the city council. As an owner and manager of rental property
in San Mateo County for almost 30 years, and as a former long-term resident, third
generation, | feel compelled to share my perspective on how these laws could impact both
tenants and landlords.

lam against these new tenant landlord proposals:

Proposals included but not limited to:

Applying “just cause” provisions starting on Day 1 of tenancy instead of after year 1
Increasing relocation payment of no-fault terminations

Requiring housing procedures to submit rent roll data through a rent registry

The new regulations propose harsher control and greater oversight of rental agreements.
While | understand the intention behind these measures is to protect tenants from unfair
practices, itis crucial to consider the ramifications for landlords as well. Many landlords,
as |, are small-scale property owners who rely on rental income to support their families
and maintain their properties. Imposing excessive regulations could discourage them, as
with me, from renting out their properties, potentially reducing the availability of housing
and driving up rental prices. Overall, good tenants lose good housing tailored to their
needs. No one wants to live in a “beehive” apartment complex full of unknowns, as in the
newly built “stack um and pack um” apartments littered along the freeway. | get applicants
coming from these apartments for my rental homes.

AB-1482 was not a necessary law. Good tenants are in demand, long term tenants were
always the goal for landlords. Rental housing is self-regulating by prospective tenants. Ifa
landlord does not maintain their rental, stay in competition with other high demand rentals,
or the rents are too high, good tenants will find other rentals -which defeats the overall goal
of the landlord. The one-year time frame for this law to be imposed after the tenant moves
in is not long enough. One year is not an investment for a tenant, | have had one family stay
up to 15 years — that is an investment worth allowing them one month’s free rent if there is a
no-fault eviction - which | have not had to do to date.

Proposing AB-1482 to begin at the start of a tenancy has no justification:

Page 1- OS r1g jorne \

--- page 744 ---
1. It appears that this law is geared towards companies with hundreds of units. The
owner of a handful of properties, such as myself, has much greater consequences
for each property than a corporation with hundreds of units scattered across several
states and /or cities within California. A single bad tenant can bankrupt a small
landlord who doesn’t have the legal staff, does maintenance on their own as
opposed to live in handymen such as in large apartments, and (for landlords | am
friends with, and including myself) might be facing age-related health issues which
appear out of nowhere. We need the longer time frame in case of emergencies,
other contingencies which require us to put our rental up for sale or do major repairs

that were unplanned.

2. The start of AB 1482 at the beginning of a tenancy does not allow the landlord the
opportunity to find out if a tenant is honorable or not.

The Bay Area is the most desirable place to live in CA. Many homes are up to if not more
than $1 million investments. Landlords have mortgages, such as us, we entrust our
personal property to complete strangers. One year is not enough time to truly get to know
tenants. We landlords need at the least one full year (actually more like three years) to
ensure that the tenant is not taking advantage of us —- such as using our rentals to establish
an AIR BNB and other unauthorized subleasing activities; also potential illegal activities —
sex trafficking, drug dealing, prostitution and so forth. Landlords must the opportunity to
come in and inspect the unit to make sure it’s being care for the way the rental contract
specifies. If this law is imposed on day one, it gives us no opportunity to ensure our
investment is secured since landlords are not allowed or discouraged to enter the unit
unless the tenant complains about a needed repair. We can enter to do maintenance, but
that is usually one year after the tenant moves in (such as replacing smoke detector /CO2
batteries), but by then, the unit could be trashed. Imposing AB 1482 at the start of a
tenancy will drive more landlords out of the rental business and cause real estate business
to stagnate. Large investors have already stopped building due to the insecurity as well as
the cyclical nature of funding for these developments. | have seen new apartments built
and not filled to capacity to date.

It does not seem fair to allow a tenant to move in, and if there is an unforeseen event which
requires them to move, they should not be allowed 2-3 months’ worth of rent to enrich
themselves at the owner’s expense. | invested over $100,000 to $150,000 to re-renovate my

The proposal for a rental registry seems to be focused on:

Te

Policing landlords. Where does the city get the authority to do this? Whoever
oversees this, needs to submit their data summarizing it to the Federal agency —
HUD, and Senators. Policing landlords means they will draft policies against
landlords. If there is no “tenant registry” then this is purely one sided, biased against
landlords.

Entrapment. No wiggle room for landlords. They will be subject to legal challenges,
no free legal help to landlords. Is there a legal requirement for this rental registry?

The government has declared us landlords as having a cash producing asset rather
than just a home and we are expected to pay taxes on revenue, but what about the
tenant who illegally subleases a rental without the consent or knowledge of the
landlord? Are these tenants required to pay a fine? Register despite violating rental
agreements? Penalized for not paying taxes on the income from our rentals? Give
proceeds to landlords if caught subleasing and making a profit? Remember: cities
are businesses that require revenue to stay profitable. But it can not keep penalizing
landlords. The rental business is drying up because of this. We need a tenant
registry to capture lost revenue dollars when tenants sublease, and assist in
preserving our investments by ensuring rules, codes, and requirements are being
followed for the safety and protection for all concerned.

This proposed law is trying to determine my profit margin without considering my
risks and costs of operation. It does not consider the amount of profit redistributed
back into my rental property to make them modern and desirable by today’s
standard, nor the cost for maintenance and repairs. It does not consider what a
contractor will charge me vs another landlord, there is no consistency, and | seem to

perhaps | will be required to change my approach and do nothing — leaving the unit

From is Leung re

Dear City Clerk McTaggart,

| am disappointed that city staff is fast-tracking a series of new laws to regulate privately owned rental housing in
the city.

| provide a much-needed resource in your city — rental housing. Yet, my voice is secondary to the voices of the
activists, amplified by city staff, who are demanding new laws.

City staffs report highlights that the No. 1 cause of renter displacement is nonpayment of rent. Your focus should
be directed toward programs and public funding that would help renters who face economic emergencies — not
the new regulations staff is promoting.

Please recognize that housing providers experience economic challenges as well. It is disheartening that staff's
report does not acknowledge the hardships housing providers also endure. Whether they are financial, regulatory
or legal hardships, we too need assistance from the city and do not need new punitive laws imposed on us.

We went through hardships during the COVID-19 pandemic, and not once did the city ask if we needed help.

| ask that you direct city staff to be fair and take a neutral approach. Do not proceed with new regulations until you
assess what problem you are trying to solve and evaluate what existing laws can be used to address those
concerns without adopting new laws that will continue to make it harder and costlier for us to provide rental housing
to the city’s renters.

Sincerely,

Lisa Leung

Item #10 — Opposed to Introduction of New Laws

via votervoice.ne

Date Fri 4/18/2025 5:10 PM
To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Clerk McTaggart,

Please keep in mind that beyond introducing complications for non-corporate owners, these proposed regulations
will have one or both of the following outcomes across our community:

1) Reduction in rental unit quality as less is invested into upkeep and improvements.

2) Inflated rents for incoming tenants to offset new costs, rent limitations, and risk of unexpected payments.

The best policy is to support the sensible development of new housing to increase supply. This solution prevents
unsustainable regulation upon local owners while providing renters with more options. With more units available,
landlords must compete to provide the best quality while keeping rents within reason as demand is lowered. This
environment is tenant-friendly without asking owners to risk the financial viability of their properties when
maintaining, improving, and leasing living spaces.

Thank you for you consideration.

Sincerely,

Kyle McCreight

761 of 891

Oh Outlook

Adopt "One Home San Mateo" eviction policy proposals

From Acheigh vers

Date Fri 4/18/2025 3:31 AM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

April 18, 2025

San Mateo City Councilmembers:

San Mateo County Democracy for America, a chartered Democratic club, commends the San Mateo City
Council’s commitment to finding ways to expand renter protections in San Mateo, in order to provide
stable and secure housing in times that are increasingly insecure.

Because renters are so vulnerable to the traumatic life-changing effects of unjust evictions, we urge you
to adopt the five eviction protection policy proposals from the “One Home San Mateo” coalition* at your
San Mateo City Council meeting on Monday, April 21.

The “One Home San Mateo” proposals:

1. Just cause for evictions from Day One

2. Prohibition of no-fault evictions during the school year
3. Right to return after a substantial remodel

4. Increased relocation assistance

5. Creation of a rental data registry

* “One Home San Mateo” coalition members:

Housing Leadership Council

Peninsula Solidarity Cohort

San Mateo Climate Action Team

The Latino Ministry of the Congregational Church of San Mateo
Peninsula for Everyone

Housing Action Coalition

The League of Women Voters of North & Central San Mateo County
United Way Bay Area

Again, thank you for your leadership on behalf of renters in San Mateo.

Sincerely,

Susie Raye
Chair
San Mateo County Democracy for America

From: Bill McQuaid

Subject: Expansion of Rent Control

My wife and | own a five unit apartment building in San Mateo.

We are opposed to any expansion of rent control in San Mateo.

We don't need more bureaucracy in the form of a rental registry and other onerous regulations.
.The state’s Tenant Protection Act of 2019 already provides comprehensive rent control and
eviction protections. The more regulations, the more likely "mom and pop" landlords like us, will
"throw in the towel" and sell out to a corporate type entity that can

afford legal counsel to navigate and decipher the more and more

complex nature of owning a small property.

If the desire of the City of San Mateo is to encourage the corporate

ownership of local real estate, more rent control and tenant eviction

protections is certainly a step in the right direction.

Bill and Maureen McQuaid

Rent Control Special Study Session 4/21/25

Date Thu 4/17/2025 4:06 PM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Council Members,
| am firmly against expanding AB1482.
Good tenants have adequate protection.

Expanding the protections will only encourage bad tenants and their attorneys to exploit the legal
process further. The courts already favor the tenants and have burdened good landlords with false
habitability claims, groundless retaliatory claims, etc. These types of cases are brought by predatory
law firms that game the system with bad tenants encouraged to play the game. Insurance companies
and landlords are being exploited with false claims due to added rent control and tenant protections.

What we need is more affordable rental housing, market-rate apartment buildings, etc.
Expanding AB1482 will discourage further future rental housing development.

| have been in the real estate business since 1978, | am a landlord in San Mateo, Alameda County, and
San Francisco. | currently deal with San Francisco's Rent Board that added a new rental registry which
adds to the yearly reporting and additional fees that all landlords have to incur.

| am 100 percent against adding any registry, as Landlords are already burdened with filing
requirements, rising insurance rates, rising maintenance costs due to rising labor and material costs.
These added requirements make any future and current landlord think twice about having a future as
a rental provider.

Rising operating expenses make rent control properties very hard to properly maintain and have
forced numerous mom-and-pop operators to sell out.

Property turnovers have led to larger rent increases due to higher maintenance costs and renovations
by investment groups with deep pockets.

| have numerous long-term tenants in San Mateo that have not seen any rent increases from me for
over 5 years.

| just recently had to send out a modest rent increase to one of my tenants due to the possibility of
added rent control concerns and the rent is still 15 to 20 percent below market.

No one understands how rent control hurts both landlords and tenants alike.

pages.

Gh Outlook

RE: New Business Item #10 - Tenant Protection

Date Mon 4/21/2025 9:54 AM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>

4/21/25

TO: City of San Mateo, Council and Staff
FROM: Suzanne Moore

RE: New Business Item #10 - Tenant Protection

Honorable Council members and staff.
Thank you for prioritizing tenant protection as part of your Housing Element.

My name is Suzanne Moore, sitting Board Member for Healthcare for the Homeless and Farmworkers
Program, and | speak for myself this evening.

Eviction remains one of the greatest barriers to finding another home; therefore, preventing evictions
is an obvious way to best assist tenants and interrupt the homelessness pipeline.

Here are how some communities chose to decrease evictions: they adopted ordinances

- that extend just cause for eviction from day one,

- ban junk fees in leases and halt THAT path to eviction for failure to pay,

- require landlords to provide access to tenant legal aid as they serve an eviction. Tenants who have an
attorney present in eviction court are more likely to stay housed.

I'm grateful to landlords who provide safe, affordable housing for our neighbors. Our tenants are an
important part of our community. We, as a community, can adopt ordinances that guide and protect.

Thank you.

Suzanne Moore

-----Original Message---—

From: Doris Nishimoto

Sent: Monday, April 21, 2025 12:52 PM

To: Rob Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Lisa Diaz Nash <Idiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Adam
Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>; Nicole Fernandez <nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>; Danielle Cwirko-
Godycki <dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org>; Prasanna Rasiah <prasiah@cityofsanmateo.org>; Rachel Horst
<rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; Noel Nish

Subject: On Further Tenant Protections

Policy induced methods to hold landlords hostage to the situational distress of a breach of contract between
landlord and tenant is not the solution.

The "off ramp" to this problem is recognizing and addressing the deficiencies between eamed income and the
need to deregulate an environment that has ADDED costs to provider housing. Please, no more added costs of
rent registries and creation of a new department to monitor the housing market in the private market. Instead, this
money could be used as rental subsidies and would have a direct and meaningful impact for tenants.

The current rental security deposit that is allowed is insufficient to cover "wear and tear" tumaround costs for an
apartment and also for the proposed "move out" (up to 3 months rent) you are considering for tenants due to AB12
bill:

In California, Assembly Bill 12 (AB 12), which went into effect on July 1, 2024, limits security deposits for most
landlords to one month's rent, regardless of whether the unit is fumished or unfurnished. This law

amends California Civil Code Section 1950.5. There are exceptions, including for smaller landlords (those who own
two or fewer properties with a total of four or fewer rental units) who may still be able to collect up to two months'
rent.

Thank you for reading.

Doris Nishimoto, D.D.S.

Housing Element Implementation — Anti-Displacement and Tenant Protection Policies

From Seema Pot! {i

Date Mon 4/21/2025 3:45 PM
To Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>

Hello City Councilmembers!

| currently serve as the Chair of the San Mateo Planning Commission but am writing on my own behalf as a resident of San Mateo.

In its certified Housing Element San Mateo commits to a Fair Housing Action Plan in order to Affirmatively Further Fair Housing as required by state law. The
actions in this plan include:

Policy H5.2.4: In conjunction with San Mateo County, develop a housing
mobility program that provides support to tenants seeking to move to high opportunity areas in the region. (No timeline given)

Action 4.1: Establish tenant protections in local ordinance to extend
measures of AB1482 related to relocation, documentation, and right to return policy in eviction cases.

Extend AB1482 provisions to require tenant relocation payments for
No Fault evictions for those with tenure less than one year. (Target timeline 2024)

Make recommendations to the City Council for establishing tenant

protection policies that include the requirement of documentation from landlords who use the substantial remodel exemption to evict tenants and a Rig
2026)

Amend the Code to strengthen enforcement penalty structure to aid
in protecting tenants from unsafe or substandard units. (Target timeline of 2024)

Action 4.2: Partner with Project Sentinel to perform fair housing
training for landlords and tenants. (Timeline Annual by July 1)

The
city also commits to Policy H 3.6, Monitor Rents, Evictions and Tenant Displacement Citywide, including:

a.

Research options and best practices for

a rental registry list, including determining necessary financial and human resources needed to establish such a program. Make recommendations to
City Council. (Target timeline 2024)

Collaborate with regional partners, such
as 21 Elements or ABAG, to develop ways to monitor rental rates, evictions and displacement citywide and countywide to track trends and use the
information to guide policy priorities. (Target timeline 2024-2031)

| encourage the City Council to follow through with these commitments to ensure that our Housing Element remains in compliance with the state, so that we
do not risk decertification and exposure to penalties like the Builder's Remedy.

Thank you for your consideration.

From Jim Baba <a
Date Sun 4/20/2025 9:00 AM
To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Clerk McTaggart,

Dear Mayor, Deputy Mayor, and Council Members:

As a housing provider it has been most challenging the last 5 years. Costs have gone up tremendously, let alone
my insurance cost which has increased 2.5 times over the past year. I'm just now getting my rents back to pre-
Covid levels, as | reduced rents during that time for my current tenants to align with the rental rate of new tenants
coming in. During this time my costs have skyrocketed. We already have plenty of state and city laws on behalf of
tenants, and do not need to further burden housing providers. Furthermore, after reading the Letter from Your City
Manager, | learned that San Mateo faces a structural budget deficit. Creating a rent registry would be costly, and
there are concems over privacy.

Gh Outlook

Vote NO on AB 1157

From Zarine Batliwalla ee

Date Fri 4/18/2025 8:56 AM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Voters and economists alike have warned about the dangers of rent control — and voters have
rejected it at the ballot box again and again. Propositions 10, 21, and 33 were all defeated by more
than 20 percentage points.

Now AB 1157 ignores the will of the voters and threatens to drive even more rental housing out of the
market. Slashing the rent cap to just 2% plus inflation will force many small property owners to sell or
convert their properties, displacing tenants and worsening the housing shortage.

Rental housing providers didn't create California's housing crisis. Penalizing them with punitive rent
caps only makes the situation worse.

Research from institutions like Stanford University and the California Legislative Analyst's Office has
shown that strict rent control reduces housing availability, discourages new construction, and

ultimately harms renters.

Please vote No on AB 1157.

photo Zarine Batliwalla

w.photo

Letter to City Council

From Rustard Mustard <r

Date Mon 4/21/2025 9:01 AM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>

Cc Nicole Fernandez <nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>; Lisa Diaz Nash <Idiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Rob
Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Adam Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org >

Dear Honorable Mayor and City Council,

In my professional life, I have worked 1 on 1 with individuals and families who were
physically and emotionally damaged by living in squalor in San Mateo. I have seen
conditions that you would never, ever allow yourself to live in. I have seen children
covered with bedbug sores on their entire bodies, homes that were infested with
mold on large sections of multiple rooms, ceilings with holes and water dripping,
stoves that didn't properly work, rodent droppings and so forth.

Over the years while my husband was serving as the Mayor and Council member, I
accompanied him when he got calls about Renter's needs.

If you think there is no need for Renter Protections at this time, then I have some

places for you to see and people to meet. I invite you to tour the reality and I

will be your tour guide.

I know a lot of landlords who are good people. They have nothing to worry about.

It is the ones who do nothing for their properties and tenants that need to be
regulated.

779 of 891


--- page 780 ---
April 21, 2025
San Mateo City Council
San Mateo City Hall
330 West 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403
Re: Anti-Displacement and Tenant Protection Policy Proposals - Housing Leadership Council of
San Mateo County
Dear Members of the San Mateo City Council,
The Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County (HLC) works with communities and their leaders
to produce and preserve quality affordable homes.
On behalf of HLC, I am writing to encourage you to adopt the policy changes set forth by our partners at
“Our Home San Mateo.” These changes are focused on preventing evictions and helping to keep your
San Mateo renters in their homes.
Eviction is so much more than a forced move. It can be a life-altering event with serious and lasting
impacts on families—undermining their economic security, mental and physical health, and overall
well-being. For community members with limited resources, the costs of eviction can push them over a
financial cliff.
Eviction can be especially traumatizing for children. Often, when families are forced to move, children
abruptly lose their connections to neighborhood, church, and school. Their entire support system
collapses. Research shows eviction is linked to declines in academic performance and an increased risk
of involvement in the juvenile justice system.
In the interest of shielding families from the negative effects of eviction, HLC would like to encourage
you to adopt the following “Our Home San Mateo” anti-displacement and tenant protection policy
proposals:
1. Just cause for eviction from Day One
2. Prohibition of no-fault evictions during the school year
3. Right to return after a substantial remodel
4. Increased relocation assistance
5. Creation of a rental data registry
780 of 891

--- page 781 ---
You have an opportunity to create a meaningful, lasting impact on the lives of your community members.
By adopting the proposed measures, you will be taking clear, tangible steps to protect San Mateo
families from the devastating effects of eviction and foster greater stability in your community. We urge
you to support these vital protections for your community members.
Sincerely,
Ken Chan
Senior Organizer
Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County
Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County
2905 S. El Camino Real, San Mateo, CA 94403 • (650) 242-1764 • hlcsmc.org

Oh Outlook

Opposing added Rent Control in San Mateo

Date Mon 4/21/2025 3:09 PM

To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; akohjikian@cityofsanmateo.org
<akohjikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; City Attorney's Office <CityAttorneysOffice @cityofsanmateo.org>; Rachel
Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>

To:

Citv

Manager Alex Khojikian,
City

Attorney Prasanna Rasiah,
Housing

Manager Rachel Horst, and
City

Council members Rob Newsom (Mayor),
Lisa

Diaz Nash,

Adam

Lorraine,

Nicole

Fernandez

Gentlepersons:

Further rent control measures are not necessary in San Mateo. We have a state law for rent
control and an online calculator showing the rate at which

rent can be increased in each of California's 58 counties. It ls County Specific. There is a
maximum of 10% regardless of the cost of living and the allowable rate has hovered around
8%.

The cost of doing business has increased phenomenally, due for the most part to expenses for
which we have no control. All of the utilities have asked for and been granted rate increases.
PGE, has asked for and been granted rate hikes in one year, Recology has just about doubled
in 10 years, now it's $42 or $47 per can in South San Francisco, Comcast has just raised it's
business and residential rates. The water department - the costs for waste water now exceed
the cost for clean water. San Bruno stopped including this fee on the water invoice and now
includes it on the tax bill. There is that 10% cap on writing off taxes paid. Another loss.

There is also the topic of maintenance and repairs and permits for same. The costs for
plumbers, painters, electricians. has gone way up. Costs increased. If the average cost paid
before was $300-$500, now | have found that it's $700-$1000.

Also, new fees and license requirements have been implemented around the state. South San
Francisco now requires rental providers to obtain a business license. That's another $300+

Then we can look at tenants, payment of rent, security deposits and damages to the property.
Security deposits are now capped at the equivalent of one month's rent. So if you have an
apartment that you rent for $2,000 and the tenant backs into your garage door - that can cost
you $1000 if it's a partial repair. You repair the garage door because, aside from the esthetics,

786 of 891


--- page 787 ---
gaps will allow rodents to enter and that will lead to the health department and/or Vector control.
Then the tenant rolls into it again, another $1000 - you've maxed the security deposit.

By the way, the tenant rolls into the door because, contrary to the lease (so written to prevent
precisely this), parks right up to the door.

You can't ask them to replenish the deposit if you'd raised the rent in the same year because
you'd then be exceeding the maximum annual percentage for an increase. If nothing else, you
could end up in court to "discuss".

This was not a hypothetical!

A property owner can live with the state mandated rent control in it's present form. Implementing
rent control as outlined, you are facilitating the demise of the small property owner. A property
owner cannot be expected to subsidize California's renters, and yet, that is the direction to
which property owners are being forcibly herded.

The reality is that costs everywhere have skyrocketed. Measures like these should be fairly
considered. You Must Consider Both Sides.

The tenant is not always right. You cannot lump all tenants or all property owners in the same
box.

Just like many other issues raised in your meetings there are already measures in place to
address bad behavior whether on the part of the landlord or the tenant.

Adding more controls to rentals in San Mateo will create more and unnecessary problems and
delays.

Please do not implement San Mateo specific rent control.

Rita Fontana

787 of 891


--- page 788 ---
City of San Mateo Council Members: Concerning New Tenant Landlord Laws

Council members, | have reviewed proposed changes affecting my rentals in California.

| am opposed to the proposals; they will negatively affect the business.

| believe these changes are hostile, poorly thought out and lack understanding of the basic

business principles, chiefly economic reality.

In my opinion, the rental registry is the lesser of the concerns because if the changes
in “just cause” and additional “relocation” proposal pass a large number of rentals
will become economically unsustainable and the rental registry will be a colossal

failure.

Let’s look at the basic balance sheet for the average landlord. The cashflow probably
looks great to most folks but the net profit is an entirely different story.

Income: Monthly rental charges. Full tenancy for a year is not realistic, there are repairs to
be made after a tenant leaves.

Costs: Property Tax, Property Insurance (if you can acquire it), painting costs after a tenant
moves out, State of California Tax, Federal Tax, Gardening fees, legal expenses, CPA costs,
repair and cleanup losses can often be greater than the rental deposit allowed by AB 1482

and nearly impossible to collect on.

Considerations:

1. The life expectancy for most appliances is less than 8 years.
2. Property insurance in California is becoming increasingly more expensive if

available.
3. Inthe past 4 years nationally the inflation rate exceeded 22% based on the Bureau

of Labor Statistics, the Bay Area exceeds this figure.
4. Many landlords use rental income to augment their retirement income and already

face aconomic/health challenges.

788 of 891


--- page 789 ---
5. Some tenants will sublet without the landlord’s permission and pocket the
undeclared income.

6. Belongings let behind cause large losses for the landlord.

7. Anon-contested eviction generally takes about 45 days, while a contested one
around 60-75.

8. The rental registry will add costs to the landlord and likely be passed along to the
tenant if they are excessive. This includes inspections which are difficult if not
impossible under AB 1482.

9. The rental registry will likely end up in litigation due to shortcomings/data risks for

the landlord and tenants.
10. Data collected by the rental registry will be shared with Federal agencies.

Gary Laughlin

FW: Subject: Opposition to Proposed Rent Control Regulations — Study Session April 21, 2025

From Rachel Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org >
Date Thu 4/17/2025 3:52 PM
To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Please see below.

From: Omar Galinlo [rrr

Sent: Thursday, April 17, 2025 3:36 PM

To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil @cityofsanmateo.org>; akohjikian@cityofsanmateo.org; City Attorney's

Office <CityAttorneysOffice @cityofsanmateo.org>; Rachel Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>

Cc: Maria Manzo Sa
Subject: Subject: Opposition to Proposed Rent Control Regulations — Study Session April 21, 2025

Omar Galindo

April 17, 2025

San Mateo City Council
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403

Subject: Opposition to Proposed Rent Control Regulations — Study Session April 21,
2025

Dear Honorable Mayor and City Council Members,

As a rental housing provider and long-time member of the San Mateo community, | am writing
to express my strong opposition to the proposed changes to the City’s rental housing
regulations, scheduled for review during the upcoming study session on Monday, April 21, 2025.

While | understand the Council's intent to address housing concerns and tenant protections, the
proposed amendments—particularly the immediate application of “just cause” protections from
the start of a tenancy, the increase in relocation assistance for no-fault terminations, and the
implementation of a rent registry—present serious concerns for local property owners like
myself.

| currently own and manage the following rental properties in San Mateo:

1. Immediate Just Cause Protections:
Under California law (AB 1482), the 12-month period before “just cause” protections apply

790 of 891


--- page 791 ---
allows landlords to make informed decisions about tenants during a reasonable trial period.
Eliminating this window removes a critical tool for property owners to ensure tenants are a good
fit, and may discourage future investment in rental housing within San Mateo.

2. Increased Relocation Assistance:

While | support fair and reasonable relocation compensation in genuine no-fault circumstances,
raising these requirements substantially above state mandates creates additional financial
burdens on landlords, particularly small-scale providers. These policies can lead to difficult and
unintended consequences, such as deferred maintenance or reduced housing availability,
which ultimately harm both landlords and tenants.

3. Rent Registry Requirements:

Mandatory submission of rent roll data introduces ongoing administrative obligations that are
both intrusive and unnecessary. Most landlords are already in compliance with applicable state
law and local codes. Arent registry adds complexity without offering meaningful insight or
benefit, especially for mom-and-pop landlords already stretched thin by compliance and
maintenance costs.

These proposals signal a shift toward overly punitive policies that disregard the challenges
faced by responsible landlords. As a member of the rental housing community, | take pride in
maintaining my properties and treating tenants with fairness and respect. | urge the City Council
to reconsider this approach and work collaboratively with property owners to create policies that
balance tenant protections with the viability and sustainability of rental housing providers.

Thank you for your attention to this matter. | respectfully ask you to oppose the adoption of
these regulations and instead pursue balanced solutions that encourage housing investment
while supporting tenants.

Sincerely,

Omar Galindo and Maria G. Manzo
San Mateo Property Owner & Housing Provider

791 of 891

--- page 795 ---
oh Outlook
Tenant Protections

Fern ae

Date Fri 4/18/2025 2:30 PM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Council,

This letter is to express my opposition to tenant protection policies you are proposing. Here's
why:

1. The people of San Mateo have already expressed their opposition to expansive rent
control beyond State Rent Control AB 1482. 60% of San Mateo voters voted against
Measure Q rent control several years ago and 62% recently voted against State Rent Control
eposmon 33. Are you just not listening/remembering or are you marching to your own
drummer‘?

2. What is the motive for setting up a rental registry and charging landlords for setting up
a bureaucracy to implement it? The obvious one is to install punitive/restrictive rent price
caps beyond the AB1482. All this will reduce the landlord’s income even more. If this is not the
motive, then there are many other free sources to obtain rental information without setting up a
landlord funded bureaucracy.

Nearly 46% of rental housing units are small rental properties of 1-4 units. Over 70% of these
are owned by individuals and managed by the same owners. These small Mom and Pops rely
on this investment not only for current income, but also like a 401K, for current or future
retirement income. They are entitled to make a reasonable profit for hard work to help them get
there. If they don’t, properties will be sold, taken off the rental market, limited new rentals added
and money put into other more profitable investments.

Mom and Pops are already facing challenging maintenance costs. The average home in San
Mateo was built in 1965. 48% of renter-occupied units are 55-95 years old. These units are
increasingly in need of costly maintenance/replacements and exacerbated with high inflation
rates and maintenance costs that continue to exceed that. Costs are becoming very significant
such as conversion from gas to electric appliances, energy efficient windows, roofs, plumbing,
sewer pipes, foundations, fences, concrete/hardscape, furnaces, electrical wiring/panels,
painting, insulation etc. added to property taxes and mortgage costs. Also, the wild fire crisis in
California is causing rental property insurance costs to escalate for all areas statewide to make
up for high localized property loses.

795 of 891


--- page 796 ---
3. The rental registry is a bad idea from so many points of view besides the financial
burden talked about in #2. Sharing rental information can raise privacy issues for both tenant
and landlord. This information could drive up prices if landlords see neighboring units paying
higher prices.

It’s going to be an added administrative burden upon landlords along with all the other State
laws that require many pages of documentation. Some landlords may not comply, unaware,
forget, leading to incomplete data. If the number of rental laws get too onerous, landlords will
become so overwhelmed with the administrative burden that they will sell and exit the rental
market and thereby avoid adding new rental properties. Trying to enforce this will be very
difficult for the city. Simple solutions to complicated problems never work.

Respectfully,

Gary lsoardi

ch Outlook
Tenant Proposals

For

Date Mon 4/21/2025 8:05 AM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

| am a small Housing Provider (1-4 units) and | oppose the proposed rental restrictions:

San Mateo Voters Have Already Spoken

The people of San Mateo have already spoken against expansive rental restrictions beyond AB1482
with measure Q several years ago with a 60% vote against and recently state proposition 33 with a
62% vote against.

Small Housing Providers Represent A Significant Share of Rental Units
46% of rental units are small Housing Providers, ma and pops
Over 70% of those are managed by small Housing Providers

Rental Buildings Are Aging Significantly
Average house in San Mateo was built in 1965.
48% of San Mateo renter-occupied units are 55-95 years old

Aging Rental Buildings Are Causing Significant Maintenace Costs

Because of the aging, maintenance costs are increasing significantly- Gas to electric appliances,
sewer laterals, electrical panel upgrades, roofs, galvanized to copper plumbing, foundations,
earthquake retrofit, hardscape, energy efficient windows, furnaces etc. And maintenance costs are
far exceeding the already high inflation rate. Added to that are escalating statewide insurance costs
due to wild fire crisis plus mortgage and property taxes.

Rental Housing Proposals will worsen Negative Cash Flow

Alot of small Housing Providers already face negative cash flow (i.e. expenses exceed rents) fora
long time until it turns positive. And Small Housing Providers hope it will turn positive before
retirement. Rentals are like a retirement 401K plan.

Rent Registry Will Morph Into Punitive Rent Restrictions
The rent registry can only be looked at as an eventual punitive rent reduction bureaucracy with
added Housing Provider costs and greater reduction of income for landlords.

Financial Instability, Over Regulation Will Cause Reduction in Rental Inventory

What all this means is financial instability for small Housing Providers. It will also force rental unit
sales, discourage new rental purchases, encourage investment in better financial opportunities and
reduce rental inventory. Added discouragement will be the burden of the numerous state and local
laws and regulations that will become too onerous to deal with for the small Housing Provider.

There Are Other Options

797 of 891


--- page 798 ---
The city should redirect its focus to supporting programs that provide rental assistance assistance
through community funded solutions and invest in robust outreach that educate renters and housing
providers on the laws already available for land-lord tenant issues.

Also, | hear these proposals might be included in the Housing Element which is a State mandate,
but it does not apply there. Is this some backroom dealing going on to get these proposals
implemented?

Respectfully

Gary Isoardi

Item #10 — No New Laws!

Date Mon 4/21/2025 2:30 PM
To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Clerk McTaggart,

As a small business owner, this is an attack on my livelihood. This is NOT San Francisco, and is a much smaller
city. Please do not force these requirements on small business owners like me.

| am disappointed that city staff is fast-tracking a series of new laws to regulate privately owned rental housing in
the city.

| provide a much-needed resource in your city — rental housing. Yet, my voice is secondary to the voices of the
activists, amplified by city staff, who are demanding new laws.

City staffs report highlights that the No. 1 cause of renter displacement is nonpayment of rent. Your focus should
be directed toward programs and public funding that would help renters who face economic emergencies — not
the new regulations staff is promoting.

Please recognize that housing providers experience economic challenges as well. It is disheartening that staff's
report does not acknowledge the hardships housing providers also endure. Whether they are financial, regulatory
or legal hardships, we too need assistance from the city and do not need new punitive laws imposed on us.

We went through hardships during the COVID-19 pandemic, and not once did the city ask if we needed help.

| ask that you direct city staff to be fair and take a neutral approach. Do not proceed with new regulations until you
assess what problem you are trying to solve and evaluate what existing laws can be used to address those
concerns without adopting new laws that will continue to make it harder and costlier for us to provide rental housing
to the city’s renters.

Sincerely,

J. Ga

--- page 801 ---
City of San Mateo Council Members Concerning New Tenant-Landlord Laws

Dear City Council Members,

lam writing to express my deep concern regarding the recently proposed tenant-Landlord
Laws that are under review by the city council. As an owner and manager of rental property
in San Mateo County for almost 30 years, and as a former long-term resident, third
generation, | feel compelled to share my perspective on how these laws could impact both
tenants and landlords.

lam against these new tenant landlord proposals:

Proposals included but not limited to:

Applying “just cause” provisions starting on Day 1 of tenancy instead of after year 1
Increasing relocation payment of no-fault terminations

Requiring housing procedures to submit rent roll data through a rent registry

The new regulations propose harsher control and greater oversight of rental agreements.
While | understand the intention behind these measures is to protect tenants from unfair
practices, itis crucial to consider the ramifications for landlords as well. Many landlords,
as |, are small-scale property owners who rely on rental income to support their families
and maintain their properties. Imposing excessive regulations could discourage them, as
with me, from renting out their properties, potentially reducing the availability of housing
and driving up rental prices. Overall, good tenants lose good housing tailored to their
needs. No one wants to live in a “beehive” apartment complex full of unknowns, as in the
newly built “stack um and pack um” apartments littered along the freeway. | get applicants
coming from these apartments for my rental homes.

AB-1482 was not a necessary law. Good tenants are in demand, long term tenants were
always the goal for landlords. Rental housing is self-regulating by prospective tenants. Ifa
landlord does not maintain their rental, stay in competition with other high demand rentals,
or the rents are too high, good tenants will find other rentals -which defeats the overall goal
of the landlord. The one-year time frame for this law to be imposed after the tenant moves
in is not long enough. One year is not an investment for a tenant, | have had one family stay
up to 15 years — that is an investment worth allowing them one month’s free rent if there is a
no-fault eviction - which | have not had to do to date.

Proposing AB-1482 to begin at the start of a tenancy has no justification:

Page 1- OS r1g jorne \

801 of 891


--- page 802 ---
1. It appears that this law is geared towards companies with hundreds of units. The
owner of a handful of properties, such as myself, has much greater consequences
for each property than a corporation with hundreds of units scattered across several
states and /or cities within California. A single bad tenant can bankrupt a small
landlord who doesn’t have the legal staff, does maintenance on their own as
opposed to live in handymen such as in large apartments, and (for landlords | am
friends with, and including myself) might be facing age-related health issues which
appear out of nowhere. We need the longer time frame in case of emergencies,
other contingencies which require us to put our rental up for sale or do major repairs

that were unplanned.

2. The start of AB 1482 at the beginning of a tenancy does not allow the landlord the
opportunity to find out if a tenant is honorable or not.

The Bay Area is the most desirable place to live in CA. Many homes are up to if not more
than $1 million investments. Landlords have mortgages, such as us, we entrust our
personal property to complete strangers. One year is not enough time to truly get to know
tenants. We landlords need at the least one full year (actually more like three years) to
ensure that the tenant is not taking advantage of us —- such as using our rentals to establish
an AIR BNB and other unauthorized subleasing activities; also potential illegal activities —
sex trafficking, drug dealing, prostitution and so forth. Landlords must the opportunity to
come in and inspect the unit to make sure it’s being care for the way the rental contract
specifies. If this law is imposed on day one, it gives us no opportunity to ensure our
investment is secured since landlords are not allowed or discouraged to enter the unit
unless the tenant complains about a needed repair. We can enter to do maintenance, but
that is usually one year after the tenant moves in (such as replacing smoke detector /CO2
batteries), but by then, the unit could be trashed. Imposing AB 1482 at the start of a
tenancy will drive more landlords out of the rental business and cause real estate business
to stagnate. Large investors have already stopped building due to the insecurity as well as
the cyclical nature of funding for these developments. | have seen new apartments built
and not filled to capacity to date.

It does not seem fair to allow a tenant to move in, and if there is an unforeseen event which
requires them to move, they should not be allowed 2-3 months’ worth of rent to enrich
themselves at the owner’s expense. | invested over $100,000 to $150,000 to re-renovate my

The proposal for a rental registry seems to be focused on:

Te

Policing landlords. Where does the city get the authority to do this? Whoever
oversees this, needs to submit their data summarizing it to the Federal agency —
HUD, and Senators. Policing landlords means they will draft policies against
landlords. If there is no “tenant registry” then this is purely one sided, biased against
landlords.

Entrapment. No wiggle room for landlords. They will be subject to legal challenges,
no free legal help to landlords. Is there a legal requirement for this rental registry?

The government has declared us landlords as having a cash producing asset rather
than just a home and we are expected to pay taxes on revenue, but what about the
tenant who illegally subleases a rental without the consent or knowledge of the
landlord? Are these tenants required to pay a fine? Register despite violating rental
agreements? Penalized for not paying taxes on the income from our rentals? Give
proceeds to landlords if caught subleasing and making a profit? Remember: cities
are businesses that require revenue to stay profitable. But it can not keep penalizing
landlords. The rental business is drying up because of this. We need a tenant
registry to capture lost revenue dollars when tenants sublease, and assist in
preserving our investments by ensuring rules, codes, and requirements are being
followed for the safety and protection for all concerned.

This proposed law is trying to determine my profit margin without considering my
risks and costs of operation. It does not consider the amount of profit redistributed
back into my rental property to make them modern and desirable by today’s
standard, nor the cost for maintenance and repairs. It does not consider what a
contractor will charge me vs another landlord, there is no consistency, and | seem to
get taken advantage of by unfamiliar contractors and businesses. The price to
replace my sewer laterals estimates from 3 different plumbing companies was:
$48,000.00; $58,000.00-$85,000.00 same company but different types of plumbing
methods; and $103,000.00. Realtors told me they paid $12,000.00 and it should not
cost that much.

perhaps | will be required to change my approach and do nothing — leaving the unit

knowledge due to the limitation created by law restricting their ability to inspect
their rental property during a tenant’s residency. Health and safety risks with
overcrowding, possible criminal activity such as drug dealing, prostitution, sex
trafficking, and unreported income by the tenant who is illegally subleasing.

Item #10 — No New Laws!

From is Leung re
