Dear City Clerk McTaggart,

| am disappointed that city staff is fast-tracking a series of new laws to regulate privately owned rental housing in
the city.

| provide a much-needed resource in your city — rental housing. Yet, my voice is secondary to the voices of the
activists, amplified by city staff, who are demanding new laws.

City staffs report highlights that the No. 1 cause of renter displacement is nonpayment of rent. Your focus should
be directed toward programs and public funding that would help renters who face economic emergencies — not
the new regulations staff is promoting.

Please recognize that housing providers experience economic challenges as well. It is disheartening that staff's
report does not acknowledge the hardships housing providers also endure. Whether they are financial, regulatory
or legal hardships, we too need assistance from the city and do not need new punitive laws imposed on us.

We went through hardships during the COVID-19 pandemic, and not once did the city ask if we needed help.

| ask that you direct city staff to be fair and take a neutral approach. Do not proceed with new regulations until you
assess what problem you are trying to solve and evaluate what existing laws can be used to address those
concerns without adopting new laws that will continue to make it harder and costlier for us to provide rental housing
to the city’s renters.

Sincerely,

Lisa Leung

Item #10 — Opposed to Introduction of New Laws

via votervoice.ne

Date Fri 4/18/2025 5:10 PM
To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Clerk McTaggart,

Please keep in mind that beyond introducing complications for non-corporate owners, these proposed regulations
will have one or both of the following outcomes across our community:

1) Reduction in rental unit quality as less is invested into upkeep and improvements.

2) Inflated rents for incoming tenants to offset new costs, rent limitations, and risk of unexpected payments.

The best policy is to support the sensible development of new housing to increase supply. This solution prevents
unsustainable regulation upon local owners while providing renters with more options. With more units available,
landlords must compete to provide the best quality while keeping rents within reason as demand is lowered. This
environment is tenant-friendly without asking owners to risk the financial viability of their properties when
maintaining, improving, and leasing living spaces.

Thank you for you consideration.

Sincerely,

Kyle McCreight

819 of 891

Oh Outlook

Adopt "One Home San Mateo" eviction policy proposals

From Acheigh vers

Date Fri 4/18/2025 3:31 AM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

April 18, 2025

San Mateo City Councilmembers:

San Mateo County Democracy for America, a chartered Democratic club, commends the San Mateo City
Council’s commitment to finding ways to expand renter protections in San Mateo, in order to provide
stable and secure housing in times that are increasingly insecure.

Because renters are so vulnerable to the traumatic life-changing effects of unjust evictions, we urge you
to adopt the five eviction protection policy proposals from the “One Home San Mateo” coalition* at your
San Mateo City Council meeting on Monday, April 21.

The “One Home San Mateo” proposals:

1. Just cause for evictions from Day One

2. Prohibition of no-fault evictions during the school year
3. Right to return after a substantial remodel

4. Increased relocation assistance

5. Creation of a rental data registry

* “One Home San Mateo” coalition members:

Housing Leadership Council

Peninsula Solidarity Cohort

San Mateo Climate Action Team

The Latino Ministry of the Congregational Church of San Mateo
Peninsula for Everyone

Housing Action Coalition

The League of Women Voters of North & Central San Mateo County
United Way Bay Area

Again, thank you for your leadership on behalf of renters in San Mateo.

Please see below.

From: Bill McQuaid
Sent: Thursday, April 17, 2025 4:24 PM

To: Rachel Horst <rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>

Subject: Expansion of Rent Control

My wife and | own a five unit apartment building in San Mateo.

We are opposed to any expansion of rent control in San Mateo.

We don't need more bureaucracy in the form of a rental registry and other onerous regulations.
.The state’s Tenant Protection Act of 2019 already provides comprehensive rent control and
eviction protections. The more regulations, the more likely "mom and pop" landlords like us, will
"throw in the towel" and sell out to a corporate type entity that can

afford legal counsel to navigate and decipher the more and more

complex nature of owning a small property.

If the desire of the City of San Mateo is to encourage the corporate

ownership of local real estate, more rent control and tenant eviction

protections is certainly a step in the right direction.

Bill and Maureen McQuaid

Rent Control Special Study Session 4/21/25

Date Thu 4/17/2025 4:06 PM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Council Members,
| am firmly against expanding AB1482.
Good tenants have adequate protection.

Expanding the protections will only encourage bad tenants and their attorneys to exploit the legal
process further. The courts already favor the tenants and have burdened good landlords with false
habitability claims, groundless retaliatory claims, etc. These types of cases are brought by predatory
law firms that game the system with bad tenants encouraged to play the game. Insurance companies
and landlords are being exploited with false claims due to added rent control and tenant protections.

What we need is more affordable rental housing, market-rate apartment buildings, etc.
Expanding AB1482 will discourage further future rental housing development.

| have been in the real estate business since 1978, | am a landlord in San Mateo, Alameda County, and
San Francisco. | currently deal with San Francisco's Rent Board that added a new rental registry which
adds to the yearly reporting and additional fees that all landlords have to incur.

| am 100 percent against adding any registry, as Landlords are already burdened with filing
requirements, rising insurance rates, rising maintenance costs due to rising labor and material costs.
These added requirements make any future and current landlord think twice about having a future as
a rental provider.

Rising operating expenses make rent control properties very hard to properly maintain and have
forced numerous mom-and-pop operators to sell out.

Property turnovers have led to larger rent increases due to higher maintenance costs and renovations
by investment groups with deep pockets.

| have numerous long-term tenants in San Mateo that have not seen any rent increases from me for
over 5 years.

| just recently had to send out a modest rent increase to one of my tenants due to the possibility of
added rent control concerns and the rent is still 15 to 20 percent below market.

No one understands how rent control hurts both landlords and tenants alike.

pages.

In closing, | believe AB1482 in its current form provides more than adequate tenant protections.

Gh Outlook

RE: New Business Item #10 - Tenant Protection

Date Mon 4/21/2025 9:54 AM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>

4/21/25

TO: City of San Mateo, Council and Staff
FROM: Suzanne Moore

RE: New Business Item #10 - Tenant Protection

Honorable Council members and staff.
Thank you for prioritizing tenant protection as part of your Housing Element.

My name is Suzanne Moore, sitting Board Member for Healthcare for the Homeless and Farmworkers
Program, and | speak for myself this evening.

Eviction remains one of the greatest barriers to finding another home; therefore, preventing evictions
is an obvious way to best assist tenants and interrupt the homelessness pipeline.

Here are how some communities chose to decrease evictions: they adopted ordinances

- that extend just cause for eviction from day one,

- ban junk fees in leases and halt THAT path to eviction for failure to pay,

- require landlords to provide access to tenant legal aid as they serve an eviction. Tenants who have an
attorney present in eviction court are more likely to stay housed.

I'm grateful to landlords who provide safe, affordable housing for our neighbors. Our tenants are an
important part of our community. We, as a community, can adopt ordinances that guide and protect.

Thank you.

Suzanne Moore

-----Original Message---—

From: Doris Nishimoto

Sent: Monday, April 21, 2025 12:52 PM

To: Rob Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Lisa Diaz Nash <Idiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Adam
Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>; Nicole Fernandez <nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>; Danielle Cwirko-
Godycki <dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org>; Prasanna Rasiah <prasiah@cityofsanmateo.org>; Rachel Horst
<rhorst@cityofsanmateo.org>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; Noel Nish

Subject: On Further Tenant Protections

Policy induced methods to hold landlords hostage to the situational distress of a breach of contract between
landlord and tenant is not the solution.

The "off ramp" to this problem is recognizing and addressing the deficiencies between eamed income and the
need to deregulate an environment that has ADDED costs to provider housing. Please, no more added costs of
rent registries and creation of a new department to monitor the housing market in the private market. Instead, this
money could be used as rental subsidies and would have a direct and meaningful impact for tenants.

The current rental security deposit that is allowed is insufficient to cover "wear and tear" tumaround costs for an
apartment and also for the proposed "move out" (up to 3 months rent) you are considering for tenants due to AB12
bill:

In California, Assembly Bill 12 (AB 12), which went into effect on July 1, 2024, limits security deposits for most
landlords to one month's rent, regardless of whether the unit is fumished or unfurnished. This law

amends California Civil Code Section 1950.5. There are exceptions, including for smaller landlords (those who own
two or fewer properties with a total of four or fewer rental units) who may still be able to collect up to two months'
rent.

Thank you for reading.

Doris Nishimoto, D.D.S.

Housing Element Implementation — Anti-Displacement and Tenant Protection Policies

From Seema Pot! {i

Date Mon 4/21/2025 3:45 PM
To Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>

Hello City Councilmembers!

| currently serve as the Chair of the San Mateo Planning Commission but am writing on my own behalf as a resident of San Mateo.

In its certified Housing Element San Mateo commits to a Fair Housing Action Plan in order to Affirmatively Further Fair Housing as required by state law. The
actions in this plan include:

Policy H5.2.4: In conjunction with San Mateo County, develop a housing
mobility program that provides support to tenants seeking to move to high opportunity areas in the region. (No timeline given)

Action 4.1: Establish tenant protections in local ordinance to extend
measures of AB1482 related to relocation, documentation, and right to return policy in eviction cases.

Extend AB1482 provisions to require tenant relocation payments for
No Fault evictions for those with tenure less than one year. (Target timeline 2024)

Make recommendations to the City Council for establishing tenant

protection policies that include the requirement of documentation from landlords who use the substantial remodel exemption to evict tenants and a Rig
2026)

Amend the Code to strengthen enforcement penalty structure to aid
in protecting tenants from unsafe or substandard units. (Target timeline of 2024)

Action 4.2: Partner with Project Sentinel to perform fair housing
training for landlords and tenants. (Timeline Annual by July 1)

The
city also commits to Policy H 3.6, Monitor Rents, Evictions and Tenant Displacement Citywide, including:

a.

Research options and best practices for

a rental registry list, including determining necessary financial and human resources needed to establish such a program. Make recommendations to
City Council. (Target timeline 2024)

Collaborate with regional partners, such
as 21 Elements or ABAG, to develop ways to monitor rental rates, evictions and displacement citywide and countywide to track trends and use the
information to guide policy priorities. (Target timeline 2024-2031)

| encourage the City Council to follow through with these commitments to ensure that our Housing Element remains in compliance with the state, so that we
do not risk decertification and exposure to penalties like the Builder's Remedy.

Thank you for your consideration.

Seema Patel

--- page 832 ---
Oh Outlook

Strong Protections of Renters Urgently Needed

From Ciara Reddy

Date Mon 4/21/2025 4:00 PM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Esteemed Council Members:

As the Council considers ways in which to expand protections under AB 1482, I encourage you to make
changes that will meaningfully enhance housing security for renters in the City of San Mateo.

According to multiple sources, renters in San Mateo comprise nearly half the population, and with the
exceedingly high rents in the City, many of them lead highly tenuous lives. One in four pays more than
50 percent of their income on rent, leaving very little for other essential needs that include food, health
care, transportation, utilities and child care. Furthermore, there is strong reason to expect that in the
coming four years, they will be subjected to takeaways that make life increasingly difficult for them.
Already there is talk of assaults upon Medicaid, housing vouchers and SNAP benefits, all of which are
vital supports for people of lower income. In an environment such as this, where the needs of our more
vulnerable community members are so readily compromised, there is ever more reason to keep renters
from being subjected to the cost and trauma they face when evicted from their homes.

Stable and secure housing is fundamental to the ability of families to thrive. As a houser, who believes
housing is a regional issue in which we all need to be mindful and proactive, please support the
proposals set forth by the coalition of organizations called “Our Home San Mateo” to help increase
housing security for renters in a regrettably insecure time.

Diana Reddy

Former Vice Mayor, Redwood City

Long-time Affordable Housing Advocate

To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Clerk McTaggart,
Please do not enact more restructuring on owners and small landlords.

| am disappointed that city staff is fast-tracking a series of new laws to regulate privately owned rental housing in
the city.

| provide a much-needed resource in your city — rental housing. Yet, my voice is secondary to the voices of the
activists, amplified by city staff, who are demanding new laws.

City staff's report highlights that the No. 1 cause of renter displacement is nonpayment of rent. Your focus should
be directed toward programs and public funding that would help renters who face economic emergencies — not
the new regulations staff is promoting.

Please recognize that housing providers experience economic challenges as well. It is disheartening that staff's
report does not acknowledge the hardships housing providers also endure. Whether they are financial, regulatory
or legal hardships, we too need assistance from the city and do not need new punitive laws imposed on us.

We went through hardships during the COVID-19 pandemic, and not once did the city ask if we needed help.

| ask that you direct city staff to be fair and take a neutral approach. Do not proceed with new regulations until you
assess what problem you are trying to solve and evaluate what existing laws can be used to address those
concerns without adopting new laws that will continue to make it harder and costlier for us to provide rental housing
to the city’s renters.

Sincerely,

Paul Tappan

Oh Outlook

NO Change to Rental Laws

Date Sun 4/20/2025 9:48 PM

To Rob Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Adam Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>; Lisa Diaz Nash
<Idiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Danielle Cwirko-Godycki <dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org >;
nfernadez@cityofsanmateo.org <nfernadez@cityofsanmateo.org>; Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org >

Dear Mayor and Council members

This is in response to the attack on property owners of our great city. This attack is relentless
and targeted at the weak and people that can not defend themselves such as many mom and
pop rental owners.

The organizers against landlords, including the city staff, goal seems to be to take away
property rights from the hardworking grandma/grandpas who have worked their whole lives on
their investment. These predators go city to city attacking landlords because they don't have
the guts to go after the big corporations and foreign owed entities that own and run enormous
high rise apartments. They have big $ and they know they can't go after them so the go after
the little guy.

All these attacks only make this worse for tenants and landlords. How?

* Because of the hostile environment of being a landlord, we know many who have already sold
or plan to sell, and no longer wish to have the liability associated with tenants. Some have even
left their properties vacant.

* The excessive rent control measures have left landlords and tenants in a state of animosity
instead of working together.

* Landlords now have no choice but to increase rents each time to the highest limits. In the
past, landlords would not do that as they had a friendly relationship with their tenants

“If your staff did the proper research and reported accurately, they would discover that as you
increase regulations/rent control/tenant protections measures, more property owners choose to
take their properties off the rental market leaving us with fewer inventory, thus increasing rents.

Based on what I've seen and heard at various council meetings, your staff spends a lot of time
interviewing tenants with little to no effort of identifying issues that affect landlords. They seem
to forget that landlords provide an essential service to our community.

I've attended several meeting at several cities and it seems to be the same people giving the
same sob stories, providing no receipts to back up what they're saying.

You need to find solutions other than attacking landlords. You want to put limits on what
landlords can do, what they can charge, how long they can rent, etc but there are no limits on
the costs of water, PGE, insurance, maintenance etc. This can not be sustained which is why
inventory will continue to decrease.

One common theme | also hear is "I can't afford to live here now that I'm retired". That is an
issue that we ALL have to contend with whether you're a renter or landlord. No one is
guaranteed they can live in their home for life. We all will have to deal with decreased income
when we retire and it is NOT the responsibility of a landlord to subsidize housing.

838 of 891


--- page 839 ---
Another theme is "I'm a teacher, | work for the city etc” and | should be able to live closer to
where | work. Their chosen job dictates their salary. If they can not afford to live close, they
must commute like so many of us do. Landlord should not subsidize. I'd love to live in the
beautiful hills of Portola Valley. | can’t afford it. No subsidy here.

Everything the city and state has done so far has only increased rents and driving landlords and
tenants apart. No where in the country has putting in rent control or highly regulating landlords
ever worked as proven time and time again.

SOLUTIONS

*You must create a more friendly environment to become a landlord and encourage rather than
restrict.

* Build more housing is not a solution as rent control is not their problem because they provide
minimal low income housing. As you know, new buildings are exempt from rent control which is
why mom and pops are being attacked.

“Provide free legal assistance to landlords. Tenants get it all the time which is why costs are so
high for landlords and which is why they are so frivolous in attacking.

*Restrict tenants ability to sue by requiring mediation which is paid for by the city. We are
already spending so much §$ on this issue. Spend it in a way that helps with actual problems
not theoretical ones.

* NGOs etc have become paid organizers to go after landlords because that's how they make
their $$ - "helping with housing". They are nothing but the problem because in the end, they
have caused these issues. NGOs have got to go.

It's unfair that there are different regulations for the mom and pop vs new building. Why?
Because they are big and powerful and provide the cities with lots of revenue when they build.

NO MORE CHANGES TO RENTAL LAWS. NO MORE ATTACKS ON LANDLORDS.

Thank you
Herman van Blommestein

Gh Outlook

Opposing Agenda Item #10

Date Mon 4/21/2025 3:17 PM

To Rob Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Adam Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>; Council
Member Lisa Diaz Nash <Idnash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Danielle Cwirko-Godycki

<dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org>; Nicole Fernandez <nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>
Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear Mayor Newsom and Members of the City Council,

As small landlord providing affordable rental housing in San Mateo, | am opposed to more housing
regulations. This will add more confusion and costs for the small housing providers in San Mateo. We do not
need a new set of laws in San Mateo beyond what is already regulated at the State level. San Mateo has a
shortage of affordable housing, please do not add more regulations to make this more costly for the small
housing providers?

Most property owners are reasonable about rental prices, try to work things out with tenants, and try to avoid
evictions. There are so many laws today which favor tenants at the State level, it would be beneficial to get a
better understanding of the current laws before adding more.

The primary cause of renter displacement is non-payment of rent according to the Staff Report. This is not
the fault of the housing provider. The focus should be toward programs and funding to help renters who face

these financial emergencies. Samaritan House helps with rental assistance in emergencies for renters.

More than 60% voted rejected expanded rent control with Proposition 33. These new local regulations will
make it more difficult for smaller landlords to offer affordable housing in San Mateo.

| oppose these new laws:

Changing tenancy to Day 1 and increasing relocation fees up to 3 months rent. This is covered in AB 1482.
Requirements to give the vacating renter a right to return to the unit after substantial remodel. A price control
measure may be included. This is not an incentive to improve a unit.

A rent registry does nothing for tenants, and impacts the privacy of landlords and tenants.

Please focus on programs and funding that provide rental assistance to the renter in emergency situations like
Samaritan House.

Thank you.

Oh Outlook

Strong Protections of Renters Urgently Needed

From Ciara Reddy

Date Mon 4/21/2025 4:00 PM
To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Esteemed Council Members:

As the Council considers ways in which to expand protections under AB 1482, I encourage you to make
changes that will meaningfully enhance housing security for renters in the City of San Mateo.

According to multiple sources, renters in San Mateo comprise nearly half the population, and with the
exceedingly high rents in the City, many of them lead highly tenuous lives. One in four pays more than
50 percent of their income on rent, leaving very little for other essential needs that include food, health
care, transportation, utilities and child care. Furthermore, there is strong reason to expect that in the
coming four years, they will be subjected to takeaways that make life increasingly difficult for them.
Already there is talk of assaults upon Medicaid, housing vouchers and SNAP benefits, all of which are
vital supports for people of lower income. In an environment such as this, where the needs of our more
vulnerable community members are so readily compromised, there is ever more reason to keep renters
from being subjected to the cost and trauma they face when evicted from their homes.

Stable and secure housing is fundamental to the ability of families to thrive. As a houser, who believes
housing is a regional issue in which we all need to be mindful and proactive, please support the
proposals set forth by the coalition of organizations called “Our Home San Mateo” to help increase
housing security for renters in a regrettably insecure time.

Diana Reddy

Former Vice Mayor, Redwood City

Long-time Affordable Housing Advocate

848 of 891

increase regulations/rent control/tenant protections measures, more property owners choose to
take their properties off the rental market leaving us with fewer inventory, thus increasing rents.

Based on what I've seen and heard at various council meetings, your staff spends a lot of time
interviewing tenants with little to no effort of identifying issues that affect landlords. They seem
to forget that landlords provide an essential service to our community.

I've attended several meeting at several cities and it seems to be the same people giving the
same sob stories, providing no receipts to back up what they're saying.

You need to find solutions other than attacking landlords. You want to put limits on what
landlords can do, what they can charge, how long they can rent, etc but there are no limits on
the costs of water, PGE, insurance, maintenance etc. This can not be sustained which is why
inventory will continue to decrease.

One common theme | also hear is "I can't afford to live here now that I'm retired". That is an
issue that we ALL have to contend with whether you're a renter or landlord. No one is
guaranteed they can live in their home for life. We all will have to deal with decreased income
when we retire and it is NOT the responsibility of a landlord to subsidize housing.

854 of 891


--- page 855 ---
Another theme is "I'm a teacher, | work for the city etc” and | should be able to live closer to
where | work. Their chosen job dictates their salary. If they can not afford to live close, they
must commute like so many of us do. Landlord should not subsidize. I'd love to live in the
beautiful hills of Portola Valley. | can’t afford it. No subsidy here.

Everything the city and state has done so far has only increased rents and driving landlords and
tenants apart. No where in the country has putting in rent control or highly regulating landlords
ever worked as proven time and time again.

SOLUTIONS

*You must create a more friendly environment to become a landlord and encourage rather than
restrict.

* Build more housing is not a solution as rent control is not their problem because they provide
minimal low income housing. As you know, new buildings are exempt from rent control which is
why mom and pops are being attacked.

“Provide free legal assistance to landlords. Tenants get it all the time which is why costs are so
high for landlords and which is why they are so frivolous in attacking.

*Restrict tenants ability to sue by requiring mediation which is paid for by the city. We are
already spending so much §$ on this issue. Spend it in a way that helps with actual problems
not theoretical ones.

* NGOs etc have become paid organizers to go after landlords because that's how they make
their $$ - "helping with housing". They are nothing but the problem because in the end, they
have caused these issues. NGOs have got to go.

It's unfair that there are different regulations for the mom and pop vs new building. Why?
Because they are big and powerful and provide the cities with lots of revenue when they build.

NO MORE CHANGES TO RENTAL LAWS. NO MORE ATTACKS ON LANDLORDS.

Thank you
Herman van Blommestein

Item #10 — No New Laws!

From Coroine Washo ii

Date Fri 4/18/2025 1:00 PM
To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Clerk McTaggart,

| am a single woman who owns a Duplex in San Mateo. It is the first property | have ever owned and these new
laws would be devastating for my small investment.

Please DO NOT VOTE YES ON THIS!

| am disappointed that city staff is fast-tracking a series of new laws to regulate privately owned rental housing in
the city.

| provide a much-needed resource in your city — rental housing. Yet, my voice is secondary to the voices of the
activists, amplified by city staff, who are demanding new laws.

City staffs report highlights that the No. 1 cause of renter displacement is nonpayment of rent. Your focus should
be directed toward programs and public funding that would help renters who face economic emergencies — not
the new regulations staff is promoting.

Please recognize that housing providers experience economic challenges as well. It is disheartening that staff's
report does not acknowledge the hardships housing providers also endure. Whether they are financial, regulatory
or legal hardships, we too need assistance from the city and do not need new punitive laws imposed on us.

We went through hardships during the COVID-19 pandemic, and not once did the city ask if we needed help.

| ask that you direct city staff to be fair and take a neutral approach. Do not proceed with new regulations until you
assess what problem you are trying to solve and evaluate what existing laws can be used to address those
concerns without adopting new laws that will continue to make it harder and costlier for us to provide rental housing
to the city’s renters.

Sincerely,

Caroline Walshe

Gh Outlook

Opposing Agenda Item #10

Date Mon 4/21/2025 3:17 PM

To Rob Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Adam Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>; Council
Member Lisa Diaz Nash <Idnash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Danielle Cwirko-Godycki

<dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org>; Nicole Fernandez <nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>
Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear Mayor Newsom and Members of the City Council,

As small landlord providing affordable rental housing in San Mateo, | am opposed to more housing
regulations. This will add more confusion and costs for the small housing providers in San Mateo. We do not
need a new set of laws in San Mateo beyond what is already regulated at the State level. San Mateo has a
shortage of affordable housing, please do not add more regulations to make this more costly for the small
housing providers?

Most property owners are reasonable about rental prices, try to work things out with tenants, and try to avoid
evictions. There are so many laws today which favor tenants at the State level, it would be beneficial to get a
better understanding of the current laws before adding more.

The primary cause of renter displacement is non-payment of rent according to the Staff Report. This is not
the fault of the housing provider. The focus should be toward programs and funding to help renters who face

these financial emergencies. Samaritan House helps with rental assistance in emergencies for renters.

More than 60% voted rejected expanded rent control with Proposition 33. These new local regulations will
make it more difficult for smaller landlords to offer affordable housing in San Mateo.

| oppose these new laws:

Changing tenancy to Day 1 and increasing relocation fees up to 3 months rent. This is covered in AB 1482.
Requirements to give the vacating renter a right to return to the unit after substantial remodel. A price control
measure may be included. This is not an incentive to improve a unit.

A rent registry does nothing for tenants, and impacts the privacy of landlords and tenants.

Please focus on programs and funding that provide rental assistance to the renter in emergency situations like
Samaritan House.

Thank you.

857 of 891

Item #10 — No New Laws!

via votervoice.net
Date Fri 4/18/2025 3:10 PM
To Martin McTaggart <mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Clerk McTaggart,

Passing more laws only protects a minor class of residents who are in place. While that is good it just passes on
the rent to income new residents and make housing unaffordable going forward. The best approach to housing is
relaxing zoning and planning barriers and permit high density and height limits where possible to facilitate more
building of housing units. This is the only true effective approach to affordable housing for the long term. Don't
copy the failed policies of San Francisco and other rent controlled cities!

| am disappointed that city staff is fast-tracking a series of new laws to regulate privately owned rental housing in
the city.

| provide a much-needed resource in your city — rental housing. Yet, my voice is secondary to the voices of the
activists, amplified by city staff, who are demanding new laws.

City staffs report highlights that the No. 1 cause of renter displacement is nonpayment of rent. Your focus should
be directed toward programs and public funding that would help renters who face economic emergencies — not
the new regulations staff is promoting.

Please recognize that housing providers experience economic challenges as well. It is disheartening that staff's
report does not acknowledge the hardships housing providers also endure. Whether they are financial, regulatory
or legal hardships, we too need assistance from the city and do not need new punitive laws imposed on us.

We went through hardships during the COVID-19 pandemic, and not once did the city ask if we needed help.

| ask that you direct city staff to be fair and take a neutral approach. Do not proceed with new regulations until you
assess what problem you are trying to solve and evaluate what existing laws can be used to address those
concerns without adopting new laws that will continue to make it harder and costlier for us to provide rental housing
to the city’s renters.

Sincerely,

Kenneth Wei

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  11
Section Name: NEW BUSINESS
Account Number: 10-1311
File ID: 25-9853
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
City Manager's Office
MEETING DATE:
April 21, 2025 
SUBJECT:
City Council Priority List – Adoption
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt the City Council Priority List for fiscal year 2025-26.
BACKGROUND:
The City Council goal setting process includes a three-step deliberation process resulting in the Adopted City Council 
Priority List for each fiscal year. The City Council Blue Sky workshop held on February 22, 2025 was the first step in this 
annual goal setting process. At that meeting, City Council received a status update on its 2024/2025 Fiscal Year Priority 
List, which contained 37 priorities within three timeframe categories. The categories represent expected completion 
within one fiscal year, 2-5 fiscal years, or 5 or more years. The List also includes a summary level work plan and estimated 
resource intensity for each priority. At the meeting staff made recommendations to remove, retain, or recategorize 
priorities based on their progress to date and work remaining. Staff was directed to:
•
Remove completed items 
•
Make minor revisions to items that needed adjusting, such as pushing out the date of the EV fleet item from 2030 
to 2035 for a more realistic completion schedule
•
Combine a few related items such as those related to stormwater management and the lagoon; and,
•
Recategorize some items to reflect a more accurate completion schedule, such as completion of the new 
Wastewater Treatment Plant and completion of the Central Park Improvements.
In addition, the Council also heard a report on staff’s existing work plan items that are unrelated to formalized Council 
priorities but are a part of each department’s daily work. Further, each Councilmember presented three (3) proposed new 
priorities to be integrated into the list of Adopted City Council priorities for the new fiscal year and deliberated on whether 
they should be retained. 
The April 21, 2025 meeting marks the second step in the process when the Council will be reviewing and providing final 
direction on the new draft 2025/2026 Fiscal Year Priority List. To assist in this conversation, Attachment 1 provides the full 
list of the original 37 Council priorities with a column on the far right noting where staff made noteworthy revisions based 
on status updates provided throughout the year or by Council at the Blue Sky meeting. Some minor changes have also 
been made since the Blue Sky meeting—for instance, an economic consultant was onboarded to assist with peer reviewing 
of mixed-use projects enabling the removal of former Priority #4. Also, the new proposed priorities that gained 
concurrence by at least three (3) Councilmembers are also integrated into this list and placed within the timeframe 
category where staff thought they were best suited. The new priorities are noted by the word “Added” in the far-right 
column and the new grand total for all priorities is 41. It is important to note that some of the proposed priorities were 
already accounted for in other work plan items and while they did not make it as stand-alone items, they are still 
864 of 891

--- page 865 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
accounted for in the Council priorities. Some examples include:
•
Exploring how small/medium apartment complexes can increase in units is part of Housing Element 
implementation work 
•
Fire House 27 improvements is part of the City’s facilities assessment work
•
Exploring lagoon pump station options is part of the Stormwater Master Plan and is captured in former Priorities 
#5 and 31 (now #42). 
•
With the Business Improvement District Priority, it was more detailed than a similar existing priority so it 
superseded the older priority.
The resource intensities included in the table remained largely unchanged for both existing and proposed priorities, as the 
projects are generally similar to how they were presented in February. For the existing priorities, the main revisions are 
related to timing/schedule and remaining work for the longer-term priorities which remain fairly resource intensive. As 
would be expected, the shorter-term priorities are less resource intensive either because they are nearly complete or just 
starting and entail primarily the exploratory or piloting phases of our work. Where the resource intensity is higher, it is 
generally indicative of a longer term priority that is nearing completion. Attachment 2 provides a clean copy of the new 
proposed Priorities.
At the meeting, staff will be available to answer any remaining questions and will be seeking final direction on the 
Priorities so we can proceed to the third and final step, which is to finalize and adopt the list as part of the City’s annual 
budgeting process. Tonight, we ask Council:
1.
Do you have any final revisions to the verbiage of any of the priorities?
2.
Do you have any final revisions to the category of any of the priorities?
3.
Are there any priorities you wish to remove? 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budget impact from this agenda item. A majority of the resource requirements for the adopted Priority List are 
incorporated into the fiscal year 2024-25 budget proposal and the remainder will be brought back as needed, as the 
projects evolve following further analysis and Council direction.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This information update is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will 
not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 - Draft Priorities Full Markup
Att 2 - Draft Priorities Clean

Christina Horrisberger

chorrisberger@cityofsanmateo.org

--- page 887 ---
Oh Outlook

4/21/2025 agenda item #11 public comment: priority list adoption

Date Mon 4/21/2025 3:45 PM
To Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear City Council,

| attended the Blue Sky Goal Setting Workshop on February 22. It
was an interesting, well-run meeting. Among the many good ideas
presented, the leaf blower ordinance in particular will not only

help our community, but also help us support our county and state
goals with respect to reducing carbon and smog-forming emissions.
Thanks to city staff and council members for their hard work, and
for showing up on a beautiful sunny Saturday.

Speaking now as a member of Quiet Clean San Mateo and the national
Quiet Clean Alliance —

Thank you Council Member Lisa Diaz Nash for sponsoring an ordinance
banning the use of highly polluting gas-powered leaf blowers. The
entire community, especially the equipment operators, will experience
better health and quality of life once the transition is made to
battery-powered tools. Good health is priceless! Because this is

a health initiative, we'd like the ordinance to go into effect no

later than January 1, 2026. This means announcing the ordinance
in June, to allow businesses a 6-month grace period to make the
transition. This is more than fair considering that some California
cities offer no grace period at all. We already have draft ordinance
language prepared to assist the city manager's office, and we are
ready to help communicate with landscaping businesses and their
customers regarding this improvement to their services.

We ask the council members and the city manager's office to let us
know precisely what they need to get this done for a January 2026
start date, including what is meant by the phrase "based on industry
best practices". Community members — that is, residents of the City
of San Mateo — are the ones who must be able to provide input to the
process.

We are here to help. Thank you.

Sincerely,

887 of 891


--- page 888 ---
Carol Wilhelmy, District 5 resident
quietcleansanmateo.org
quietcleanalliance.org

888 of 891


--- page 889 ---
lo M Outlook

City Priorities - Electric Leaf Blower Transition

From Dustin Chase

Date Mon 4/21/2025 3:41 PM

To City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>
Cc Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>

I’d like to comment on the gas leaf blower regulation initiative. I have played a major
role in it, and I’d like to talk about why.

I am a part of a vulnerable, marginalized minority that tends to be severely harmed by
gas leaf blower pollution.

I am also part of a privileged, powerful minority. I have some resources and power that
many others do not have.

I am in a unique situation - I am both in a vulnerable, marginalized group and ina
powerful, privileged one. One of our responsibilities as privileged people is to look out
for those who are more vulnerable. I feel it is my role to use my experiences to help me
understand other vulnerable people, and to use my privilege to help shield them.

With this idea, our community asks “what can we do for our most vulnerable
neighbors?” I thank Council Member Diaz Nash for bringing this important idea
forward. We urgently need to transition to electric leaf blowers.

We know leaf blowers are not necessary for plant care. Blowing leaves is a luxury; it is
for the convenience of the property owner, who, rightly or wrongly, feels using hand
tools would take too long.

Very well. This ordinance will allow leaf blower use in San Mateo, but it will require
employers to switch from gas leaf blowers to electric leaf blowers.

This change is primarily for landscaping workers. Right now, they have to accept serious
short and long term health risks, not to mention ugly confrontation and nasty conflict
with other community members, just to make a living. That is not fair.

889 of 891


--- page 890 ---
Using gas leaf blowers is not fair to the rest of the community, either. The community
cannot adapt to them because they are impossible to use safely. But it’s possible to adapt
to the electric models. For starters, they have zero local emissions, so all the large
volumes of toxic substances and carcinogens that gas leaf blowers spew into our
neighborhoods, lingering near the ground long after the crews are gone, will be
eliminated.

The electric leaf blowers are also significantly quieter than gas models, and the noise
does not carry nearly as far or penetrate physical barriers as easily as gas leaf blower
noise does. In my experience, many electric leaf blowers are very quiet, and I can be
nearby without any trouble. Some electric leaf blower models are troublesome, but I
find my ear defense works well in those cases. We may not like electric leaf blowers, but
we can live with them.

Landscaping businesses tend to resist electric leaf blowers, but they like them once they
get used to them. These are safer, quieter tools with much lower operating costs. Workers
will have to adapt, but with the harms of gas leaf blowers being so great, that adaptation
is little to ask.

I want to acknowledge that many of us want to ban all leaf blowers and for good reasons,
including health and environmental protection. I’m sorry we cannot provide the
complete relief you have been searching for. We are doing the best we can.

We are offering harm reduction. In return, we are asking for the community's help.
Please help us spread the word about this upcoming rule change, and the reasons for it.
Please change your lawn service to electric leaf blowing as soon as possible, and ask
your neighbors to do the same.

Please be patient as we transition. I understand many of you are very frustrated, but
please don’t plan to rely completely on code enforcement. If you see a violation, try to
work things out. A simple conversation with your neighbor may be all you need to find
relief, and that diffuses any unnecessary conflict. If a landscaping worker is struggling
with this change, please think about what you could do to help them. Some simple
understanding might go a long way.

890 of 891


--- page 891 ---
We can’t live with gas blowers, but we can live together. Let us compromise and carry
on as a more healed community where everyone has a better chance to meet their
potential.

Thank you.

Dustin Chase
San Francisco Resident, former San Mateo resident

891 of 891
